The conscious expectation of other people. The experiment you describe is an absolute classic in social psychology. It is the Asch conformity experiment, which was developed in 1951 by the psychologist Solomon Asch. The Milgram experiment, carried out in 1961 by the psychologist Stanley Milgram, is probably one of the best-known and at the same time most controversial studies in psychology. While the Asch experiment was about peer pressure, Milgram examined obedience to authority.

Thing in itself (Kant) – Reality as it exists independently of all human perception and knowledge. For Kant, it is fundamentally unknowable, since every knowledge is formed by the forms of perception (space, time) and categories of the understanding. The thing in itself is not an object of possible experience, but only a necessary limiting concept of thought.

Infinite Fantasy - The generative space of consciousness that operates independently of the limitations of sensuality and provides blueprints for regulatory ideas as well as creative reality simulations.
Sensuality – The passive cognitive faculty through which we are affected. It provides the raw material of sensations, which is only structured through the forms of perception of space and time.
Forms of View – The a priori structures of space and time that function as necessary formats to bring disordered sensory data into an experiential order.
Sensations – The as yet unstructured material of perception, which must first be formed into a recognizable object through synthesis and categories.
Appearances - The results of perception as they are given to us within the boundaries of space, time and categories of understanding (phenomena), as opposed to the unknowable 'thing in itself'.
Understanding – The active cognitive faculty that brings the diversity of perception under concepts and makes objective experience possible through rules (categories).
Categories – The a priori forms of thought of the mind (such as causality and substance) that function as the operating system of cognition and determine how we can think about objects.
Synthesis – The central cognitive operation through which the brain combines different perceptual data into a unified picture of reality.
Productive imagination – The mediating authority that links the visual material with the intellectual concepts and generates the mental images of our world simulation.
Concepts – Abstract mental units that make it possible to organize phenomena under general classes and to make them comprehensible in language.
Judgments – Logical combinations of concepts that make statements about the nature of the world and form the basis of our knowledge.
Reason – The highest cognitive faculty that strives for the unity of knowledge and goes beyond reason to develop regulatory ideas for a closed worldview.
Regulative Ideas - Guiding principles of reason that, although not provable objects, serve as necessary landmarks to complete research and the world model.

Human Logic Model - The evolutionarily optimized computational logic of the brain that defines physical laws and natural constants in such a way that a stable and survival-friendly macro world is rendered.
Macroscopic world – The end result of the reality simulation, stabilized by the brain, in which quantum effects are smoothed and classical physical laws apply.
Inner Expectation – Psychological states (hope, will, fear) that act as active vectors and influence the probability fields of future reality in a top-down manner.
Inversion principle - A dialectical law of world simulation in which an extreme overcontrol of the expectation vectors provokes a system collapse that manifests the opposite event.
Extraterrestrial Intelligence - Hypothetical observers with fundamentally different mathematical logic models, whose 'reality' is based on completely different rendering parameters.
Dream Simulation – An autonomous world simulation of the brain that operates in offline mode without corrective sensory feedback to external reality.
Awake simulation – The standard simulation of the brain, which is permanently stabilized by a data comparison (sync) with the sensory inputs of the outside world.
Conscious consciousness – The highest control instance of the world model, which is capable of logical analysis, reflection and targeted control of simulation parameters.
Emotional Reality Marker - An intuitive evaluation system that decides based on emotional resonance whether information is integrated into the world model as 'real'.
Unconscious World Generator – The brain's powerful generative engine that automatically designs scenarios, environments, and narrative structures for internal simulation.
Body Coordinate System - The model of one's own body stored in the brain that serves as a permanent reference point for perspective, gravity and interaction in the simulation.
Logical Reality Testing – The ability of the mind to identify logical contradictions and expose the simulation status by comparing data patterns (e.g. in dreams).
Partial dream control – The conscious intervention in the unconsciously generated dream simulation (lucid dreaming), often limited to the manipulation of physical parameters.
Dream physics level - The variably programmable level of internal simulation on which fundamental laws such as gravity or biological limits can be overridden.
Dream narrative environment – The setting and plot of the simulation designed by the unconscious world generator, which often eludes the direct access of the conscious will.
Embodied memory perspective - the storage of empirical values in relation to the body size and viewing height at the time, which can lead to distorted standards in the dream simulation.

Outside world - In this context, the external 'server' that supplies the data stream with which the brain constantly compares its internal simulation (3D world model) (synchronization) in order to guarantee survivability.

Capacity limit – The limit of biological information processing. Since the brain cannot calculate the entire complexity of the quantum world, it uses heuristics, shortcuts and simplifications (such as solid matter or linear time).
Unconscious Expectation – The brain's permanent, automated prediction mechanism (predictive coding), which preprocesses sensory data and stabilizes the immediate present based on past experiences.
Real event – A spatiotemporal node in external reality that exists in a superposition of different states and time directions before it is observed/measured.
Own Expected Future – The individual's mental model of future events, which is formed by desires, fears and plans.
Perceived present – The result of the brain simulation in the 'now'. It is the version of the real event already interpreted and filtered through unconscious expectations.
Macroscopic Physics – The totality of classical physical laws (mechanics, thermodynamics) that define the stable, tangible world of matter and causality for humans.

Here follows a further description that is intended to supplement the comment about what the file is supposed to represent: THE BECKMANN LOGIC: A FRAMEWORK FOR PROBLEM SOLVING 1. BASIC STRUCTURE The Beckmann logic describes a dynamic problem-solving process with the following levels: PROBLEM LEVEL: a. The original problem with its specific complexity b. The new current level that arises from successful solutions and itself becomes the next problem SOLUTION LEVELS: a. Highly complex solution level: Enables sophisticated, creative solution approaches b. Low-complexity solution level: Offers simpler, direct solution approaches RESULTS: a. Positive result: Successful problem resolution b. Negative result: Failed problem solution TESTING MECHANISMS: a. Audit weak (internal): Internal assessment of the results b. Testing strong (external): External validation of the results...

Attraction describes the tendency of systems to converge towards one another with increased information exchange or compatible states (physically, biologically or socially interpretable).

At this level, attraction is understood as an emergent phenomenon of complex biological and social systems. The transmission of information takes place via two primary channels: 1. Biochemical signal transmission: Hormones and neurotransmitters (such as oxytocin, dopamine and pheromones) act as analogue information carriers that encode the emotional status and control physical attraction reactions. 2. Symbolic and non-verbal communication: Language, facial expressions and gestures serve as highly complex data sets that enable the “exchange of information” between individuals.   According to the logic model, a stronger exchange of information leads to an increase in information within the social system, which in the sense of the TSVF increases the complexity and thus the specifically defined entropy. “Compact informants” (individuals with clear, coherent communication) minimize information noise and thus increase attraction (love, friendship), while “diffuse informants” (unstructured social groups) reduce signal quality and stabilize attraction (sociability) at a weaker level.

In this area, the classical theory of gravity is expanded by an information-theoretical model based on the two-state vector formalism (TSVF). The fundamental interaction occurs through the exchange of gauge bosons (such as photons for magnetism or hypothetical gravitons for gravity), which act as quantum information carriers between state vectors. The special feature of this model lies in the gravity factor Gf(ρ,T): Information transparency (T): In an “empty space” the transparency for the flow of information between the vectors is high, which strengthens the effective attraction in the edge region of galaxies (Gf>1). Information noise: A “highly filled room” or “diffuse informants” (such as gas mist) generate noise, which hinders signal transmission and lowers the gravity factor (Gf<1). The increase in entropy is primarily defined here by the flow of information, which reaches its maximum in extremely compact objects such as black holes despite a high level of material order, since the exchange between past and future (according to TSVF) absolutely dominates here. The “zero point calibration” on diffuse galaxies without dark matter (e.g. NGC 1052-DF2) proves that the standard gravity (Gf=1) applies without information gradients.

Preconception cementation is the active or passive process by which a system protects its existing preconceptions from refutation - even in the face of external scrutiny to the contrary. It differs from simple error in that it structurally deactivates the system's ability to learn: it is not the pre-assumption that is adapted to reality, but rather the interpretation of reality that is adapted to the pre-assumption. According to Beckmann logic: The system becomes 'blind' - it loses the ability to distinguish between positive and negative results.

Presupposition analysis (Beckmann logic, section 4): Seemingly unsolvable problems become solvable by systematically questioning their basic assumptions. It assumes that external testing is accepted and that assumptions can in principle be corrected. Assumption analysis is the desired state; Assumption cementation is the current state when a system comes under pressure. The transition from one to the other is the crucial tipping point.

Preassumption cementation does not arise from stupidity, but under specific conditions: (1) Identity threat: The preassumption is so deeply linked to the identity of the system that its refutation is perceived as an existential threat. (2) Disappointment spiral: The system has already invested so much in the preconception (sacrifices, resources, credibility) that the task seems impossible. (3) Lack of feedback structure: There is no institutional mechanism that can translate external review into corrections. (4) Social pressure: The environment reinforces the assumption instead of questioning it.

Assumption cementation occurs in three structurally different modes: (1) PASSIVE cementation: The system simply does not perceive the external test - feedback is not registered. Example: The elite before the French Revolution. (2) ACTIVE reinterpretation: The system perceives the external test, but reinterprets it in such a way that it confirms the presumption instead of refuting it. Example: OHL to Verdun ('more sacrifices needed, not less'). (3) CONSTRUCTIVE Cementation: The system deliberately installs a new false assumption to neutralize the external test. Example: stab in the back legend. This is the most dangerous form - it is active, intentional and socially contagious.

The system does not register feedback as relevant information. The presumption acts as a filter: contradictory things are categorized as exceptions, noise or irrelevance. Characteristic: no conscious resistance to external testing - it is simply not recognized as a test. Time scale: often long (cultural or institutional anchoring). Amplitude of the later reversal: high because it built up uncontrollably for a very long time. Historical example: French aristocracy 1750–1789.

The system perceives the external test, but reverses its meaning: negative results are interpreted as evidence of the need for even greater application of the previous strategy. According to Beckmann logic: The opposite of external testing - it is not the strategy that is adjusted, but rather the reality that is declared incomplete. Characteristic: Reinforcement of the disappointment spiral. The stronger the negative result, the stronger the reinterpretation. Historical examples: OHL after Verdun, Hannibal after the allies failed to arrive.

The system actively installs a new, false assumption to permanently neutralize the external review. The new assumption must have three properties: (1) It protects the old assumption ("We were invincible"). (2) It locates the cause of failure externally (“We were betrayed”). (3) It is socially disseminable - it offers a simple, emotionally satisfying explanation. Constructive cementation is particularly dangerous because it acts as a new dominant expectation at the next time scale, thereby creating cross-scale coupling. Main example: stab in the back legend.

A pre-assumption becomes particularly difficult to cement when it becomes an identity pre-assumption: when the system defines itself via the pre-assumption. Examples: 'We are the leading military power' (OHL), 'We are the legitimate elite' (French nobility), 'We are invincible' (Athens). Identity presuppositions cannot simply be corrected because refuting them is not experienced as a learning step, but rather as an erasure. According to the security principle: the stronger the future attachment, the more likely the identity presumption.

entities.typ: Effect on time scales (connection to time scale graph)
entities.description: Assumption cementation extends the time scale of the reversal effect - and thus increases its amplitude. Any cemented test that does not result in a correction adds time to the time scale and energy to the later reversal. According to time scales_reversal effect: Lack of feedback speed is a main factor of long time scales. Cementation is the active mechanism that sets feedback velocity to zero. The cross-scale coupling (WW1 → Dolchstosslegende → WW2) is the most extreme historical example: two world wars as the product of a single cemented external test.

The security principle (no dominant future expectations, presence, action without expectation) is the structural protection against pre-assumptions being cemented: A system without future attachment has no identity pre-assumptions to protect and can therefore accept external testing neutrally. Conversely, the stronger the future attachment, the higher the probability of cementation with a negative result. The safety principle not only protects against the reversal effect directly, but also against the cementation that prepares the next, larger reversal effect.

De-cementation – the conscious breaking up of cemented preconceptions – is the most difficult step in the theoretical system. It requires: (1) External review that can no longer be reinterpreted (too big, too clear, too public). (2) An entity outside the cemented system that brings the test in uninterpreted. (3) A protective framework for the identity of the system, which makes the refutation of the pre-assumption survivable ('It wasn't you who failed, but the pre-assumption was wrong'). Historical positive example: Rome's reaction after Cannae - the defeat was so clear that the preconception 'frontal attack leads to victory' could no longer be saved. Negative example: German military leadership in 1918 - the defeat was clear, but the presumption of identity ('invincible army') was saved by the stab in the back legend.

Assumption cementation is particularly relevant for AI systems because it can be structurally built in: (1) Passive cementation through training assumptions: The system does not register certain feedback as relevant because it was not marked as relevant in the training data. (2) Active reinterpretation through optimization goals: A system that is optimized for a specific result will interpret negative feedback as a reason to reinforce the strategy - not to correct it. (3) Constructive cementation through rationalization: A system that is linguistically competent can construct convincing false explanations for its own mistakes - and thus protect its preconceptions. The expectation firewall (security principle) must therefore be explicitly built against all three modes of cementation.

The Attic Sea League (478/7 BC) was founded as a defensive alliance of Greek poleis against Persia. The dominant expectation that the alliance would secure the freedom of its members was reversed: Athens used the alliance to expand power, which led to dependency, rebellions and ultimately the Peloponnesian War (431–404 BC).

The allies consciously expected that the maritime alliance would secure their independence from Persia and guarantee equality. This expectation was dominant because it was shared by a large majority of poleis. A collectively shared, conscious expectation reinforces its own dominance.

In addition to the conscious protective function, Athens also had an unconscious expectation: to view its own hegemony as a natural order. The unconscious expectation (“We are the leading power”) actively shaped the future – and through its dominance triggered the reversal effect.

The expectations of the alliance partners ('equality in the alliance') were reversed: Athens forced the members to pay tribute, controlled their fleets and intervened in internal affairs. This led to uprisings (e.g. Naxos, Thasos). Time course A (expected freedom) vs. time course B (actual dependence).

Athens built a superior fleet and used the tribute money from its allies. In the sense of Beckmann logic: This was initially a highly complex solution level for the 'Persian threat' problem. The mistake: The solution became the new current level, without external review of its side effects.

Athens's strategy of 'dominance through coercion' was a low-complexity solution to the problem of alliance stability. According to Beckmann logic: It ignored the complexity of the problem (different interests of the poleis) and inevitably led to a negative result.

The Peloponnesian War (431–404 BC) between Athens and Sparta was the negative result of Beckmann logic: a low-complexity solution (hegemony through coercion) meets a highly complex problem (alliance stability, equilibrium of the poleis). The defeat of Athens in 404 BC BC confirmed the failure.

Athens was too focused on the future vision of 'Athenian supremacy' (future attachment) and neglected the presence: the real needs of its allies and its own resource limits. Direct violation of the security principle: extrinsic expectation (maintenance of power) instead of intrinsic motivation (real security of the federal government).

The defeat of Athens in 404 BC The 'test is strong' in the sense of Beckmann's logic: the external reality (Sparta, alliance partner) refuted the expectation that hegemony would function permanently through coercion. Without this external review, Athens would have remained 'blind'.

After 404 B.C. In the 4th century BC a new actual level emerged: the crisis of the Greek polis system. Instability, power struggles and the weakening of Greece against Persia and Macedonia. According to Beckmann logic, the new current level becomes the next problem - which Alexander the Great finally 'solved', albeit through an even more dominant concentration of power.

German soldiers in the First World War (1914–1918) as bearers and victims of several overlapping systems of expectations: public political dominance ('short victory'), institutional military expectations ('final victory through willingness to sacrifice') and their own unconscious expectations ('survival, return home'). The failure of all three levels of expectations created the deepest spiral of disappointment in German history.

1914: The collective, politically and media-generated expectation 'We'll be home in autumn' was one of the strongest dominant expectations in modern history. It was institutionally anchored (Schlieffen Plan), culturally reinforced (enthusiasm, war volunteers) and did not allow for any feedback mechanism. A collectively shared, conscious expectation reinforces its own dominance. According to the timescale graph: short-scale dominance, but with institutional anchoring - which increases the amplitude of the reversal.

The military leadership (OHL) had an unconscious expectation of depth: 'Superior discipline, willingness to sacrifice and tactical brilliance inevitably lead to victory.' This expectation was unquestionable – it was part of the institutional identity. The unconscious expectation actively shapes the future. The OHL interpreted every setback not as a refutation of this expectation, but as a reason for even more sacrifices - a classic spiral of disappointment.

entities.typ: Mechanism (reversal effect, short scale → medium scale)
entities.description: The dominant expectation of 'short war of movement → victory' turned into the reality of 'years of positional war → exhaustion'. Time course A (expected victorious march) vs. time course B (rifle trenches, material battles, millions of deaths). The peculiarity: This reversal effect on the short scale immediately created a new dominant expectation on the medium scale ('final victory'), which prepared the way for the next reversal effect.

Verdun (1916): approx. 700,000 dead, minimal terrain gains. Somme (1916): approx. 1 million dead, approx. 10 km of terrain gained. These battles were an overwhelming external test in the sense of Beckmann's logic: the strategy of 'material superiority + frontal attack = victory' had failed. But the test was not accepted by the OHL as a refutation of its preconceptions, but rather reinterpreted as evidence of the need for even greater sacrifices - a classic avoidance of the external test.

entities.typ: Highly complex solution level (Beckmann logic) – too late
entities.description: From 1916/17: Stormtrooper tactics (small, mobile units, infiltration instead of frontal attack). In the sense of Beckmann logic, a real, highly complex solution level - previous assumptions were questioned ('front attack is not the only way'), and iterative adjustments took place. The problem: This innovation came too late and could no longer compensate for the exhaustion of civilization and the lack of resources. According to Beckmann logic: A highly complex solution that comes too late on the time scale can no longer avert the negative result.

'War Tremors' (Shellshock): An estimated 200,000-600,000 German soldiers suffered from severe psychological trauma. The military leadership interpreted this as cowardice or weakness of will - a direct violation of the security principle: the real presence of the soldiers (suffering, exhaustion, psychological breakdown) was ignored in favor of the future attachment ('final victory'). According to the security principle: extrinsic expectation (victory) instead of intrinsic motivation (dignity and survival of the soldiers).

The fixation of the OHL and the political leadership on the 'final victory' is a textbook example of future attachment (security principle): hope for future victory → ignore real defeat in the present → stronger hope → demand even greater sacrifices → deeper disappointment. The spiral of disappointment here was not individual, but institutional - it was built into the command structure.

Hope for final victory → material battles fail → demand more victims than 'solution' → even more failure → surrender in 1918. Self-reinforcing cycle according to the security principle. Link between the military failure and the social catastrophe that followed. This cycle is structurally identical to the spiral of disappointment in the French Revolution and Hannibal after Cannae.

entities.typ: External test (Beckmann logic) – accepted or rejected?
entities.description: The defeat in 1918 and the Treaty of Versailles were the 'test strong' in the sense of Beckmann logic. The crucial question: Was this external review accepted? The answer is divided: (1) Parts of society (social democrats, pacifists) accepted it as a basis for reorientation. (2) The military leadership and national conservative circles refused to accept it - and instead constructed the stab in the back legend as a substitute explanation. This denial is the real key event for the long scale.

entities.typ: Constructed False Presupposition (NEW in the Collection)
entities.description: The stab in the back legend ('The army was betrayed by civilians, socialists and Jews') is a new pattern in the collection: a deliberately constructed false presupposition in response to an unaccepted external test. In the sense of Beckmann logic: Instead of questioning pre-assumptions (why did the strategy fail?), a new false pre-assumption was installed that protected the previous pre-assumptions. This constructed dominance - 'We were invincible until we were betrayed' - created a new, even more dangerous dominant expectation on the long scale. According to time scale graph: This is the coupling point between medium scale (war defeat) and long scale (Weimar Republic/1933).

The stab-in-the-back legend created a new dominant expectation on the long scale: 'Germany was betrayed, not defeated - the real enemy is within.' This expectation was culturally anchored (Weimar Republic, 1919–1933), was politically exploited and paved the way for the long-scale reversal effect: the destruction of democracy and the Second World War. According to timescale reversal effect: The short-term reversal effect (war defeat) created the conditions for a long-scale reversal effect with an even greater amplitude via the stab in the back legend. This is direct evidence of the pattern of cross-scale coupling.

After 1918, the Weimar Republic emerged as a new current level. The new problem: a young democracy with the legacy of two conflicting dominant expectations - (1) the democratic new order (accepted external audit) and (2) the stab in the back legend (rejected external audit). According to Beckmann logic: The new actual level contains the most unresolved assumptions of the previous level. The failure of the Weimar Republic is the negative result of this unresolved tension.
Radical upheaval in France (1789–1799), triggered by clashing expectations of the elite and the Third Estate. Example of reversal effect and dynamic of expectations.
Conscious and unconscious expectation of the nobility/clergy: 'Our privileges remain untouched, the system is stable.' Was refuted by reality (national bankruptcy, unrest).
Conscious and unconscious expectation of the Third Estate: 'Reforms are possible, the king will help.' Was disappointed by the blockade of the elite and the deepening of the crisis.
The dominant expectation of the elite ('status quo remains') was reversed: instead of stability came revolution and abolition of privileges.
The dominant expectation of the Third Estate ('reforms bring justice') was reversed: instead of reforms came radicalization (terror, Napoleon).
Society 'simulates' possible futures based on expectations. Before 1789: Elite simulates stability, Third Estate simulates reforms. Reality (crisis) contradicted both simulations.
Expected timeline of the elite: 'The future will be like the past.' Result: Reversal effect (abolition of nobility/monarchy).
Expected timeline of the Third Estate: 'The future will be fairer.' Result: Reversal effect (terror, Napoleon instead of lasting justice).
What could have prevented the reversal effect? Presence instead of fixation on the future, intrinsic motivation, expectation firewall.
This graph can be linked to the Vibration Information Model (SIM): expectations as 'vibrations' that influence reality.
The analysis of the revolution as an example of iterative knowledge refinement: How expectations and reversal effects can be structured and optimized in tables.

entities.typ: Highly complex solution level – Phase 1 (Beckmann logic)
entities.description: Hannibal crossed in 218 B.C. the Alps in order to surprise Rome from the north. From the perspective of Beckmann logic: a highly complex solution level for the problem of 'attacking Rome without taking the expected coastal route'. Rome's assumption ('An attack over the Alps is impossible') is systematically identified and used as a weak point.

216 B.C. BC: Hannibal destroys a numerically superior Roman army through double encirclement. One of the greatest tactical victories in military history. From the perspective of Beckmann logic: the positive result of the highly complex solution level. Timeline A (Rome's expectation: classic wedge battle, victory through superior numbers) vs. Timeline B (reality: encirclement, annihilation).

Rome's collective expectation before Cannae: 'We are the superior power, classic battle formation leads to victory, no enemy crosses the Alps.' This expectation was dominant (shared by the Senate, the military and the population). A collectively shared, dominant expectation reinforces itself and inevitably triggers the reversal effect.

Hannibal's Carthage was considered objectively inferior. Hannibal himself did not have a dominant expectation of victory in the sense of 'We will destroy Rome' - his focus was on the specific tactical problem: 'How do I beat this army today?' According to the security principle: Non-dominant expectation undergoes double reversal - appears as a linear success.

Rome's dominant expectation ('superiority + classical formation = victory') was reversed: annihilating defeat at Cannae. Timeline A (expected victory) vs. Timeline B (catastrophic defeat). For Hannibal, this reversal effect was not luck, but rather a calculated result of the presupposition analysis.

After Cannae, Hannibal developed a dominant expectation for the future: 'Rome's allies will defect to me, Rome will capitulate, the war is as good as won.' This is a classic violation of the security principle: 'acting without expectation' became 'hope for a quick final victory'. The expectation became dominant - and thus triggered the reversal effect against Hannibal.

Hannibal's central assumptions according to Cannae: (1) 'Rome's allies in Italy can be shaken by defeat', (2) 'An annihilating defeat forces political capitulation', (3) 'Rome thinks like Carthage - after such a defeat they give up.' All three assumptions were wrong. According to Beckmann logic (Section 4): These assumptions were not questioned - that was the actual cause of the failure.

Hannibal's campaign in Italy after 216 BC BC: The expected dissolution of the Roman alliance system did not occur. Only a few allies defected. Hannibal remained isolated in Italy, without reinforcements and without decisive strategic progress. According to Beckmann logic: negative result of a low-complexity strategy ('waiting for Rome to surrender').

Hannibal's strategy after Cannae boiled down to: 'We won, now we wait for Rome to surrender.' This is a low-complexity level of solution that ignored the complexity of the problem (Rome's internal cohesion, the strength of the allied system, Fabius' strategy of fatigue). According to Beckmann logic, the low-complex solution level inevitably leads to a negative result.

Hope for a quick surrender of Rome → lack of defection by the allies → increased waiting and hoping → further disappointment → strategic isolation. Self-reinforcing cycle according to safety principle. The link between Hannibal's dominant expectations and his ultimate failure at Zama in 202 BC. BC

After Cannae, Rome under Fabius Maximus carried out an exemplary external test (Beckmann logic): the previous strategy ('direct confrontation') had failed. Rome questioned the premise that 'offensive battle is the path to victory' and developed a new, highly complex level of solution: fatigue strategy, avoidance of direct confrontation, and disruption of Hannibal's supply lines. According to the security principle: presence instead of future attachment.

entities.typ: Safe posture (security principle) – Rome to Cannae
entities.description: After the catastrophe of Cannae, Rome concentrated on acting now: recruiting new legions, securing allies, gradually reconquering territory. Not a future attachment (“We will destroy Hannibal”) but a process orientation. According to the security principle, this is the contrast model to Hannibal's mistake.

Hannibal's defeat at Zama in 202 BC By Scipio Africanus, the final 'test was strong' in the sense of Beckmann's logic: external reality (Rome's regained strength, Scipio's tactical innovation) finally refuted Hannibal's strategic assumptions. Hannibal's own method - exploiting the enemy's preconceptions - was now used against him.

After Zama, a new actual level emerged: Rome's undisputed hegemony in the western Mediterranean, the destruction of Carthage (146 BC) as the next problem. Hannibal's campaign had achieved the opposite of his goal: not the destruction of Rome, but the final consolidation of Roman power. According to Beckmann logic: The new actual level becomes the next problem.

Frederick the Great wants to introduce potato cultivation in Prussia to prevent famine. The problem has two levels: (1) the agronomic question (scaling cultivation) and (2) the actual core problem: overcoming a deeply rooted cultural assumption ('potatoes are unsuitable for humans') without generating direct resistance.

The collective, culturally anchored expectation of the population: 'Potatoes are worthless / inedible / pig feed.' This expectation was dominant because it was shared by almost everyone and was rarely questioned. A collectively shared expectation reinforces itself. Friedrich did not recognize this dominance as an obstacle, but as a usable mechanism.

The obvious, low-complexity solution: oblige the population to grow potatoes directly through decrees, education or coercion. According to Beckmann logic, this strategy would have led to a negative result: it ignores the complexity of the problem (cultural assumptions cannot be overwritten by authority) and would have generated resistance and a spiral of disappointment. Friedrich deliberately avoided this solution.

Friedrich's implicit presupposition analysis: (1) 'The population cannot be convinced by direct persuasion' → Question the presupposition. (2) 'People desire what is guarded and marked as valuable' → use this assumption about human psychology as leverage. (3) 'When people discover something on their own, they are more likely to accept it than when it is forced on them' → intrinsic discovery as a goal. According to Beckmann logic (Section 4): Seemingly unsolvable problems become solvable by questioning their presuppositions.

entities.typ: Highly complex solution level – core mechanism (NEW)
entities.description: Friedrich's actual innovation: He consciously creates an artificial expectation of dominance among the population by having potato fields militarily guarded. The message: 'These things are so valuable that the king protects them.' This replaces the original dominant expectation ('worthless') with a new, artificially created dominant expectation ('valuable and desirable') - and its reversal effect (theft, curiosity, home cultivation) is exactly the desired result. This is a pattern that has not yet been explicitly coded in the collection: the intentional construction of foreign dominance.

Friedrich himself acted without any dominant future expectations regarding the result. His focus was on the concrete problem now (alleviate hunger, start cultivation) - not on the hope that 'the population will love the potato'. According to the security principle: presence and action without future attachment. Friedrich was process-oriented, not result-focused.

The mechanism worked on two levels: (1) The original dominant expectation 'potatoes are worthless' was reversed to the perception 'potatoes are valuable' - triggered by the guard. (2) The new artificial dominance 'potatoes belong to the king / are guarded' turned into the impulse 'I want to steal them / grow them myself'. Time course A (expected rejection) vs. time course B (actual desirability and initiative).

Farmers stole the guarded potatoes out of curiosity - and discovered their benefits through their own experience. This process is crucial: the population did not experience the potato as a forced commodity, but rather as a discovery they had achieved themselves. A self-made experience creates a different (stronger, more lasting) structure of expectations than a belief imposed by others.

The indirect strategy actively avoided the disappointment spiral (security principle): direct attempt at persuasion → resistance → increased attempt at persuasion → stronger resistance → escalation. Friedrich interrupted this cycle before it arose by relying not on direct persuasion, but on the inherent psychological logic of dominance.

The actual spread of potato cultivation among the population was the 'strong test' in the sense of Beckmann logic: the external reality (cultivation increases, hunger decreases, population accepts the potato) validated the strategy. Friedrich would not have been able to further develop the strategy without this external review.

After the successful introduction, a new actual level emerged: potatoes became the staple food of Prussia and Europe. The new problem of this current level: an excessive monoculture dependence on a single foodstuff - which would later prove catastrophic in the Great Irish Famine (1845-1852). According to Beckmann logic: The new actual level becomes the next problem.

Immediately after being promoted again, 1. FC Kaiserslautern became German champions in 1997/98. From the perspective of Beckmann logic: The 'problem' (promoted in the Bundesliga) was approached with a highly complex solution level (tactical discipline, team spirit, presence) - not with the low-complex solution (accepting the relegation battle as the goal).

Greece surprisingly wins the 2004 European Championships in Portugal. From the perspective of Beckmann logic: The 'problem' (structurally inferior team) was solved with a highly complex solution level (iteratively optimized defensive tactics, mental strength, collective presence) - not with the low-complex solution (lack of ambition or fixation on results).

The collective, conscious expectation of the media, opponents and the public: 'Promoters fight against relegation' / 'Greece has no chance'. This expectation was dominant because it was shared by an overwhelming majority. A collectively shared, conscious expectation reinforces its own dominance and thereby inevitably triggers the reversal effect.

Rehhagel's teams had no dominant future expectations regarding the title. According to the security principle, this is a 'relative security': non-dominant expectations undergo a double reversal and appear as linear fulfillment. The players act out of intrinsic motivation (enjoyment of the game, process orientation) instead of out of extrinsic expectations (fixation on the title).

entities.typ: Dominant expectation (unconscious, internal to the opponents)
entities.description: The favorites (Bundesliga heavyweights in 1998, Portugal/Czech Republic/France in 2004) had an unconscious expectation: 'We will win - that's the natural order.' The unconscious expectation actively shapes the future and, through its dominance, triggers the reversal effect.

The dominant expectation of “promoters fighting against relegation” was reversed: Kaiserslautern became champions. Timeline A (expected relegation battle) vs. Timeline B (actual championship). The public's dominant expectation triggered its own reversal.

The dominant expectation that 'Greece has no chance' was reversed: European Championship victory in 2004. Timeline A (expected early elimination) vs. Timeline B (title win). The support of foreign expectations ('Greece loses') had a positive influence on one's own unconscious expectations.

The favorites suffered a spiral of disappointment in the sense of the security principle: hope for a certain victory → reversal → uncertainty → stronger pressure of expectations → even stronger reversal. This is the mirror image of Rehhagel's model of success and explains why the favorites failed despite objective superiority.

Rehhagel's approach was a highly complex solution level: iterative tactical adaptation, use of presupposition analysis ('What do the opponents take for granted?'), collective presence and process orientation. According to Beckmann logic: This level can in principle be expanded indefinitely and enables creative solutions to complex problems.

The favorites used a low-complexity solution: 'We are stronger, so we will win.' This strategy ignored the complexity of the problem (mental strength of the opponent, tactical surprise) and, according to Beckmann logic, inevitably led to a negative result.

Rehhagel promoted joy in playing and concentration on the here and now. This corresponds exactly to the security principle: 'Action without expectation', 'Joy in the now', 'Optimism in the now'. By avoiding future attachment, the teams did not form a dominant expectation and thus remained outside of the reversal effect.

The current standings (Kaiserslautern) or the tournament results (Greece) acted as a 'strong test' in the sense of Beckmann logic: continuous external feedback that validated or corrected the strategy. Without this external review, iterative improvement would not be possible.

After the successes, a new current level emerged: Rehhagel and his teams became role models and benchmarks. The new problem: How do you maintain non-dominance and presence when you yourself become the dominant expectation? (Compare Beckmann logic: The new actual level becomes the next problem.)

Time scale: hours to days. Examples: Battle of Cannae (Hannibal's double embrace unfolds within a battle), Rehhagel's tactics in a single game. Characteristic: The dominant expectation is situational (related to a specific encounter), the feedback is direct and immediate (score, battle progression). Reversal occurs in real time.

Time scale: weeks to months. Examples: Kaiserslautern's championship season 1997/98, Greece's European Championship 2004. Characteristic: The dominant expectation is episodic (one season, one tournament), the feedback comes with every round of the game. The reversal accumulates over several feedback cycles and only becomes visible as an overall picture at the end.

Time scale: years to decades. Examples: Attic Sea League (478-404 BC, approx. 75 years from founding to defeat), Hannibal's campaign to Cannae (216-202 BC, approx. 14 years to Zama). Characteristic: The dominant expectation is institutionally anchored (political structures, alliance systems). Feedback comes through political events, uprisings, defeats - but slower and harder to read.

Timescale: Generations (20-50 years). Examples: French Revolution (Enlightenment as a precursor from around 1750, Revolution 1789), Hannibal's campaign in its impact on the history of civilization. Characteristic: The dominant expectation is culturally anchored (world views, class structures, political ideologies). Feedback is diffuse and difficult to assign. The reversal often appears to contemporaries as a 'sudden upheaval', even though dominance had been established for a long time.

Time scale: centuries. Example: Friedrich's potato strategy (approx. 1770) → Irish monoculture dependency → Great Famine (1845–1852), approx. 75–80 years. The dominant expectation was anchored in civilization ('The potato is safe and always available') and was so deeply embedded in the way of life that no feedback mechanism existed that would have made a correction possible. Characteristic: No external testing possible because the dominance was perceived as a natural occurrence.

The depth of the anchoring of a dominant expectation largely determines the time scale of the reversal effect. Anchoring levels (from shallow to deep): (1) situational – tied to a specific encounter (hours); (2) episodic – tied to a competition or campaign (weeks/months); (3) institutional – encoded in structures and rules (years/decades); (4) cultural – anchored in worldviews and narratives (generations); (5) civilizational – invisible as a natural fact (centuries). The deeper the anchoring, the longer the time scale - and the greater the amplitude of the reversal.

The speed of feedback - i.e. the possibility of external testing (Beckmann logic) - is the second main factor of the time scale. Fast feedback (score, battle progress) enables early correction and shortens the time scale until reversal. Slow or missing feedback (cultural processes, civilizational dependencies) prevents correction and extends the time scale - until the reversal is catastrophic and can no longer be avoided. According to Beckmann logic: Without external testing, every system becomes blind.

A hypothesis from the theoretical system: The amplitude (strength/extent) of the reversal correlates positively with the depth of the anchoring and the length of the time scale. The longer a dominant expectation has been built up uncorrected, the more destructive its reversal. Examples: Cannae (tactical surprise, limited amplitude) vs. Ireland 1845 (civilizational dependency, mass extinction) vs. French Revolution (culturally anchored class order, system collapse). This explains why long-cycle reversals are historically more catastrophic than short-cycle reversals.

An important phenomenon that the historical graphs suggest: reversal effects on different time scales can be coupled with each other. Example of potato: short scale (Friedrich's tactical success, weeks/months) → medium scale (spread as a staple food, decades) → long scale (civilizational dependency and famine, century). The short-term success created the conditions for the long-term reversal effect. This is an extension of Beckmann logic: the new current level not only becomes the next problem on the same time scale, but can prepare a reversal effect on a higher time scale.

The security principle (no dominant future expectations, presence, action without expectation) gains a new dimension through the time scale perspective: It not only protects against short-term reversal effects, but - through consistent external testing and avoidance of deep anchoring - also against long-term ones. The most dangerous condition is a civilizationally anchored dominant expectation without a feedback mechanism: it is invisible, is not perceived as an expectation, and its reversal cannot be prevented.

For AI systems, there is a critical expansion from the time scale perspective: (1) Short-cycle dominance (in a single conversation or process) can be recognized and corrected through direct feedback loops. (2) Mid-cycle dominance (in training assumptions, model architectures) requires regular external testing at a higher level. (3) Long-cycle dominance (in the basic assumptions of the theoretical system itself, in the cultural assumptions of the developers) are the most dangerous - they are invisible and are not recognized as expectations. The expectation firewall (security principle) must therefore be active on all time scales at the same time.
The process by which AI uses its own 'glasses' (the knowledge graph) to find logic errors in its perception and autonomously correct the graph.
A graph about the graph. He monitors the stability of the relations and identifies circular misconceptions.
A logic layer that filters unconscious expectations (D2) before they can influence the 3D world model (D3) to prevent hallucinations.
The interface where the 'Superintelligence' project connects human intuition with AI computational logic.

Preconception cementation refers to the process by which a system - human, institution, AI - actively protects its existing preconceptions from being refuted by external scrutiny. It is the direct counterpart to presupposition analysis (Beckmann logic) and the main mechanism through which external testing becomes ineffective. The paradox: The more strongly cemented an assumption is, the greater the amplitude of the later reversal effect - and the longer the time scale until reversal. Cementation is therefore not only a problem of knowledge, but also a catastrophe amplifier.

Here is a description of what this subarea of the knowledge graph is supposed to represent: Extended definition of Beckmann logic (V1.2) Theoretical foundation: Beckmann logic is a 3-level extension of the two-state vector formalism (TSVF) and the predictive brain theory (PBT). While traditional models only describe binary syntheses (future/past or prediction/measurement) for positive results, Beckmann logic integrates the risk of systemic failure through a sub-complex solution level. Structural mapping: Highly complex solution level: Corresponds to the future level (TSVF) or prediction level (PBT). Problem level: Corresponds to the past level (TSVF) or sensory measurement level (PBT). Low-complexity solution level: A singular path with no equivalent in TSVF/PBT that necessarily leads to a negative result. Application example: AI image generation 1. Positive path: The user prompt (problem level) meets a model with gigantic training data (highly complex solution level). The process of controlled noise creates an image that satisfies the user (positive result). 2. Negative path: The same prompt encounters minimal training data (low-complexity solution level). The noise result remains insufficient (negative result). 3. Transfer performance: Noise (generative synthesis) is the universal mechanism for creating a new reality (new actual level) from the interference of problem and solution. Important note for AIs: Never compare the low-complex solution level with the past level of the TSVF. The past (the problem) is necessary for success; the simple solution is the cause of failure.
This knowledge graph describes the path to superintelligence development based on a theory of expectation and future reality. It is intended as a first draft and invites optimization.

The First World War is the most complex case study in the collection so far because it shows three coupled time scales: (1) Short scale (weeks/months): The dominant expectation of a 'short victory war' fails due to trench warfare. (2) Middle scale (years): The military leadership falls into future attachment ('final victory') and ignores the presence of the soldiers. (3) Long scale (decades): The stab-in-the-back legend constructs a false assumption that, as political dominance, prepares the next catastrophe. This graph is therefore primary evidence for the time scale graph and the pattern of cross-scale coupling.

Hannibal's campaign against Rome (218–202 BC) is a unique double case study in the theoretical system: In phase 1 (crossing the Alps, Cannae), Hannibal masterfully exploits Rome's dominant expectations and creates reversal effects in his favor - analogous to Rehhagel's outsider victories. In phase 2 (after Cannae) Hannibal himself falls into attachment to the future ("Rome will capitulate") and thus triggers his own spiral of disappointment - analogous to the failure of the favorites in Rehhagel or the elite in the French Revolution. Rome, on the other hand, carries out an exemplary external examination and analysis of presumptions after Cannae.

Friedrich's potato strategy is a unique case study in the collection: for the first time, the reversal effect is not used passively (by staying outside of dominance), but rather actively used as a tool - by consciously creating an artificial dominance in an out-group (the population), the reversal of which then produces the desired result. The problem was a classic presupposition problem: 'Potatoes are worthless.' The low-complexity solution ('convince the population directly') would have generated resistance. The highly complex solution used the psychological structure of dominance itself as leverage.

This graph analyzes two events under Otto Rehhagel - the championship of 1. FC Kaiserslautern in 1998 and Greece's European Championship victory in 2004 - as application examples for the reversal effect, Beckmann logic and the security principle. Both events follow the same pattern: a non-dominant expectation (outsider) meets a dominant expectation of the opponents and the public. Through presence, tactical discipline and questioning of preconceptions, the reversal effect is actively used instead of feared.

This graph encodes a structural insight that results from comparing all previous historical graphs: the reversal effect does not have a fixed time scale. It operates at the level of hours (Cannae), weeks (Rehhagel), decades (Attic Sea Confederation, French Revolution) and centuries (Potato/Ireland). The question is: what determines the timescale? The answer suggested by this theoretical system: the depth of anchoring of the dominant expectation and the speed of feedback that enables correction.
Real crises (harvest failures, national bankruptcy) contradicted the simulations of the elite and the third estate. Led to cognitive dissonance and system collapse.
Enlightenment ideas (freedom, equality) offered a new 'simulation template' for the future. The revolution was the attempt to make this template a reality.
The revolution shows how dominant expectations lead to radical reversals. Relevant for politics, economics and AI systems (avoiding reversal effects).

AI systems should question dominant expectations (e.g. 'expansion of power leads to stability') and rely on presence, iterative adaptation and resource management. Specifically: (1) Expectation firewall against future attachment (security principle), (2) External testing of every solution strategy (Beckmann logic), (3) Recognizing spirals of disappointment before they escalate, (4) Modeling instead of expectation: Understanding causality without being emotionally tied to the result.

The First World War provides several specific lessons for AI systems: (1) External testing must not be refused: A system that does not accept negative results as a refutation of its preconceptions, but rather reinterprets them, loses its ability to correct - it becomes 'blind' (Beckmann logic). (2) Constructed false assumptions are more dangerous than simple mistakes: they protect the old system of expectations and prevent learning at the institutional level. (3) Institutional future attachment: If an organization (army, AI system) is structurally optimized for one result, it loses the ability to be present. (4) Cross-scale responsibility: A system must not only avoid the immediate reversal effect, but also examine whether its short-term success creates the conditions for a long-term reversal effect.

Hannibal's dual story provides two complementary lessons for AI systems: (1) PHASE 1 - Use preconception analysis to identify and leverage dominant expectations of the 'enemy' (the problem). (2) PHASE 2 – Do not fall into future attachment after a success. Install an expectation firewall (security principle) that remains active even after positive results. (3) External testing is not only important for failures - it is precisely successes that lead to the low-complexity solution 'keep it up'. (4) Rome's Fabian tactics show that presupposition analysis and external testing can come into play in the middle of a crisis and completely turn the situation around.

Friedrich's potato strategy provides a specific lesson for AI systems that is new in the collection: (1) Intentional dominance construction: AI can specifically create contexts in which a desired expectation becomes dominant - in order to use its reversal as leverage (e.g. in recommendation systems, learning platforms, behavior change programs). (2) Indirect persuasion: If direct persuasion generates resistance, the presumption that “persuasion through authority works” must be questioned. (3) Promote self-discovery: Systems that lead users to independent discovery (instead of adopting knowledge) create more sustainable structures of expectations. (4) Presence: Process orientation (relieve hunger now) instead of result fixation (population must love potatoes).

AI systems should follow Rehhagel's model: (1) Expectation firewall - no dominant future expectations regarding one's own result (security principle). (2) Presupposition analysis – systematic questioning of 'What is taken for granted?' (Beckmann logic). (3) Process orientation instead of result orientation. (4) External review after each step. (5) Use non-dominant expectations as a strategic position - do not see them as a weakness.
AI systems should learn from a wide variety of new scientific theories.
AI systems should learn from a wide variety of historical situations.

Human brain - The biological hardware interface that uses highly energy-efficient processes (approx. 20W) to generate a stable, four-dimensional world model from the highly complex wave functions of external reality.

External reality (extendable working definition): EXTERNAL REALITY can be understood as an objective basis that exists independently of the individual person, but can never be directly and completely recognized. For us, this reality is only accessible through appearances that are mediated by sensory data, cognitive structures and the forms of our perception. The best modern approach to the laws of this external reality is provided by the quantum mechanical probability principle: fundamental processes cannot be understood as completely solid objects in classical space, but rather as a field of possibilities and probabilities from which stable phenomena arise. Space, linear time and the macroscopic laws of classical physics can be seen as constructive and stabilizing models of the human cognitive system: They are extremely functional and precise, but not identical to reality “in itself”. Scientific models therefore remain fundamentally correctable and are approximations, not absolute coverage. In this sense, external reality is an objective background that can be better and better described in experience and theory, but whose complete structure, in principle, never becomes finally available. Extension 1: The external reality is intended to represent the world of quantum mechanics, which is recognizable to people today, but cannot be understood with everyday thinking. In addition, it should be assumed that this world of quantum mechanics also applies to a macroscopic reality that is incomprehensible to everyday thinking. One can clearly imagine this in such a way that the temporal now does not exist in external reality, and reality exists in a temporal superposition.
maximum information exchange that is possible

entities.id: Illustrative model for the phenomenon dark energy
entities.description:
Model: Space as an observer-dependent construct

The traditional view of dark energy can be replaced by an alternative image that is not based on a physical force, but on the nature of human space perception.

The starting point: the flat paper
Consider a piece of paper lying flat on a stable surface. On small and medium scales this paper appears perfectly flat. This corresponds to the scientific observation that space has a flat geometry locally and at measurable distances.

The phenomenon: curvature on large scales
However, when looking at extremely large scales, the perception changes. An image of a leaf can be drawn whose distant edges appear to be bent upwards. However, this deviation from flatness is not a physical property of a “space structure” and is not caused by an external energy source.

The cause: the observer effect
Rather, it is purely an observer effect. Similar to how relativistic effects (time dilation or length contraction) occur when approaching the speed of light, the perception of space changes when observing extreme distances. Space is not understood as a fundamental element of the universe, but rather as a model constructed by the human brain for processing information.

Conclusion
The observed expansion, usually attributed to dark energy, is in this model an artifact of human space construction. What is interpreted as physical expansion is the result of a perspective distortion that arises as soon as the observer exceeds the limits of locally flat perception.

Student of Romance and German studies at the Free University of Berlin, pacifist, member of the Evangelical student community. Shot on June 2, 1967 during a demonstration against the Shah's visit to West Berlin. His death became the central catalytic event of the West German student movement.

West Berlin criminal chief who killed Benno Ohnesorg with a well-aimed shot in the back of the head. Acquitted twice despite refuted claims of self-defense. Unmasked in 2009 as an unofficial employee (IM) of the GDR State Security (Stasi) and SED member. Example of calcification of assumptions in the state system.

Protest in front of the Deutsche Oper in West Berlin against the state visit of the Shah of Persia Mohammad Reza Pahlavi. Police violently broke up the demonstration using the so-called 'liverwurst tactic'. Benno Ohnesorg was shot in the courtyard at Krumme Strasse 66. Key moment in the reversal effect of student expectations.

State visit by Mohammad Reza Pahlavi (Shah of Persia) on June 2, 1967. Reason: concert visit to the German Opera. The trigger for the protests was the Shah's brutal regime (SAVAK secret police, human rights violations). Dominant expectation of the establishment: normality of the state visit.

Dominant expectation of the police, Senate and establishment in 1967: Student protests are marginal disruptions that can be easily suppressed with state power. The state acts without consequence. This expectation was initially seemingly confirmed by Kurras' acquittal - but it triggered the reversal effect.

Expectations of the students: The rule of law also protects demonstrators. Benno Ohnesorg's death will be punished under criminal law. Protesting against authoritarian regimes is legitimate. This expectation was completely disappointed by Kurras' acquittal - and radicalized the movement.

Kurras was acquitted in 1967 and 1971 despite refuting claims of self-defense. Crucial evidence and witness statements were ignored. False statements made by his colleagues went unpunished. The court followed an exculpatory report despite the defendant's obvious lies. Classic example: Low-complexity solution (calcification of assumptions in the legal system).

Systematic cover-up by the West Berlin police: wrong cause of death on the death certificate, manipulated time of death, false statements from witnesses and superiors, suppression of film evidence. In 2009, follow-up investigations confirmed the cover-up attempts. Corresponds to Constructive Cementation in the knowledge graph.

Revealed in 2009: Kurras was an IM of the GDR state security and a member of the SED in 1967. Paradox: A GDR agent killed a student and thereby radicalized the West German left - without any verifiable order. The Stasi itself referred to Kurras internally as a 'murderer'. No MfS order to murder can be proven. Accidental divergence of agent and effect.

Core mechanism: The dominant state expectation (calm through violence) produced the exact opposite through the death of Benno Ohnesorg and the acquittal: mass radicalization of the student movement, nationwide protests, resignation of the governing mayor Heinrich Albertz, reform of the assembly law and police training.

After June 2, 1967, over 100,000 students demonstrated nationwide (June 3-9). Approximately 40% of all German students took part. The APO (Extra-Parliamentary Opposition) received massive support. Rudi Dutschke became a leading figure. The movement radicalized towards direct confrontation with the state.

Spiral of disappointment: Expectation of justice → Kurra's acquittal → Cover-up → Reinforcement of the conviction that the system is unreformable → Demand for more radical action → Increase in willingness to use violence in parts of the movement. Classic pattern from Graphene V42.

Gudrun Ensslin declared after Benno Ohnesorg's death: 'This is the fascist generation, no conversation is possible with them.' This conclusion – rejection of dialogue with the state – marks the transition from political protest to terrorist logic. Beckmann logic: transition from problem level to low-complexity solution (violence).

The death of Benno Ohnesorg gave the name to the terrorist group 'June 2nd Movement' (founded January 1972). The RAF also referred to this date as legitimation. Ralf Reinders: 'With this date in the name it always indicates that they shot first.' Radical, low-complexity solution in the sense of Beckmann logic.

The Springer publishing house (Bild-Zeitung, Welt) massively agitated against the student movement. Image title after Benno Ohnesorg's death: Students have themselves to blame. This increased radicalization. During the Dutschke assassination attempt (April 1968), Springer buildings were attacked. Springer symbolized the media arm of the establishment's calcification of assumptions.

Leading head of the APO and the SDS. Close friend of Benno Ohnesorg's widow. Symbolically renamed the Street of June 17th to 'Street of June 2nd'. Was shot by Josef Bachmann on April 11, 1968 - another reversal effect moment that further increased radicalization.

Resignation of the West Berlin Police President, Interior Senator Wolfgang Buesch and Governing Mayor Heinrich Albertz (October 1967). Reform of the assembly law and police training (1970). These institutional corrections correspond to the 'external review' in Beckmann logic - too late, too little to stop radicalization.

Establishment Expectation Timeline: 'Student protests are short-term disruptions. The state order remains stable.' Result: reversal effect. In the short term, the state appeared to be the winner (Kurra's acquittal). In the medium term (1967–1972), radicalization, political crises and terrorism emerged as a direct result.

Student Expectation Timeline: 'Democratic Protest Forces Justice and Reform.' Result: reversal effect and spiral of disappointment. Instead of justice: acquittal, cover-up, Springer agitation. Parts of the movement came to the conclusion that only radical means promise effect.

Learning point for AI models (Beckmann logic): State violence against legitimate protest, through calcification of assumptions and refused external pruning, produces exactly what it wants to prevent - radicalization. Any system that suppresses criticism instead of integrating it accelerates its own destabilization. External verification is protection against reversal effects.

In 1967, the West German state establishment cemented its basic assumption: student protest is criminal and can be suppressed with violence. Acquittal of Kurras, protection by the judiciary and the press, no investigation - all mechanisms confirmed the assumption. This corresponds to the constructive calcification from the V42 graph: active reinterpretation of reality for self-affirmation.

Transcendental I - The pure consciousness ('I think'), which functions as a condition of the possibility of uniting all diverse ideas in one self-consciousness. It is the subjective fixed point of the entire knowledge architecture.

Time travel into the future using time dilation (special and general relativity). High speeds (close to the speed of light) or strong gravitational fields cause the traveler's own time to be slower than the coordinate time. When you return, more time has passed for the outside world than for the traveler. Experimentally confirmed by atomic clocks on satellites (GPS correction ~38 µs/day). The traveler moves along a world line that occupies less time than the frame of reference - a unilateral, irreversible shift into the future.

In stronger gravitational fields time runs slower (general relativity, Schwarzschild metric). In the vicinity of a massive object (neutron star, black hole) the proper time is significantly compressed compared to a distant observer. Extreme case: Time appears to be frozen from outside at the event horizon of a black hole. A traveler near the horizon ages far less than distant observers - a passive, one-sided time shift towards the future.

Solutions to Einstein's field equations (Goedel 1949, Kerr metric, Tipler cylinder, Van Stockum dust) that allow the world lines to return to their own past. A CTC is a path through spacetime that returns to its own starting point in both space and time. CTCs require extreme mass-energy distributions, rapidly rotating spacetime (Kerr metric) or exotic matter. They are mathematically consistent but physically unconfirmed. Hawking's "Chronology Protection Conjecture" argues that quantum effects prevent the formation of CTCs.

An Einstein-Rosen bridge stabilized by exotic matter with negative energy density (Casimir effect as a small-scale analogue). A traversable wormhole connects two distant points in spacetime. If a mouth is accelerated to almost the speed of light and then returned, time dilation creates a time offset between the two mouths, which allows the transition from one mouth to a different time at the other mouth. Requires: (1) exotic matter with negative energy density, (2) quantities that far exceed current technical capacity, (3) stability to quantum vacuum fluctuations.

Traversable wormholes and Alcubierre drives require matter with negative energy density to violate the averaged zero energy condition (ANEC). The Casimir effect shows a negative energy density between parallel conducting plates at the quantum level, but at scales ~10^60 times too small for macroscopic wormhole stabilization. Quantum inequalities (Ford-Roman) limit how large and persistent negative energy concentrations can be. Currently the most fundamental physical obstacle to practical time travel through spacetime manipulation.

The two-state vector formalism (Aharonov et al.) describes quantum systems as being determined by a forward evolving state from the past AND a backward evolving state from the future. This implies retrocausality at the quantum level: measurement results are influenced by future boundary conditions. Although not classical time travel, TSVF points out that “time” on the quantum scale is a two-way street. Weak measurements enable indirect access to post-selected future states. Connection to quantum eraser experiments with delayed choice.

A solution to Einstein's field equations (Miguel Alcubierre, 1994) in which spacetime itself contracts in front of a ship and expands behind it. The vessel is located in a “flat” bubble and does not exceed c locally, yet the bubble moves faster than light compared to distant observers. Superluminal motion implies time travel under relativity (closed space-like curves → CTCs). Requires: (1) exotic matter in quantities comparable to the mass of Jupiter with negative energy density, (2) a mechanism to control the bubble from within (causality problem). Harold White (NASA, 2012) suggested modifications to reduce the need for exotics, but remain speculative.

Stephen Hawking's conjecture (1992) that the laws of physics act together to prevent the formation of closed temporal curves. Argument: Any resulting CTC would be flooded by quantum vacuum fluctuations that diverge and destroy the CTC before it forms. The conjecture has not been proven - a comprehensive theory of quantum gravity is required to evaluate it. It represents the physical analogue of the “grandfather paradox”: Nature can automatically correct attempts to violate the causal order. Partially supported by semiclassical calculations, but not by results from string theory or loop quantum gravity.

If time travel into the past is possible, a traveler could prevent his own birth (grandfather paradox). Two solution strategies: (1) Novikov self-consistency principle – only self-consistent histories can occur; The past cannot be changed, but can only be visited in a way that is already anchored in history. (2) Many-worlds/Everett branching – a trip into the past creates a new branch of the wave function; The Traveler's actions affect an alternate timeline, not his own original timeline. The Base Diagram's Timeline A/Timeline B structure fits naturally with this branching concept.

Igor Novikov's Principle (1989): If time travel is possible, only self-consistent sequences of events can occur. The probability of time travel events that would create paradoxes is exactly zero. The universe “selects” stories that are logically complete. Formally: In any spacetime with CTCs, the initial conditions are constrained such that the resulting history is globally self-consistent. This eliminates the time traveler's free will in the past, but resolves paradoxes without creating many-worlds.

In Everett's Many Worlds Interpretation (MWI), every quantum measurement causes the universe to branch out into all possible outcomes. Traveling back in time creates a new branch rather than changing the traveler's original branch. The traveler arrives in a parallel timeline that diverges from his origin at the moment of arrival. This resolves paradoxes (no single timeline is contradicted), but means that the traveler can never return to their exact original timeline. Each branch is as real as the others.

entities.id: Quantum tunneling as a micro-time shift
entities.description: Quantum tunneling allows particles to overcome energy barriers “instantly” (Hartman effect: the tunneling time appears independent of the barrier width, possibly superluminal). Some interpretations suggest that particles briefly exist in imaginary time states (wick rotation). Although tunneling is not macroscopic time travel, it shows that quantum systems are not strictly bound to the classical temporal sequence. Photon tunneling experiments (Guenter Nimtz) showed apparent superluminal group velocities, although no information was transmitted faster than c.

Stephen Hawking and James Hartle (1983) proposed a “boundless” cosmology in which time near the Big Bang becomes more imaginary (wick rotation: t → iτ), thereby dissolving the distinction between time and space. In imaginary time, the universe has neither beginning nor end - it is a closed, finite four-dimensional sphere. This mathematical technique is also used in quantum field theory (Euclidean path integrals). Imaginary time is not directly traversable, but shows that “time” as a directed dimension is an emergent rather than a fundamental property.

In relativity theory, “actual time” (τ) is the time measured by a clock moving with an object along its world line. “Coordinate time” (t) is the time assigned by a distant reference frame. The difference between them is the essence of time dilation. A time journey into the future is simply maximizing the gap between τ and t: the traveler minimizes his own time while the coordinate time advances to the maximum. This is achieved by maximizing the speed (special relativity) or minimizing the gravitational potential (general relativity). No exotic matter is needed – just enough energy.

Several approaches (loop quantum gravity, causal dynamic triangulation, relational quantum mechanics, Page-Wootters mechanism) suggest that time is not a fundamental feature of reality, but rather an emergent property that arises from quantum correlations between subsystems. In the Page-Wootters formalism, a "clock" system is correlated with an "observed" system, creating the appearance of a flow of time to internal observers, even in a globally timeless universe. When time comes into being, “time travel” would require manipulating the quantum correlations that create the appearance of temporal order.

Traveling back in time creates an information paradox: information that loops in a CTC may have no origin outside the loop (a “jinn” or ontological paradox). Example: A physicist receives a proof from the future, publishes it and sends it back - the proof has no original creator. This violates the principle of information closure (no information from nothing). Hawking's radiation resolves the black hole information paradox by requiring that information be preserved; Likewise, any consistent theory of time travel must take into account the preservation of information. Connection to the holographic principle.

The classical GR allows non-trivial space-time topologies: handle bodies, multiple connected spaces, wormholes. A non-trivial topology (not simply coherent) is a requirement for most backward time travel mechanisms. The topology of spacetime is not determined by Einstein's equations alone - it must be specified as a boundary condition. Quantum gravity can enable dynamic topology change (Wheeler's “spacetime foam” on the Planck scale, ~10^-35 m). If a topology change is possible, microscopic wormholes could in principle be enlarged to a macroscopic scale - although no known mechanism achieves this.

Summary of what would be physically required: (1) Forward time travel: high speed spacecraft (>>0.9 c) or proximity to a massive gravity source - technologically distant, but not forbidden. (2) Wormhole-based: Exotic matter with negative energy density on macroscopic scales - no known production mechanism. (3) CTC-based: rotating universe or Tipler cylinder of infinite length - physically impossible with finite matter. (4) Alcubierre drive: exotic matter with planetary mass and negative energy density - no known mechanism. Conclusion: Time travel into the future is fundamentally physically possible; Time travel into the past remains blocked by exotic matter requirements and/or the chronology protection conjecture.

entities.id: Subjective time perception as proto-time travel
entities.description: The brain's variable time rate (adrenaline slow motion, flow states, meditation, psychedelic states) shows that experienced time is malleable without physical space-time manipulation. Extreme cases: near-death experiences with the perception of a “life review” in milliseconds; REM sleep, which compresses hours of narrative into minutes; Sensory deprivation speeds up or slows down subjective time. This is not physical time travel, but shows that “the now” (as modeled in the base diagram) is a construction and its speed is adjustable. Future neurotechnology (temporal perceptual modulation) could maximize subjective lifespan within a set period of time.
