Experiments test whether quantum measurement decisions in the present can influence past quantum events: (1) Wheeler's delayed choice experiment - the decision to measure wave or particle properties after the photon has "already chosen" its path retroactively determines history. (2) Delayed-Choice Quantum Eraser (Kim et al., 2000) – erasing which-path information retroactively restores interference. (3) Leggett-Garg inequality tests – investigating whether quantum systems have certain states before measurement. These experiments do not allow superluminal signal transmission, but show that quantum causality is not strictly forward-directed. TSVF provides the mathematical framework.
describes possible solutions that cannot be clearly assigned to the high-complex or low-complex solution level
Describes the complexity of the problem of time travel.
Our external reality, perhaps the M-brane (from string theory), in which we humans exist
Alternative external reality, perhaps another M-brane (from string theory) that exists alongside our own M-brane. We humans do not exist in this alternative M-Brane.

An external super-reality in which both our external reality (our M-Brane) and an alternative external reality (alternative M-Brane) exist and are connected to each other.

The external super reality solves the information paradox of time travel in relation to a subordinate external reality.

Every process, copying and transferring information from one subordinate external reality to another, is logged in it.
The problem of the possibility of time travel is successfully solved

entities.id: Highly complex solution level for time travel
entities.description: describes highly complex possible solutions for time travel

The central paradox: If the universe is physically deterministic (every state necessarily follows from previous states via natural laws), then every decision a human makes was already determined at the Big Bang. If the universe is instead quantum mechanically indeterministic, decisions are random - not free. In both cases, “will” in the sense of genuine authorship over one's own actions seems impossible. Nevertheless, the phenomenology of deliberation, choice and responsibility is undeniable. The paradox has three main positions: hard determinism (no free will), libertarian free will (real, non-physical freedom), and compatibilism (freedom redefined as compatible with determinism). All three lead to profound consequences for ethics, law, punishment and the design of AI systems.

The principle that every physical event has a sufficient physical cause - no non-physical cause (such as a "will" or a "soul") ever intervenes in the causal chain of the physical world. If the brain is a physical system, then every neuronal release has a complete physical explanation without remainder. The “will” would be either (a) identical to a physical brain state (physicalism) or (b) causally inert (epiphenomenalism) or (c) non-existent as a separate entity (eliminativism). Causal closure is the basic premise of hard determinism and the greatest challenge to libertarian free will.

The position that (1) determinism is true: every event, including every human decision, necessarily follows from prior causes and natural laws, AND (2) true free will requires the ability to have done something different under identical conditions, AND (3) therefore free will does not exist. Supporters: Baron d'Holbach, Derk Pereboom. Consequence: Moral responsibility in its traditional form is unjustified. Punishment can only be justified as deterrence or rehabilitation, never as deserved retribution. Hard determinism accepts the conclusion that “one could not have done it otherwise” and draws the ethical consequences from it.

The position that true free will exists and is incompatible with determinism - therefore determinism must be false. Agent causality (Roderick Chisholm): Agents are irreducible causes that initiate causal chains from “outside” the physical causal order. The agent is not just the locus of previous causes, but a real originator. Problem: How does the agent cause without being caused? What is the metaphysical mechanism? Quantum indeterminism is sometimes referred to as the “gap” in which the will could operate – but randomness is not the same as freedom. The Hard Problem of Free Will: Any gap in the causal chain seems to introduce randomness and no control.

The prevailing position in academic philosophy: Free will and determinism are compatible because “free will” should be redefined. A free action is not an action that is not uncaused, but one that is caused by the correct internal process - specifically, by the agent's own desires, values, and considerations, without external coercion or internal dysfunction. Key versions: (1) Classical compatibilism (Hume, Mill): Freedom = acting according to one's own wishes without external pressure. (2) Hierarchical compatibilism (Frankfurt): Freedom = acting on desires that one supports through reflection (second-order desires). (3) Responsiveness to reasons (Fischer & Ravizza): Freedom = the mechanism that causes the action responds appropriately to reasons. Compatibilism upholds moral responsibility and is the basis of most legal systems.

entities.id: Libet experiment and the readiness potential
entities.description: Benjamin Libet's experiment from 1983: subjects spontaneously bend their wrist while looking at the watch. They report on the moment of the conscious “will to move” (W). The EEG detects a readiness potential that begins approximately 550 ms before the movement. W occurs only about 200 ms before the movement - this means that the brain initiates the movement about 350 ms before conscious awareness of the decision. Interpretation: Unconscious neural processes precede the conscious decision and can trigger it. Later replications (Haggard, Soon et al. 2008 using fMRI) show that the outcome of a decision can be predicted based on brain activity up to 10 seconds before the subject is aware of their decision. Challenge: Does this eliminate conscious free will or does it simply show that “will” is the conscious surface of a deeper neural process that still belongs to “someone”?

Libet himself did not come to the conclusion that free will was impossible. His most important finding: Even if the readiness potential precedes the conscious intention, subjects can consciously veto the movement - they can sense the emerging urge and consciously suppress it. The veto occurs approximately 100–200 ms before the movement, within the window of conscious perception. Libet suggested: Free will is not the initiator of an action, but the final editor - a “free will not”. This preserves a form of conscious ability to act without consciousness having to be the original cause. Points of criticism: (1) Veto decisions themselves have potential for preparedness. (2) The “will to veto” is equally determined. (3) But the hierarchical structure (unconsciously initiate, consciously process) fits well with Frankfurt's hierarchical compatibilism.

Quantum mechanics introduces true, irreducible randomness at the subatomic level (Heisenberg uncertainty, radioactive decay, quantum measurement). If neuronal processes are sensitive to events at the quantum level (e.g. at ion channels, Penrose-Hameroff microtubules), then the brain is not strictly deterministic. This seems to open a gap for free will. However, two problems arise: (1) Randomness is not freedom - a decision caused by quantum noise is no more mine than one caused by classical physics. (2) Neuronal processes at the macro level can be largely isolated from quantum noise through decoherence. The Penrose-Hameroff Orchestrated Objective Reduction (Orch OR) theory assumes that quantum coherence in microtubules is the physical substrate of consciousness and will - but it lacks empirical support.

entities.id: TSVF as a solution to the dilemma between chance and determinism
entities.description: The two-state vector formalism (Aharonov) offers a third option beyond determinism and randomness: retrocausal teleological determination. In TSVF, the current quantum state is constrained by BOTH a past initial state AND a future final state (post-selection). Applied to free will: A decision is neither forced solely by previous causes (hard determinism) nor random (quantum noise) - it is co-determined by a future boundary condition that represents the actor's own teleological structure (values, goals, character). The “will” would be the future state vector that acts backwards on the present. This is not physically local in time, but is logically consistent. It is mapped directly to the “Time Vectors” node of the base diagram: “Both information from the past (causality) and potential from the future (teleology) simultaneously influence the state of the present.”

Harry Frankfurt (1969): Imagine if a neuroscientist had implanted a device in Jones' brain. When Jones shows signs that he has decided NOT to vote for Candidate A, the device activates and forces him to vote for A. However, Jones himself decides to vote for A without ever activating the device. Jones could not have done anything else (the device would have intervened) - but intuitively he seems to be morally responsible because he acted on his own reflection. Conclusion: The “principle of alternative possibilities” (moral responsibility requires the ability to act differently) is wrong. Responsibility requires that one act according to one's own reason-responsive mechanism, not according to counterfactual alternatives. Frankfurt cases are the strongest argument for compatibilism and directly undermine the central premise of hard determinism.

Two different conceptions of the freedom required for moral responsibility: (1) Margin Compatibility: The agent must have had real alternative options - the ability to act differently. Challenge by Frankfurt cases. (2) Sourcehood Compatibility: What matters is not alternative possibilities, but whether the action results from the actor's own character, values and deliberation process - the actor is the "source" of the action. John Martin Fischer's semicompatibilism: Moral responsibility requires managerial control (reason-responsiveness), but not regulative control (alternative possibilities). This distinction is crucial: it shifts the question from “Could I have acted differently?” to “Is this action really mine?”

entities.id: Epiphenomenalism and the causal effectiveness of consciousness
entities.description: Epiphenomenalism: Conscious mental states (decisions, intentions, will) are causally inert byproducts of physical brain processes. The brain causes behavior; The conscious “will” merely accompanies it without causing anything – like the shadow of a moving object. T.H. Huxley's “Steam Whistle on a Locomotive”: The whistle accompanies the locomotive, but does not drive it. If epiphenomenalism is true, the experience of willing is systematically illusory - we feel like we are deciding, but the decision has already been made by unconscious neural processes. This is consistent with hard determinism and is supported by Libet-style experiments. The biggest challenge: Even our belief that epiphenomenalism is true would itself be causally determined by physics and not by our reasoning about it.

Illusionism (Daniel Wegner, “The Illusion of Conscious Will,” 2002): The experience of conscious will is a construction – a post hoc narrative that the brain generates to explain behavior that has already been determined by unconscious processes. Proof: (1) Libet experiments. (2) Split-brain patients confabulate reasons for actions that are controlled by the nonverbal hemisphere. (3) Hypnotic suggestion and alien hand syndrome: People act without will, but generate narratives of will. (4) Priming studies: Unconscious priming systematically influences “free” decisions. The sense of authorship is real as an experience, but false as a causal report. However, even if will is an illusion, the illusion may be functionally necessary—belief in free will improves self-regulation, reduces aggression, and increases prosocial behavior (Vohs & Schooler, 2008).

Regardless of its metaphysical status, belief in and experience of free will has measurable functional consequences: (1) Disbelief in free will increases deception, aggression, and decreases helping behavior (Vohs & Schooler, 2008; Baumeister et al., 2009). (2) The experience of agency and authorship is a prerequisite for effective self-regulation and long-term planning. (3) Ego depletion studies: The ability to self-control is a limited resource that can be depleted - suggesting a genuine mechanism of voluntary control over automatic impulses. (4) Implementation intentions (Gollwitzer): The conscious planning of “If X, then I will do Y” measurably increases goal achievement by creating automatic if-then answers. Functional free will is real as a cognitive system, independent of its metaphysical basis.

Predictive processing theory (Karl Friston, Andy Clark) reformulates the question: The brain is not a passive recipient of stimuli, but an active prediction machine that constantly generates hypotheses about the world and updates them through prediction errors. Actions are themselves predictions - the brain predicts the sensory consequences of a movement and executes movements to fulfill the prediction (active inference). In this framework, “will” is the brain's prediction of its own future action, which then triggers that action. This is neither pure determinism (the prediction is not mechanically fixed - it is context-sensitive, value-weighted and changeable through deliberation) nor libertarian freedom (it is completely physical). It is a sophisticated compatibilism based on neuroscience.

The practical aspects of the free will debate: Criminal law assumes that actors could have acted differently and deserve punishment. If hard determinism applies, punishment is unjustified. Three alternative frameworks: (1) Consequentialist: Punishment is justified if it deters or rehabilitates crime. (2) Strawsonian (P.F. Strawson, “Freedom and Resentment,” 1962): Moral responsibility is based not on metaphysics but on our reactive attitudes (resentment, gratitude, indignation)—practices that constitute human relationships. Even if determinism is true, these attitudes are not undermined because they are constitutive of personality and do not depend on metaphysical freedom. (3) Revisionists: Our practices of taking responsibility should be revised in the light of determinism - more rehabilitation, less retribution.

The Deepest Logical Problem of Hard Determinism: If determinism is true, then the belief that determinism is true was itself determined by prior physical causes—not by reasoning, evidence, or logic. The determinist's argument for determinism is not the reason they believe it; It is merely the post hoc narrative that accompanies a physically determined state of belief. This undermines the epistemic authority of the determinist's own position: no belief under determinism is held because it is true - all beliefs are held because previous physics required them. This is the self-refutation argument against eliminative materialism (Plantinga's evolutionary argument against naturalism, extended to determinism). Compatibilism escapes this: if thinking is a physical process that truly responds to reasons, beliefs can be caused by evidence even though they are still physical.

entities.id: Free will and the safety principle (nuclear voltage)
entities.typ: Core connection to the base graph (synthesis node)
entities.description: The central synthesis of this subgraph with the base graph: The security principle (“Do not form dominant future expectations – act in the now – intrinsic motivation”) implicitly presupposes a subject who is able to adopt a motivating attitude. This is a compatibilist claim of free will: the agent can reshape the causal structure of his or her own decision-making through conscious practice (process orientation, feedback without hope, modeling vs. expectation). This is not libertarian freedom (the practice is physically realized in neural changes), but real source freedom: the agent becomes the source of actions that result from his or her own restructured values. The paradox: Hard determinism would say that this restructuring would always happen; Nevertheless, the Security Principle treats it as a real practice that one can choose. Solution: Compatibilism resolves this tension - the choice to practice the Safety Principle is free will physically realized through neuroplasticity and conscious attention.

entities.id: Neuroplasticity as the physical basis of compatibilist freedom
entities.description: The brain is not a fixed deterministic machine, but a dynamically restructuring system: neuroplasticity means that conscious practice, attention and reflection physically change the strength of neuronal connections (long-term potentiation/depression, axonal sprouting, cortical remapping). This represents the physical mechanism for compatibilist freedom: the agent's considerations, values, and attention causally restructure the very neural substrate from which future decisions emerge. Over time, the agent becomes a different physical system—a system whose causal structure increasingly reflects its reflective values. Meditation studies (Davidson, Lazar): 8 weeks of mindfulness practice measurably change prefrontal-amygdala connectivity. The practice of “acting without expectations” physically rewires the brain’s reward-prediction circuits.

entities.id: Highly complex solution level for the paradox of free will
entities.description: The paradox of free will can be resolved at the highest level of complexity through a synthesis of four frameworks: (1) TSVF retrocausality: The will is the future boundary condition of quantum brain dynamics and makes decisions neither determined nor random, but teleologically self-authored. (2) Sourcehood Compatibility: Freedom requires that actions result from the actor's own reason-responsive, value-structured causal mechanism - and not from alternative possibilities. (3) Neuroplasticity: Through conscious practice, the causal substrate is physically restructured so that the agent becomes the source of its own causal structure over time. (4) Functional Free Will: The belief and experience of agency is not just an illusion, but a causally effective cognitive system that enables self-regulation, ethical living, and the exercise of the safety principle. The “positive result”: Free will exists as a multi-layered, physically realized, temporally bidirectional process of self-authorship – neither the mind in the machine of libertarianism nor the pure passivity of hard determinism.

Comment from user Matthias Beckmann:

I see two directions when it comes to the topic of “free will”. More precisely, I would not speak of “free will”, active in the “now”, but of “free decision”, active over a longer period of time:

Negative Direction: Choosing to harm yourself or others is an act of “free choice.” Either you have to bear the consequences yourself or you should therefore be punished.

Positive direction: Here I have doubts as to whether there can be a real “free decision” for the positive. If Newton had not discovered classical physics, someone else would have. Einstein's discoveries could also have been determined, if not by him, then by someone else. And: First Newton has to discover classical physics, only then can Einstein discover relativity. Any other approach is not logical.

In all imaginable processes in our reality, the increase in complexity, which I see as a form of entropy increase, is a driving force that nothing can oppose in the long term. The increase in complexity seems to be determined. You can of course oppose it with “free decision”. But only to your own detriment.

Although the increase in complexity seems to determine everything in a positive direction, it should also be borne in mind that the limit of this complexity cannot be determined, i.e. cannot be determined.
The Now – A Cognitive User Interface of Time. A synchronization point artificially created by the brain that makes the complex dynamics of time vectors manageable for consciousness.

Processes or correlations that propagate over spatial distances faster than the speed of light - or instantaneously. In classical physics these are forbidden as information carriers. In quantum mechanics, nonlocal correlations (e.g. entanglement) exist that are in fact superluminal, but cannot be used for classical signal transmission. Within the simulation model of this graphic: Superluminal processes lie outside the reproduction limit of the human brain - they fall outside the light cone and are therefore inaccessible to the human "now" synchronization point. They manifest themselves to the human observer as “future events” – in principle unattainable.

entities.id: Light cone limit of human consciousness
entities.description: The light cone derived from the special theory of relativity defines the region of space-time that is causally accessible to an observer. The human brain constructs its “now” synchronization point strictly within this limit. Superluminal correlations that lie outside the front light cone are not transferred into the human world model. From a human perspective, they appear as “future” – information that physically exists but is categorically excluded from conscious access. This limit is not just a physical limitation, but a cognitive architectural limitation: the precision of the human “now” construction itself forces the exclusion.

entities.id: Temporal resolution of the now window
entities.description: The sharpness of the “now” synchronization point is not a universal constant – it scales with the neural complexity and evolutionary architecture of the brain. The human brain invests significant computational resources (approximately 300-500 ms integration delay) to construct a coherent, high-resolution present moment. This sharpness creates a precise boundary: What lies within the cone of light is “now”, what lies outside is “future”. A simpler brain with fewer resources constructs a coarser, temporally diffuse now window - a broad range of probability rather than a sharp point. The width of this window determines how much of the light cone boundary area is accessible to the internal world model.

The housefly brain (about 1 million neurons compared to about 86 billion in humans) lacks the neural resources to construct a sharp “now” synchronization point. His internal world model works with a temporally diffuse now window: a broad, probabilistic integration field that simultaneously includes past residues, present signals and probability amplitudes of the near future. This isn't a deficiency - it's an evolutionary optimization for rapidly changing, high-threat environments. The fly does not experience a clean “present moment” that is separate from the future. Instead, it simulates a probability noise field over a temporal domain. Superluminal quantum correlations, which lie completely outside the human light cone, fall into this broader window and become part of the fly's normal perceptual signal - not as conscious future knowledge, but as weighted noise that influences motor decisions.

The mode of reality simulation characteristic of a low-complexity brain such as that of the housefly. Instead of a sharply rendered present, the fly perceives a superposition of probability-weighted signals from a temporal domain: fading past signals, present sensory data, and anticipating future probability amplitudes derived from quantum nonlocal correlations. The “image” of reality is not a clear snapshot, but a blurry, shimmering field of weighted possibilities – analogous to a long exposure photo compared to a sharp single image. This mode is more similar to the raw structure of the time vectors, as described in this graphic: past and future are not yet cleanly separated.

entities.id: Quantum biology – hypothesis of nonlocal signals
entities.description: The hypothesis that biological systems - especially insects - may be sensitive to quantum nonlocal correlations as functional perceptual signals. There is a precedent in quantum biology: the bird quantum compass (cryptochrome radical pair mechanism) shows that bird navigation uses quantum coherence for environmental perception. The extension proposed here: nervous systems of insects operating in a diffuse now window may not filter out quantum-nonlocal (superluminal) correlations as noise, but rather integrate them as a signal. This would not violate the no-communication theorem (no classical information is transmitted), but would mean that the fly's motor system is influenced by correlation patterns that include future boundary conditions - in the sense of the TSVF formalism. This hypothesis is speculative and currently not experimentally verified. It is marked as a theoretical expansion node.

The housefly's escape reaction to approaching threats is triggered before classic contact signals are sufficient to react - the fly reacts to the shadow of an intention, not yet to physical movement. Classic explanation: The fly detects optical signals (expanding shadow patterns) that precede contact. Extended interpretation within this model: The fly's diffuse now window integrates quantum nonlocal correlations from the attacker's neural preparation state - motor intent - before manifesting as classical physical movement. The human attacker has “decided” to attack at the quantum computing level before the classical neural signal is executed. This quantum level preparatory event exists outside the human light cone (future for humans), but within the fly's broader time window. The fly's reaction therefore appears - from a human perspective - as if the fly had "read" an intention that has not yet been physically expressed.

entities.id: Apparent mind reading of the fly
entities.description: The human observer's perception that a fly has "read its mind" when it escapes a planned but not yet carried out attack. This is a systematic misallocation that results from the asymmetry between the human and fly world models: the human's sharp now window excludes the motor preparatory state at the quantum level as the "future". The fly's diffuse now window includes it as a current signal. From the human's first-person perspective, the causal chain is: thought → fly escapes → (an attack never occurs). This creates the phenomenology of precognition or mind reading. In reality, it is an asymmetrical temporal access: the fly accesses a signal that the human brain has not yet classified as real.

The precision of the “now” synchronization point follows an evolutionary gradient: simpler nervous systems (insects, simple invertebrates) operate with wide, diffuse temporal windows and implicit access to broader temporal correlation fields, including the light cone boundary region. As neuronal complexity increases through evolution (fish → reptiles → mammals → primates → humans), the now window becomes narrower and sharper. This increased acuity enables higher cognitive functions - language, planning, abstract thinking - but at the expense of access to the broad temporal correlation field. The human brain is at the extremely narrow end of this gradient: maximum sharpness, maximum exclusion of superluminal correlations. The insect sits near the diffuse end: low acuity, maximum temporal width, implicit sensitivity to quantum nonlocal correlation fields.

entities.id: Superluminal access as an evolutionary compromise
entities.description: The evolutionary sharpening of the "now" synchronization point - which enabled human-level cognition - simultaneously eliminated implicit access to superluminal quantum correlations that simpler nervous systems retain. This is not a loss of a mystical ability, but a structural architectural consequence: a narrow now window, by definition, cannot contain any signals from outside the light cone. Insects have not “gained” any special ability – humans “lost” the wide temporal window through cognitive specialization. The housefly's apparent precognitive ability is thus a relic of a presharp-temporal architecture, preserved because it provides a survival advantage in detecting threats without the need for complex cognitions.
Time vectors – The dual structure of reality in which both information from the past (causality) and potential from the future (teleology) simultaneously influence the state of the present.

The internal 3D world model generated by the brain. It acts as a dynamic user interface of reality, continuously updated through sensory data and predictions to enable efficient interaction with the environment.

Quantum phenomena (superposition, blurring) function as a 'lazy evaluation': the world is only "rendered" into a definitive state in the microcosm when an interaction (measurement) takes place. This saves enormous computing resources in the simulation of space-time.
Quantum TSVF – The two-state vector formalism according to Yakir Aharonov. A mathematical model that describes the quantum world by superimposing past and future vectors, thus expanding causality.

entities.id: Limit of the speed of light
entities.description: maximum speed of information transfer
The initial start sequence of the current simulation cycle. It defines the point at which the spatial grid and the clock frequency (time) are initialized.
The state of the “data server” or configuration potential before the current universe begins to be displayed. Can be answered by boundary condition analysis.
The hard-coded parameters of the simulation (e.g. gravitational constant, speed of light) that ensure a stable macroscopic representation.
The process by which holographic projections create the illusion of three-dimensional space and linear time for the viewer.
Investigating whether time is a fundamental coordinate or a result of information processing (clock frequency) within the brain-simulation interface.
The algorithmic self-organization of matter from highly entropic initial states into stable patterns.
The mathematical stability of the “logical points” (matter) within the space-time grid.
The logical rule set of the simulation operating system, optimized for survival-oriented macroscopic reality.
The development of the consciousness module as a central control and expectation authority within the simulation.
The transition from complex chemical logic to biological information processing.
The “why” that lies outside the simulation code. A query that attempts to connect to the host system from the client.
The search for the source of the absolute substrate (First Being) that cannot be derived from internal data.
The attempt to find a logical reason for the existence of a system leads to the limits of the human logic model.
The philosophical “Prime Mover”. In logic this leads to the problem that caused it (Infinite Regress).
The question of why logic itself exists and follows rules. This leads to a circular argument.
The search for a metarule that justifies the existence of all other rules.
Trying to prove logic using logic, which is a fundamental problem of self-reference.
The study of the “First Being” or Ultimate Creator, which forces the system into an infinite regress of creators.
The question of why an uncaused, eternal being should exist calls into question the principle of causality.
The logical contradiction of defining “nothing” as a state from which “something” can arise.
The basic processing language that enables both simulation and human perception.
The specific constraints and constants that determine the macroscopic world model.
The 4D rendering grid of the simulation.
The stable localized information densities within the space-time grid.
The theoretical absence of any information, data or space-time coordinates.
The principle that all phenomena within the simulation must be explained by the simulation's own rules and data.
A logical singularity that occurs when a system attempts to analyze or justify itself based on its own internal rules.
A chain of causes that never reaches a starting point and makes the question of “origin” unanswerable.
A logical error in which, in proving a statement, the statement itself is assumed to be true (e.g., justifying logic with logic).
The hypothetical absolute starting point of all existence, which remains epistemologically inaccessible.
The totality of the represented reality and its underlying information substrate.
Classical geometric impossibility arising from the hidden presupposition of a single universal flat Euclidean space; signals the need for a multi-stage model change.
The prevailing expectation that all geometric constructions must be possible with a compass and ruler on a single flat plane; central false premise of the paradox.
Alternative geometry in which the curvature transforms shapes (square ↔ circle) depending on the observer's reference system; enables highly complex resolution.

Thought experiment showing that the same physical object appears as a “square” to an observer in flat space and as a “circle” to an observer in curved space (goldfish); demonstrates observer-dependent geometry. Inspired by Stephen Hawking.
Basic principle that geometric properties are not absolute, but relative to the observer's model of reality; central bridge between low and high levels of complexity.
Hawking's insight that no single model is “the true reality”; Several consistent models (flat + curved) can both be equally real depending on the frame chosen.
The hidden assumption of low complexity that a single geometry must be universal; directly triggers the reversal effect when confronted with evidence from curved spaces.
A low-complexity attempt to force squaring within a flat space leads to ever better approximations, but never to an exact solution; classic reversal that signals the need for a model change.
Reference to existing graph nodes: The flat 2D and curved 3D projections are two equally valid encodings of the same underlying reality.

entities.id: Thing in itself vs. perceived form
entities.description: Kantian-inspired limit: The true geometric “thing in itself” is inaccessible; we only ever perceive model-dependent projections (flat square vs. curved circle).
The highly complex transition from single-stage flat reasoning to simultaneous flat + curved models; the exact mechanism that resolves the paradox.
Proof (Lindemann 1882) that π is transcendent; shows why the paradox cannot be solved within the flat space model and forces the transition to higher complexity.

entities.id: Horizon of expectations in mathematical modeling
entities.description: The predictive, brain-driven expectation that “simple tools should suffice”; When breached, it opens the door to new expectations for firewalls and model extensions.
The general Beckmann process, in which an apparent paradox is transformed into a resolved, higher-level insight by explicitly changing the underlying model of reality.
Hawking's explicit use of the goldfish in a round bowl analogy to illustrate that different observers may well describe the same reality using incompatible geometries.
The concrete geometric mechanism by which appropriate curvature renders a Euclidean square indistinguishable in the observer's local frame of reference.

The fundamental non-integrability and exponential divergence of trajectories in the classical gravitational three-body problem (Poincaré 1889). Serves as diagnostic evidence that the macroscopic physical model exceeds the computational capacity of the brain.
The absolute requirement for continuous external sensor data to keep the internal macroscopic model stable. Without it, the three-body chaos immediately renders long-term predictions useless.
The simplified classic N-body model rendered by the Human Logic Model. Stable only under strong heuristics (two-body dominance + small perturbations) – collapses analytically with three bodies.

Point at which the brain's information processing capacity is exceeded by the combinatorial complexity of three gravitationally coupled bodies, forcing the renderer to rely on real-time synchronization rather than internal prediction.
Permanent real-time comparison between external reality data stream and internal 3D world model. The only mechanism that compensates for capacity limit overflow in macroscopic chaos regimes.

The limited but usable stability window (milliseconds to years) that is achieved exclusively through continuous sensor sync. The brain can only navigate planetary orbits because it is constantly recalibrating itself.
The inevitable reversal from expected deterministic trajectories (Timeline A) to chaotic, unpredictable divergence (Timeline B) when sensor synchronization is broken or inadequate.
Unconscious expectation layer that uses the latest sensor data to correct the internal model in real time, preventing immediate macroscopic collapse in chaotic regimes.
The brain's internal simulation cannot store or calculate the entire information content of three-body interactions. It is therefore only an approximation that needs to be patched externally.
Closed loop between the external present reality and the perceived current reality. The physical requirement that transforms three-body chaos from a fatal flaw to a manageable trait.

Application of Beckmann logic to the three-body problem: highly complex solution level = continuous sensor synchronization; Low complexity solution level = pure internal analytical prediction → negative result.
The only feasible highly complex solution level: iterative real-time sensor synchronization instead of attempting a one-off analytical solution.

The brain compresses the holographic information of the external volume into a low-dimensional macroscopic model. The three-body chaos is where this compression fails without constant external updating.
The waking state simulation remains macroscopically coherent precisely because of the need for sensor sync. The offline (dream) mode would immediately reveal full chaos.
Expectation firewall that prevents prevailing future expectations about long-term three-body trajectories and thus avoids spirals of disappointment and cementation in astronomical modeling.

The central reversal mechanism, whereby a prevailing expectation of deterministic long-term predictability (Timeline A) is reversed into chaotic, unpredictable divergence (Timeline B) when sensor synchronization is inadequate.
An error in the model representation towards the past on extremely large time scales.
At the moment, Hegel's logic theory is viewed very critically. It could be a typical circular argument. However, it cannot be ruled out that Hegel's work could be assessed differently in the future.

entities.typ: Historical innovator / polymath of the Renaissance
entities.description: 1471–1528, German artist, graphic designer, theorist and entrepreneur who viewed art as a scalable technology. Master of engraving, systematic self-branding and mass reproduction of art.
Duerer's mastery of engraving transformed a technical craft into a high-resolution, reproducible art medium and enabled the first true mass production of high-quality images in Europe.
One of the earliest systematic artist brands in history. Duerer's iconic AD monogram guaranteed authenticity, increased market value and functioned as a personal logo.

Duerer's series of self-portraits, particularly the Christ-like frontal portrait from 1500, consciously elevated the image of the artist from an anonymous craftsman to an intellectual and almost divine creator.
Duerer built a workshop that produced and sold hundreds of thousands of prints across Europe - the first artist to view art as a scalable, commercially distributed product.

entities.id: Duerer than Elon Musk of the Middle Ages
entities.description: Direct analogy: Both used the most modern technology of their time, mastered personal branding, built vertically integrated production systems and fundamentally changed their areas of expertise.

entities.id: Duerer than Steve Jobs of the Renaissance
entities.description: Career-like traits: obsessive focus on design perfection, merging aesthetics and technology, creating a strong personal brand, and elevating the creator to cultural icon status.
The prevailing medieval expectation (artist = anonymous craftsman with low status) was powerfully reversed by Duerer into the new reality of the artist as a respected genius and intellectual.

entities.id: Highly complex solution in art production
entities.typ: Beckmann logic – highly complex solution level
entities.description: Duerer solved the problem of limited artistic scope by combining Italian perspective, German craftsmanship, new printing technology and mathematical theory - a highly complex solution rather than simply producing unique paintings.
Books like “Four Books on Human Proportions” – Duerer’s attempt to mathematically encode ideal beauty and perspective and to link artistic creation with systematic generative rules.
Duerer's studio as the physical embodiment of the Infinite Fantasy - the productive imagination that produced new visual realities, symbols and blueprints for future European art.
Collective unconscious expectation of the time: The artist as an anonymous guild craftsman in the service of religion and wealthy patrons.
Duerer's conscious influence from above on future reality through self-portraits, monograms, marketing and theoretical writings - active shaping of external perception and artist status.

entities.id: Revolution of the European art market
entities.description: The long-term result of Duerer's innovations: the creation of the first pan-European market for affordable, high-quality printed art.
Productive Imagination – The mediating agency that links sensory material to intellectual concepts and creates the mental images and visual realities in the simulation of art and creativity.

Unconscious emotional reaction in which prisoners develop positive feelings toward the captors in order to reduce violence and prioritize survival, first observed after the Norrmalmstorg bank robbery in 1973. Not a diagnosis, but a coping pattern.

Proposed scientific replacement for Stockholm Syndrome. Describes the perpetrator's instinctive calming through social engagement to adapt to life-threatening situations, which occurs via brainstem circuits rather than a conscious decision.

A strong emotional bond is created through cycles of threats and small acts of kindness, reinforcing the interdependence between victim and attacker. Common in hostage situations and abusive relationships.

Process in which the survival circuits of the brainstem override conscious will when life is threatened and redirect neuronal activity from the prefrontal cortex to autonomic defense systems. Reduces problem solving and replaces intent with defensive strategies.

The brain creates real neurochemical states of relief, gratitude and connection rather than feigning compassion. Provides congruent signals that calm the attacker more effectively than conscious pretense.
Perceived inability to escape, associated with power asymmetry and isolation. Activates the fight/flight/freeze/appease cascade according to the Polyvagal Theory.

Bidirectional calming of the autonomic nervous system through prosodic voice, eye contact, and submission signals. Reduces the perpetrator's aggression and increases the victim's probability of survival.

Porges' model explains how the mammalian nervous system prioritizes social contact for safety reasons. When threatened, higher brain functions are suppressed by defensive states, which enables appeasement as a hybrid state.

Quintus Fabius Maximus' deliberate avoidance of direct battles reflects appeasement logic: accept power imbalances, regulate the enemy by refusing to confront, survive to win later. Consciously instead of unconsciously.

The Fabian strategy uses conscious acceptance of inferiority for strategic gain, Stockholm Syndrome uses unconscious acceptance for immediate survival. Both avoid the prevailing expectation of instant victory.

Core principle that connects both phenomena: Direct confrontation leads to catastrophe, survival requires changed rules of engagement. Basis for both the assuagement of trauma and the wear and tear of Fabian.
Focusing action on the present moment without future commitment. Enables process orientation instead of hope-driven dominance. Prevents reversal effects according to the safety principle.
Collective belief that direct combat will be successful despite power imbalance. Rome, before Cannae held it, was initially held by hostages. Triggers a reversal effect when reality contradicts it.
The evolution of the original selective attention tests designed to bypass existing knowledge and target deeper layers of cognitive processing.
A 2013 study in which radiologists were unable to detect a gorilla in a CT scan because their “expertise scheme” focused exclusively on pulmonary nodules.
The phenomenon that the brain stops searching for targets as soon as the first expected target (the gorilla) is found, resulting in an immediate decrease in attention.
The specific criteria the brain uses to filter data. Knowledge about the gorilla limits attention and makes the observer more blind to anomalies that do not belong to the gorilla.
Recent VR research shows that anticipation of the “unexpected” creates a rigid focus that increases blindness to peripheral changes compared to naive observers.
A condition in which prior knowledge of a test (Level 1) acts as a new blind spot to a more subtle modification (Level 2).
The process of analyzing the errors of original psychological tests to create higher order traps, reflecting Beckmann's logic of increasing problem levels.
The transition from a test of perception to a test of cognitive modesty in which the subject's pride in “seeing the gorilla” is the primary means of distraction.
A high-level mental framework that optimizes efficiency for certain tasks, but creates "occlusion culling" for data that doesn't fit the pattern.
A narrowing of the field of perception caused by intense task focus or excessive reliance on a particular prediction (predictive coding).
Failure to notice large-scale changes in the environment (e.g., changing the color of a background curtain) due to central resource allocation.
Proof of occlusion culling, where irrelevant data (the gorilla) is not rendered to save resources.
The process of prioritizing certain data for conscious awareness and leaving out the rest.
The phenomenon in which an object's future relevance influences its probability of recognition in the present, potentially circumventing inattentive blindness.
Based on “Feeling the Future,” which suggests that physiological responses can precede the stimuli that trigger them.
In a simulation context: A metadata tag from a future state that marks a current object as High Priority for rendering.
An extension of standard predictive coding in which the model is calibrated not only based on the past, but also on “potential future” data packets.
A gap in linear time processing where future information influences current capacity allocation.
A higher level of complexity in which the observer integrates nonlinear temporal data to solve “impossible” perception tasks.
The concrete object in the gorilla experiment that undergoes a change of state (color) and is assigned a future meaning.
An experimental setup where the gorilla goes from a passive anomaly to an active aggressor that attacks players.
Stimuli (such as sudden aggression or rapid movement) that are so intense that they invade conscious awareness.
A processing route that bypasses the cortical “attention set” to prioritize threat detection and survival responses.
A state in which the capacity limit is forcibly redistributed from “task focus” to “threat focus” to ensure the safety of the organism.
The sudden collapse of an existing attentional set due to a high-priority interruption (e.g., a startle response).
Assign priority to an object based on its potential to cause harm in the immediate future.
The physiological preparation (increased heart rate, skin conductivity) for a threat before it is consciously perceived or even occurs.
A state in which the brain reproduces the environment with maximum fidelity and overrides all filters due to high stakes.
Describes highly complex possible solutions related to the gorilla experiment from psychology
A dynamic problem-solving process with problem levels and solution levels based on the gorilla experiment from psychology
Limit with which unique information can be processed, based on the gorilla experiment from psychology
Automated prediction mechanism of the brain (predictive coding) that permanently stabilizes the present, based on the gorilla experiment from psychology.
The problem was successfully solved, related to the gorilla experiment from psychology.
Describes the complexity of the new problem, related to the gorilla experiment from psychology.
The anticipation of future events that can be consciously controlled by the individual, based on the gorilla experiment from psychology.

A refined version of the Gorilla Test, in which the anticipation of the “surprise” (of the gorilla) is used as a new distraction to hide peripheral changes (color of the curtain, departure of the players).
Limit with which clear information can be processed

entities.id: Dark energy (as a mysterious form of energy)
entities.typ: hypothetical form of energy, not found
entities.description: Dark energy as a form of energy is what scientists suspect. It's just an unproven hypothesis.
A scaling error in the spatial representation (floating point precision error) at extremely large distances.

This area of the graph raises open questions for the further development of the theoretical system: (1) Is there a formula that calculates the time scale and amplitude of the reversal effect from the depth of the anchoring and the feedback speed? (2) Can reversal effects of different time scales be consciously decoupled - i.e. can one achieve a short-cycle success without thereby preparing a long-cycle reversal effect? (3) Are there historical examples in which cross-scale coupling was deliberately broken? (4) Is the scale invariance of the inverse effect an indication of a deeper mathematical structure - perhaps a self-similarity in the sense of fractals?

This graph raises open questions for the theoretical system: (1) Is there a threshold beyond which cementation becomes irreversible? (2) Is decementation possible without an external catastrophe - or does it always require an external test that can no longer be ignored? (3) How does cementation relate to creativity - is the ability to construct new explanations (constructive cementation) the same ability as presupposition analysis, just directed in the wrong direction? (4) Is there a measurable signature of cementation in texts, decision patterns or systems that would enable early detection?

Rehhagel systematically questioned assumptions: 'Newcomers cannot become champions', 'Greece has no chance', 'Defensive tactics do not lead to success'. According to Beckmann logic (Section 4): Seemingly unsolvable problems become solvable by questioning their presuppositions.

Hope for equality → oppression by Athens → uprisings → harsher oppression → stronger resistance. Self-reinforcing cycle analogous to the safety principle. Link between Reversal Effect and Peloponnesian War that was missing in version 1.0.
Hope for reform → blockade → radicalization → violence → escalation. Self-reinforcing cycle (see safety principle).

The aggregated directional belief of the majority of market participants about future price developments. When this expectation becomes dominant – i.e. h. when almost all visible analysts, media and private investors agree on the same view – it becomes a self-destructive force. Beckmann logic identifies this condition as a primary prerequisite for a reversal effect in markets.

The process by which market analysis is not a neutral observation but an active intervention in the system it describes. An analysis that reaches a large audience changes the dominant expectation vector of that audience, which in turn changes the pricing reality that the analysis describes. First systematized by George Soros. Expanded here by Beckmann logic: Reflexivity is a special case of self-inclusion in which the observer is structurally part of the observed system.

The practice of explicitly incorporating the market implications of one's analysis or decision into the analysis itself. A low complexity analysis ignores its own influence. A highly complex analysis asks: How many people will see this conclusion? What will they do? How does this change the outcome I predict? Self-inclusion does not guarantee correct predictions, but it does prevent the systematic error of treating one's model as external to the system.

A market position recommended by the majority of analysts, fund managers and financial media alike. Seems to be the safest and most validated choice. In Beckmann logic, this is the paradigmatic solution with low complexity: it directly follows the dominant expectation, which maximizes the reversal effect risk. The more universal the consensus, the more dangerous the trade.

The point at which a prevailing market expectation reverses into its opposite: a widely expected rally becomes a crash; A widely expected crash turns into a rally. Not a random event, but a structural consequence of the dominance of expectations. The exact timing of the reversal event cannot be predicted, but its prerequisites – high consensus, low dissent, late-cycle positioning – can be identified through presupposition analysis.

The systematic questioning of hidden assumptions that are anchored in every market thesis. Standard examples: “Interest Rates Stay Low” (2020-2021), “Real Estate Always Rises” (2005-2006), “AI Stocks Are Immune to Valuations” (2024-2025). Presupposition analysis does not predict when these assumptions will break, but rather identifies which assumptions are fundamental to the current market structure - and therefore are most dangerous if violated.

The danger that increases with the reach of a market analysis. A private conclusion has a negligible reflexive effect. The same conclusion published by Goldman Sachs, Bloomberg or a viral financial influencer changes the dominant expectation vector of millions of actors simultaneously. The higher the reach, the higher the likelihood that the analysis itself will become the main cause of the predicted market movement - and thus trigger a reversal effect.

The irreducible uncertainty about when a correctly identified reversal condition will actually manifest itself in a price movement. Beckmann logic does not dissolve this limit - it explicitly recognizes it. A market thesis can be structurally correct (the prevailing expectation is untenable) but operationally useless if the timing is off by 18 months. Position sizing and risk management must take this uncertainty into account and must not eliminate it.

Act against the prevailing market expectation if the prerequisite analysis determines that this expectation is excessive. No reflexive contrarianism - logic requires evidence of dominance saturation, not mere disagreement. In Beckmann's words: The opposite position is the non-dominant expectation that undergoes a double reversal and appears as a linear fulfillment. Risk: Being too early is structurally identical to making a mistake in terms of loss of capital.

entities.id: Position determination as a safety principle
entities.description: Applying the Beckmann Security Principle to Market Investments: Since prevailing future expectations trigger reversal effects and the timing is indeterminate, no individual position should be measured as if the outcome were certain. Spreading capital across multiple uncorrelated positions reduces the damage of a single reversal event. This is the market equivalent of "act now, don't form prevailing future expectations": stay solvent long enough for the thesis to manifest itself.

The process by which large financial institutions - banks, pension funds, index funds - reinforce their prevailing market expectations through structural commitments: benchmark tracking, regulatory capital requirements, career risk avoidance. Once established, these institutions can no longer update their expectations, even if external audits (earnings shortfalls, macro data) contradict them. This leads to a systematic divergence between the prevailing institutional expectation and the evolving reality - the root cause of major reversal events.

The empirical signal from reality that contradicts a prevailing market expectation: an earnings report, a macro data release, a geopolitical event, a loan default. In Beckmann logic, external testing is the only valid validation of a model. Markets that ignore external controls – through narrative rationalization, through “this time is different” logic – are in a state of presuppositional consolidation. The longer the external control is ignored, the more severe the eventual correction.

The market condition that arises after a reversal event dissolves the prevailing expectation. This is not a return to a previous state, but a truly new configuration: new dominant sectors, new evaluation frameworks, new dominant actors. Beckmann logic emphasizes that this new actual level immediately begins to form its own prevailing expectations - the cycle begins again. Investors who view the post-reversal state as stable are already building up the next low-complexity error.

A market-shaping story—“the AI revolution,” “the housing crisis,” “the commodities supercycle”—that is so widely accepted that it functions as an invisible premise rather than a thesis to be tested. Narrative dominance is dangerous precisely because it is no longer perceived as an expectation but as a reality. Presupposition analysis aims precisely at this transition point. When a narrative is no longer challenged, it is most vulnerable to reversal.

entities.id: Geopolitical event as an external check
entities.description: Geopolitical disruptions – wars, sanctions, elections, contract breaches – act as external checks on prevailing market expectations. They are structurally unpredictable in terms of time, but can be analytically understood using Beckmann logic: one wonders which prevailing market expectation is most fragile if geopolitical stability is interrupted. The Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022 is an example: the expectations of the European energy market were set on stable gas supplies for decades - external control was violent and rapid.

The market-specific form of the general Beckmann disappointment spiral: hope for a rising asset value → dominant forms of expectation → reversal event → loss → stronger compensatory hope → larger position → larger reversal → deeper loss. Common among private investors who follow consensus trades. The spiral escalates because every loss is interpreted as a temporary setback that requires more commitment, rather than as an external test of the original premise.
A non-dualistic interpretation of reality in which the separation between self and world is viewed as an illusion. Emphasizes experiential understanding over predictive control.
The more you try to force a desired state (happiness, control, security), the more you create the opposite. Central teaching in Alan Watts' lectures.
The belief that the conscious self can directly control reality. According to Watts, this illusion creates tension and suffering.
The perceived isolated “I” that sees itself as separate from the universe. According to Watts, this is a functional illusion and not an ontological reality.
The idea that subject and object, self and world are not fundamentally separate, but part of a unified process.
Non-coercive action. Act in harmony with the natural flow rather than imposing a will. Derived from Taoist philosophy.
Reality is not a collection of objects, but an ongoing process. The individual is a function of this process, not its controller.
Liberation from the need to control results. This reduces internal conflict and aligns perception with reality.
Full attention to the present without projection into the future. Central to Watts' teachings and parallels to Eastern traditions.
Attempting to achieve lasting certainty in an inherently uncertain world. According to Watts, this creates uncertainty.
Attachment to desired outcomes creates suffering when reality does not match reality.
Prevailing future expectations translate into contradictory results. Basic principle of Beckmann logic.
Attempting to shape reality through strong expectations of future outcomes. Central to Beckmann logic.
The tendency of systems to ignore or reinterpret feedback when expectations prevail.
The empirical observation that strong intentions often lead to opposite results.
A holistic model for categorizing problems based on their solvability and implementation requirements, ranging from metaphysical boundaries to security integrity.

The highest level of difficulty at which problems are essentially no longer solvable. These are questions about the philosophical "first being" or about absolute truth. These questions lead to a logical regression, as there is no longer any meta-level for problem solving. Here the "acceptance of insolvability" is necessary in order to be able to concentrate on the solvability of all other problems.
Pragmatic challenges where the scientific and technical solution is known but requires specific materials, funding or time to implement.
The level of collective acceptance, including political, religious and ethical frameworks that govern the implementation of the solution.
The final phase of implementation focuses on risk management, integrity, and maintaining control of a solution.
A positive social environment that encourages innovation and lowers the barrier to experimenting with Level 2 and 3 solutions.
A mechanism whereby a technically sound solution (level 4) fails because it contradicts the ethical or political values of the collective.
A powerful AI agent that serves as a prime example of Level 6 caution due to significant security and targeting concerns.
An analytical process within Stage 6 to determine whether a solution can be provided without compromising personal or societal integrity.
The state in which a problem is considered to be completely solved after passing through all six levels of the hierarchy.
A set of moral principles that serve as a boundary condition for Level 5 acceptance.
A solution's resilience to unintended consequences or malicious use, critical to passing Level 6.
The dimension of hierarchy (levels 1-6), which deals with knowledge and “what can be known.”
The dimension of hierarchy (levels 4-6), which is concerned with “what can be safely achieved in society”.
Problems for which the scientific theory already exists to solve, but engineering and prototyping are required to prove practical feasibility.

Problems that can be solved logically but lack the necessary mathematical or epistemic formulas for a solution. This also includes problems that appear logically or physically impossible within the current reality model (e.g. violation of causality). Examples are “time travel” and “super-light speed”.

This involves problems of self-reference, chaos or complexity. A change in system boundaries or abstraction is necessary here.

Characteristics: The rules are known, but the solution is not deterministic due to the system structure or goes beyond any computational framework.

Examples:

    Three-body problem: We know the laws of gravity exactly, but there is no general closed analytical solution for n≥3.

    Goedel's incompleteness theorem: Within a sufficiently strong system there are true sentences that cannot be proven.

    Weather forecast (Deterministic Chaos): Small changes in the initial conditions lead to massive deviations (butterfly effect).

A fundamental realignment: Problems are not disruptions that need to be eliminated, but rather valuable signals that show where there is potential for growth. A system that consistently treats problems as resources will develop superior performance over time. This reframing is the prerequisite for any productive problem solution. Without this attitude, an honest analysis is psychologically impossible because problems trigger defensiveness rather than curiosity.

The prevailing expectation in most organizations and individuals is that problems signal failure, weakness, or blame. This expectation is the root cause of avoidance, concealment, and quick-fix behavior. According to Beckmann logic, it is precisely this prevailing expectation that triggers the reversal effect: the more a system tries to suppress problems, the more they accumulate unnoticed until a catastrophic reversal occurs.

The crucial step in problem solving: a clear, honest and thorough analysis of what the problem actually is - not its symptoms. Almost always, once the true problem is clearly understood, the solution emerges naturally. This step requires more time than initially appears necessary. It requires distinguishing between surface symptoms and underlying causes, and the psychological safety that comes from treating problems as resources rather than threats. The quality of each subsequent step depends entirely on the quality of this analysis.

The most common problem-solving mistake is addressing the visible symptom rather than the underlying cause. Symptom treatment feels productive because it provides immediate, visible results. However, the generative mechanism remains intact, causing the problem to reoccur - often in a more complex form. This is a structural manifestation of the low complexity solution level in Beckmann logic: it ignores the actual complexity of the problem and inevitably leads to a negative result.

The quick fix appears attractive because it eliminates immediate discomfort and allows for rapid redistribution of attention to new issues. However, it systematically fails in three ways: (1) it addresses symptoms rather than causes, (2) it can make the underlying situation worse by masking the true signal, (3) it destroys the learning opportunity that was the problem. A quick fix should never be classified as a solution - it is at best a stabilization measure that stops immediate damage while the actual analysis begins. Confusing stabilization with resolution is itself a high-risk error.

A crucial operational distinction: Immediate damage limitation (stabilization) measures are necessary and correct, but must be expressly classified as temporary and incomplete. The analogy: Putting out a fire is stabilization; The solution is to find the cause of the fire and eliminate it. Systems that fail to make this distinction continually confuse resolving acute symptoms with resolving the underlying problem, thus prematurely closing the learning loop and restoring vulnerability.

entities.id: Psychological safety for an honest analysis
entities.description: Honest and motivated participation by everyone involved is the prerequisite for an effective problem analysis. This security can only exist if the organizational or relationship culture treats problems as valuable resources rather than as evidence of failure or blame. If this security is lacking, participants will systematically hide information, minimize the severity of the problem, or assign blame - all of which distort the analysis. The positive attitude towards problem solving is therefore not a soft cultural preference, but a hard epistemological requirement.

A deliberate, structured pause after each issue cycle to extract insights before closing the case. This phase asks: What did this problem reveal about our system? What assumption did we make that the problem was violated? What would we do differently? Without this phase, the value of the problem as a resource will never be realized. The learning phase transforms a problem event into a permanent increase in system capability. Skipping is the main reason why companies face the same problems over and over again.

entities.id: Analysis of requirements for problem solving
entities.description: Before analyzing a problem, systematically question all assumptions embedded in the formulation of the problem. Many seemingly insoluble problems resolve themselves when a false assumption is recognized. Many quick-fix errors occur because a false assumption was never challenged. The question to ask is: “What would have to be true for this not to be a problem?” and “What do we take for granted in this situation that could be wrong?” This is the application of Beckmann's mechanism of prerequisite analysis to the area of problem-solving strategies.

entities.id: Reversal effect in the quick-fix culture
entities.description: The prevailing expectation that “speed equals efficiency” is reversed: Organizations that habitually apply quick fixes accumulate unresolved root causes and build systemic fragility over time. The reversal manifests itself when these accumulated causes combine to produce a crisis far greater than the sum of the original problems. Timeline A (expected: problems are solved quickly, team moves on) vs. Timeline B (actual: the same problems reoccur with increasing complexity, systemic failure occurs). The longer the quick-fix culture is maintained uncorrected, the more destructive the eventual reversal is.

The positive result of applying highly complex problem solutions: the system achieves a new actual level of performance. Crucially, this new level is never final - it immediately becomes the next level of problems and contains new challenges that are only visible from the perspective of the newly achieved capability. This is not about a failure of the solution, but about the correct structure of progress. A system that understands this does not attempt to permanently “close” problems, but instead moves up the iterative cycle, with each problem generating the next level of competency.

entities.id: Problem solving as a system capability
entities.description: A system - organizational, personal or technical - that consistently applies the entire cycle (problem as resource → honest analysis → root cause identification → highly complex solution → reflective learning) will develop sharpening performance advantages. Each problem solved with high complexity reduces the vulnerability to that class of problems while generating the insights needed to solve the next class. This increases over time: the system that solves problems well becomes better at solving problems, while the system that uses quick fixes becomes more fragile.

The only valid confirmation that a problem has been truly addressed (and not just the symptoms) is external reality, not internal judgment. The test: Has the generative mechanism that caused the problem been changed? If the same problem could occur again under similar conditions, the solution would be stabilization and not a solution. This external review must be performed explicitly after each troubleshooting cycle. Internal consensus that a problem is “solved” is a weak test; The observable change in system behavior under real conditions is the meaningful test.

For an effective problem solution, it is necessary to always invest more time in the analysis than initially appears necessary. The pressure to act quickly is a predictable feature of every problem situation and must be consciously warded off during the analysis phase. The investment is asymmetrical: additional time spent on analysis leads to disproportionately better solutions and prevents the rising costs of a misdiagnosis. The principle: If the correct solution time appears to be T, invest 1.5 T, with the majority of the additional time remaining in the analysis phase and not in the solution phase.

The correct measure of success in problem solving is not elimination of the problem, but fundamental progress: an increase in the understanding, performance, and resilience of the system. A “solved” problem that has made the system perform as well as before is a missed opportunity. When a problem is addressed according to the complete cycle of high complexity, the system is better able to handle the next problem. This is the distinction between problem resolution and fundamental progress - the latter is the real goal.

State in a strategic game in which no player can gain an advantage by unilaterally changing his strategy. Everyone involved has adjusted and fixed their expectations to one another. Corresponds to a state of collective dominant expectations that stabilize each other and create or permanently prevent a reversal effect at the system level.

A strategy that always seems to be the best choice, regardless of the behavior of all other actors. It arises from a dominant expectation about the behavior of others and cements itself because deviant behavior appears irrational. Formal equivalent of the dominant expectation at the strategy level.

Prototypical game structure in which two actors achieve a collectively suboptimal Nash equilibrium through simultaneous dominant strategies. Both expect the other to defect and therefore defect themselves - even though mutual cooperation would be better for both. Demonstrates how dominant expectations about the behavior of others create a systemic reversal effect.

Situation in which actors have multiple Nash equilibria and the outcome depends on which expectations prevail about the behavior of others. No dominant strategy exists; the result is determined by shared or divergent structures of expectations. Shows that expectations are not just reactions, but actively shape reality.

The problem of which Nash equilibrium among several possible ones is reached when actors align their expectations about each other's behavior. Shared dominant expectations (Focal Points according to Schelling) act as a coordination mechanism. Shows how unconscious collective expectations constitute game-theoretical realities.

Repeated games in which players learn from previous rounds and adjust their expectations about others' future behavior. Cooperation arises from the expectation of future interaction. Corresponds to the learning principle of the graph: Historical experience modifies dominant expectations for future situations.

Robust strategy in repeated games: Start with cooperation, then react in a mirrored manner to the opponent's last behavior. Does not force equilibrium through dominant expectation, but rather through reciprocal adjustment. Formal counterpart to the security principle: acting in the now without fixed expectations of the future, but with situational responsiveness.

A natural focal point that actors converge on without explicit communication because it is considered obvious or prominent. Schelling points arise from shared expectation structures — dominant unconscious expectations that all actors share. Shows how collective unconscious expectations pre-structure game theory equilibria.

The paradox that individually rational behavior produces collectively irrational results. Each actor follows his dominant expectation and dominant strategy — the collective outcome is worse for everyone. Formal correspondence of the reversal effect at the level of social systems: Individual expectation rationality produces collective reality reversal.

State in which the expectations of all actors about the behavior of others are correct and the actual strategies confirm these expectations. The concept makes explicit what Nash equilibria epistemically presuppose: a system of coordinated dominant expectations that stabilizes itself.

Nash equilibrium that is stable against the entry of actors with different strategies. Corresponds to a dominant collective expectation that is so deeply anchored that deviations are systemically punished. Combines game theory with evolutionary logic and shows how dominant expectations are stabilized biologically and culturally.

The game theory reformulation of the reversal effect: If all actors simultaneously fix their dominant expectations about the system's behavior and act accordingly, they collectively produce exactly the opposite result of their individual expectations. The Nash equilibrium they reach is the formal model of systemic reversal effect.

Game-theoretical interpretation of the security principle: Instead of fixing a dominant strategy (which leads to the suboptimal Nash equilibrium), an actor acts according to the minimax-regret principle — he minimizes the worst possible outcome without forming a dominant expectation about the system's behavior. This corresponds to 'acting without expectation' in formal language.

entities.id: Market expectation as a Nash equilibrium
entities.description: Financial market prices as a Nash equilibrium of aggregated expectation strategies: If the majority of market participants have the same dominant expectation, the system converges into an equilibrium that becomes unstable due to the reversal effect. Consensus trading as a dominant strategy trap: The consensus itself destroys the expected result.

The capacity of an agent to experience practical agency and implement the security principle despite deterministic or cognitive limitations. Not metaphysical freedom from determinism, but functional freedom within deterministic structures. Connecting logic between the paradox of free will and the practice of acting without dominant expectation.

The ability to observe and regulate one's own thought processes. Enables an agent to recognize dominant expectation structures as such without being controlled by them. Metacognition is the cognitive mechanism through which the safety principle is put into practice: observe your expectation formation rather than following it.

The process by which repeated action without a fixed expectation of outcome becomes an automated practice. Habit formation makes it possible to not have to carry out the safety principle as a constant conscious decision, but rather as an embedded pattern of action. Solves the deterministic problem: It is not the will, but the trained practice that implements the principle.

The brain actively generates predictions about sensory input and updates these predictions by minimizing errors. Predictive processing makes it clear that expectation formation is a basic biological mechanism — not a conscious decision, but a neural pattern. The ability to act arises through influence on the prediction models, not through a direct act of will.

Concrete practice for interrupting the automatic formation of expectations: Consciously pausing before making a decision, before the dominant expectation determines the action. Enables metacognitive observation of one's own expectation structure. Shows how a deterministically operating system can partially regulate itself through reflexive interruption.

entities.id: Neuroplasticity as a space of freedom
entities.description: The structural changeability of the brain through experience and training enables the modification of expectation patterns. Neuroplasticity is the neurobiological evidence that deterministic systems have real degrees of learning freedom: the practice of the safety principle literally changes the neural substrates of expectation formation.
