The practical consequence of philosophical compatibilism: If freedom is compatible with determinism, then the security principle is not undermined by determinism, but is compatibilistically founded. Acting based on your own values and attitudes (intrinsic motivation) is a true expression of freedom, even in a deterministic system.

The feedback loop between observation of one's own expectations (metacognition), interruption (expectation-disruption) and modified action (habit formation). Shows that the ability to act under constraints is not a one-time act of will, but a continuous regulatory process. The system observes itself and modifies its expectations.

The ability of consciousness to refrain from an action that has already been prepared (according to Libet). The veto function shows that freedom of action lies not in initiating but in interrupting. An agent cannot prevent dominant expectations from arising, but he can interrupt their translation into actions — this is the neurologically proven mechanism of the security principle.

The insight that restrictions (deterministic, cognitive, situational) not only limit freedom, but also structure and enable it. A constraint defines the space for meaningful actions and makes decisions possible in the first place. The security principle is itself a constraint - a restriction on the formation of expectations that creates freedom of action in the now.

entities.id: Process orientation as a practice of freedom
entities.description: Consciously focusing on the process of action rather than its result. Process orientation is the practical attitude that implements the security principle in everyday life: not forming a dominant expectation about the result, but instead maximizing the quality of current actions. Combines compatibilist freedom with concrete practice.

The resolution of the core paradox: The security principle does not presuppose metaphysical freedom of will. It presupposes that an agent can observe the formation of his expectations (metacognition), interrupt them (veto function) and modify them through practice (neuroplasticity, habit formation). These four mechanisms operate within deterministic frameworks and still generate functional freedom of action.

The phenomenon whereby a single cognitive distortion in a thought system becomes so strong that it overwhelms all other sources of information and completely controls perception. Corresponds to the principle of dominant expectation in the knowledge graph on the individual psychological level: an assumption occupies the entire perception system and makes correction signals invisible.

The tendency to preferentially seek out, interpret, and remember information that confirms an existing belief. Acts as a self-reinforcer of dominant expectations: the stronger an expectation, the more selective the information intake - and the more difficult it is to correct before the reversal effect.

The cognitive tendency to base judgments and decisions disproportionately on an initial reference value (the anchor), even if this is irrelevant or arbitrary. In the context of Beckmann logic: The anchor is often the initial dominant expectation that distorts all subsequent evaluations.

The tendency to judge the likelihood of an event based on how easily a similar example comes to mind. Lively, emotionally charged expectations are thereby systematically overestimated - which promotes the emergence of dominant expectations.

The empirically proven principle that losses are psychologically weighted about twice as much as equivalent gains. Loss aversion reinforces dominant expectations: The expectation of a loss is felt so intensely that actions are taken that trigger the reversal effect - avoiding the loss creates it.

The systematic overestimation of one's own ability to judge and predict. Actors with high self-overestimation develop particularly strong dominant expectations and underestimate the probability of the reversal effect because they consider their model of reality to be more reliable than it is.

The irrational tendency to incorporate irretrievable investments (time, money, energy) that have already been made into future decisions. In relation to Beckmann logic: The sunk effort reinforces the dominant expectation of the original plan and prevents the process-oriented action that the security principle demands.

The phenomenon that individuals align their behavior and beliefs with the perceived majority opinion, regardless of their own information. Herd behavior transforms individual cognitive biases into collective dominant expectations — it is the social amplification mechanism of the reverse effect.

The tendency of groups to place pressure for conformity over critical thinking and to suppress dissent. Groupthink creates collective confirmation bias at the group level: the dominant expectation of the group is no longer questioned by anyone until the reversal effect occurs.

The reinforcement of a belief or behavior simply by the fact that it is already widespread. The bandwagon effect is the mechanism by which an initially random dominant expectation becomes a market expectation or social belief—a direct precursor to market reversal events.

A structured method for preventing errors in advance: You assume that a project has failed and analyze in retrospect what could have gone wrong. Effective counterbalance to overconfidence and dominant expectations as it forcefully activates counterfactual thinking and makes the reversal effect visible before it occurs.

The ability to construct mental scenarios that deviate from actual reality ('What if...?'). Serves as a cognitive tool against dominant expectations: Anyone who actively plays through alternative scenarios loosens the cognitive fixation on a single image of the future and reduces the probability of the reversal effect.

The unconscious filter through which the brain only perceives and processes those environmental stimuli that are compatible with existing expectations and schemas. Selective perception is the fundamental mechanism that enables all other perceptual distortions and explains the persistence of dominant expectations.

The state in which all actors in a system hold the same dominant expectation and no individual actor deviates unilaterally from this expectation because everyone assumes that everyone else is adhering to it. Models the game-theoretic stabilization logic that makes the reversal effect inevitable in the Beckmann model. The dominant expectation is not an irrational error - it is the equilibrium result of rational individual strategies.

The expectations of an individual actor, which are rationally based on the anticipation of the expectations of other actors. Each actor chooses his expectation strategy not in isolation but in response to what he expects from others — whereby expectations stabilize each other before equilibrium is collectively established.

An actor's rational decision to adopt the system's dominant expectation because deviation has a cost as long as everyone else adheres to the dominant expectation. Rational conformity is not blind obedience - it is the game-theoretically optimal answer to an existing equilibrium that the individual actor cannot break alone.

The process by which individual rational conformity decisions cumulatively stabilize a dominant collective expectation. The anchoring does not arise through central control, but emerges from decentralized individual decisions - a feature that makes the balance particularly difficult to break.

The feedback loop through which an existing expectation-Nash equilibrium reinforces itself: Because everyone else holds on to the dominant expectation, conformity is rational — and because conformity is rational, everyone else holds on. This mechanism makes the equilibrium stable against individual attempts at deviation and grows with increasing system size.

The real or anticipated disadvantages that an actor suffers if he is the only one to deviate from the dominant expectation while everyone else clings to it. Costs of deviance can be social, economic, or reputational — what matters is that their mere anticipation is enough to force rational conformity, even if the dominant expectation is objectively misguided.

The systematic inability of the actors in equilibrium to anticipate the reversal effect, because the equilibrium itself fixes attention on the dominant expectation. Equilibrium blindness is not an individual cognitive weakness, but a structural feature of stable equilibria of expectations - it is actively generated by the self-reinforcement mechanism.

The moment or event at which the costs of deviation for one or more actors decrease so much that individual deviation becomes rational — and thus the collective anchoring of expectations begins to erode. The signal point is the game-theoretical prerequisite for the break in equilibrium and thus for the reversal effect.

The collapse of the expectation-Nash equilibrium when enough actors deviate from the signal point so that the equilibrium can no longer be maintained in a self-reinforcing manner. The break in equilibrium is not gradual, but typically occurs as a rapid tipping point - the critical mass of deviant actors triggers a chain reaction.

entities.id: Security principle as a deviation strategy
entities.description: The game-theoretical reformulation of the security principle: Anyone who acts in a process-oriented manner without expectation of the future deviates from the dominant collective expectation strategy - and has therefore stepped out of the expectation-Nash equilibrium before the equilibrium break occurs. In game theory, the safety principle is the only strategy that remains rational regardless of the equilibrium state of the system.

The fundamental problem that all actors in the system face at the same time: Which expectation becomes dominant? The coordination problem is not solved through rational deliberation, but through focal points, historical precedence or power asymmetries - and the result of this solution is the expectation-Nash equilibrium.

An expectation that, because of its salience, historical familiarity, or social visibility, acts as a natural coordination point for the expectation coordination problem. Focal point expectations solve the coordination problem without actors explicitly communicating — they become dominant because everyone expects everyone else to expect them to be dominant.

entities.id: Reversal effect as an equilibrium consequence
entities.description: The causal connection between a stable expectation Nash equilibrium and the reversal effect: Because the equilibrium shapes reality through collective expectation projection, the moment of equilibrium breaking becomes a reversal effect event. The reversal effect is therefore not just a psychological or temporal phenomenon, but rather the consequence of collective equilibria of expectations that can be predicted using game theory.

A non-linearly coupled earth and social system in which physical feedback, human expectations and political reactions intertwine with a time delay. The node forms the core of the subgraph because climate is understood here not only as a natural process, but also as a space for expectations and action.

The property that climatic effects only become visible with a time delay, even though their causes are already taking effect. As a result, expectations can appear to be confirmed for a long time before the material counter-reaction becomes apparent.

A process in which a change further reinforces its own causes or consequences. In graph logic, this corresponds to a material spiral of disappointment, because late correction builds up ever greater counterforces.
A process in which deviations are dampened and a system is returned towards stability. The node represents the climatic equivalent of process-oriented self-correction.

A threshold range after which a system state changes to another state abruptly or with difficulty in reversing. For Beckmann logic, the tipping point is the physical form of a long-prepared reversal event.
The loss of bright reflective surfaces, which absorbs more energy and increases further warming. The node shows how a local physical effect can force a system-wide expectation correction.
The release of bound greenhouse gases from thawing soils, whereby what was initially a slow change gains additional momentum. This shows how late feedback can return not neutrally, but reinforcing.

The absorption of large amounts of heat by oceans, which simulates stability in the short term and returns stored changes in the long term. The node explains why a seemingly stable present can contain an already charged future.
The assumption that a system is safe because the expected negative feedback is not yet visible. This all-clear is dangerous because it interprets inertia as a refutation and not as a storage effect.

The expectation that future technology will lift the current burden of action. It can promote realistic innovation, but becomes dangerous when it replaces present action and acts as an attachment for the future.

The unspoken assumption that current forms of energy and prosperity can be continued in the long term. It acts as a background filter that slows down external scrutiny and makes short-term convenience appear as realism.

The physical feedback from the system through extreme events, measurement data, crop failures, water stress or infrastructure load. It is the material control that tests expectations not with arguments, but with real consequences.

A form of action that reduces risks without clinging to a guaranteed good future. It translates the security principle into climate practice: improving robust processes now, without a salvation narrative.

The event in which expectations that have seemed stable for a long time turn into their opposite due to cumulative physical feedback. Expected controllability turns into loss of control, late correction turns into abrupt compulsion to act.

A linguistically encoded pattern of interpretation that becomes so dominant in a community or institution that it renders alternative perspectives invisible. It serves the same function as the dominant expectation in the model: it defines what is considered normal, probable and possible and insulates against corrections.

The linguistic framing of an issue that determines which aspects remain visible and which remain invisible. Framing does not create new facts, but it determines the expected framework within which facts are interpreted. A strong frame can make the same issue appear as a threat or an opportunity.

Through repeated linguistic reproduction, a narrative gains the status of something self-evident. Repetition is the primary mechanism through which a narrative goes from being consciously stated to being unconsciously presupposed. This corresponds to the transformation of a conscious expectation into a dominant expectation.

The ability of media or institutional actors to determine, through the selection of topics, what a community thinks about — not what it thinks, but what it concerns itself with. Agenda setting creates the thematic basis on which dominant narratives can emerge.

The principle that language not only describes reality, but actively creates it. What is not named hardly exists in social space; what is often named acquires reality status. This principle explains why narratives are not just reactions to reality, but also have the effect of shaping reality.

A feedback structure in which a narrative shapes perception, perception shapes action, action creates facts that appear to confirm the narrative. Narrative loops are the linguistic equivalent of self-fulfilling prophecies and reinforce dominant narratives to the point of being uncorrectable.

The social mechanism by which minority opinions are silenced because those who hold them fear social isolation. The spiral of silence indirectly reinforces the dominant narrative: non-dominant expectations become invisible even though they exist.

A narrative that is explicitly built against a dominant narrative and challenges its basic assumptions. Counter-narratives are a prerequisite for narrative reversal effects, but they often fail because they argue within the frame of the dominant narrative rather than leaving the frame itself.

The conscious reframing of an issue by changing the linguistic frame of reference. Reframing is the operative technique of narrative reversal: it leaves the existing frame instead of fighting it, thereby creating a new space of expectations.

The moment when a dominant narrative loses its stabilizing power and turns into a counter-narrative. Narrative tipping points arise when the discrepancy between narrative and experienced reality becomes too great to be bridged through repetition.

The transfer of the reversal effect to narrative structures: A dominant narrative that is shielded from reality corrections for too long can turn into its opposite - and the new narrative becomes just as dominant as the old one. The narrative reversal effect explains why propaganda that is sustained for too long leads to credibility collapse.

The linguistic protection mechanism that immunizes a dominant narrative against counterinformation. Techniques: Discrediting sources, rebranding criticism as misinformation, tabooing questions. Corresponds to the expectation firewall in the model, but at the linguistic level.

The narrative equivalent of the security principle: narrative design that relies on process openness instead of fixing results. A narrative in safety mode explicitly names uncertainties, leaves room for revision and thereby prevents narrative reversal effects due to accumulated gaps in reality.

The systematic testing of a narrative against external sources of reality that are not controlled by the narrative itself. Corresponds to the external reality check in the model applied to language systems. External narrative checking is the most important protection mechanism against narrative loops.

Method for recognizing whether an expectation only exists or is already dominant in the system. The node combines indicators, thresholds and testing mechanisms so that dominance is not only recognized by the reversal effect.
Observable characteristic that indicates increasing dominance of an expectation. A single indicator doesn't prove anything, but several converging indicators make expectation dominance plausible.
Degree of affective and cognitive commitment to a future outcome. High intensity makes an expectation harder to correct and increases the likelihood that it will override other expectations.

The extent to which an expectation is maintained despite contradictory feedback. Resistance to correction shows that the expectation is no longer just a hypothesis, but rather shields itself from testing.
Frequency with which the same expectation is reproduced in thought, language, media or institutions. Repetition transforms conscious statements into unconscious assumptions.
Emotional energy tied to an expectation, such as hope, fear, pride, or hurt. The stronger the charge, the less neutrally a system can process different information.
Social pressure not to openly doubt a shared expectation. It makes dominance visible because opposing positions become risky not only in terms of content but also socially.

Structural model that understands dominance as exceeding several thresholds. An expectation becomes dominant when intensity, repetition, resistance to correction and social pressure together form a critical zone.

Composite image of the dominance of an expectation. The profile shows which indicators are strong, which are missing and whether an expectation is anchored more individually, collectively or institutionally.
Testing an expectation through feedback that is not generated or controlled by the expectation itself. It separates real stability from apparent confirmation.
Time gap between expectation, action and visible feedback. It makes diagnosis more difficult because missing consequences can be mistakenly read as confirmation.
Misinterpreting a neutral or delayed signal as evidence of one's own expectations. It stabilizes dominance without providing real external scrutiny.
Weak but recurring sign that an expectation is moving into the dominance zone. Early warning signals are important because late corrections often only become visible on the graph after the reversal.
Applying diagnostics to decide whether a system should move to a secure, present-oriented mode. It translates measurement into protection practice without wanting to force future results.

The acoustic equivalent of dominant expectation: tonality produces precise predictions in the listener about what tone, harmony, or resolution will come next. The longer a tonal expectation is built up, the stronger its dominance — and the more powerful the effect when it is broken or fulfilled. Tonal anticipation is the central mechanism through which music creates tension, meaning and emotional impact.

A state of acoustic instability created by dissonance, unresolved leading tones, or dominant seventh chords. Harmonic tension is the structural precursor to the musical reversal effect: it builds up pressure of expectation that must be discharged. The strength of the tension correlates directly with the amplitude of the emotional impact of the subsequent resolution or rupture.

The moment when harmonic tension transitions to stability — typically by returning to the tonic or an expected chord change. Resolution corresponds to the case in which a dominant expectation is fulfilled linearly (time course A). The emotional impact of resolution is directly proportional to the previous intensity of tension: long-built tension dissipating creates catharsis; Immediate resolution without building up tension produces no effect.

The moment when a maximally built-up tonal expectation is not fulfilled but broken - by an unexpected chord, a change in rhythm or a harmonic turn that falls outside the established framework. The dissonance break is the musical model of the reversal effect: a strong tonal expectation reverses into its opposite. The most famous musical innovations in history arise from calculated disruption of dissonance.

The culturally learned and collectively shared expectation of what music should sound like through repeated listening. Listening habit is the social reinforcement of tonal expectation: what a community perceives as 'normal' or 'beautiful' becomes a dominant sound norm through shared listening practice. Listening habits are difficult to correct - new sounds that contradict them are initially perceived as wrong or disturbing.

The institutionally consolidated collection of works, styles and composition rules that is considered the benchmark of musical quality. The musical canon is the institutional embodiment of dominant musical expectations: it determines what counts as 'good music' and initially makes deviant styles invisible or illegitimate. The canon has a self-reinforcing dynamic — it reproduces itself through educational institutions, criticism and support systems.

The historical moment in which a dominant musical style is replaced by a new movement that was initially viewed as a violation of the rules or a provocation. Style revolutions follow the pattern of collective reversal: a style becomes so dominant that it creates its own rigidity until a countermovement—often from the fringes—breaks it. Examples: Atonality vs. late romanticism, punk vs. progressive rock, hip-hop vs. disco production.

The phenomenon that musical innovators are initially rejected precisely because their works break the dominant listening expectations - and are later considered classics for precisely that reason. Beethoven was considered 'unplayable', Stravinsky's Sacre du Printemps caused a scandal, Coltrane was described as 'anti-jazz'. The innovation paradox shows that the dissonance break that initially creates rejection is often a prerequisite for later canonization.

The condition in which a dominant musical style becomes so standardized and repetitive that it prepares for its own break in dissonance. Stylistic rigidity arises when the musical canon no longer tolerates deviating forms and systematically excludes new developments. This state is the musical form of the maximum cementation of preconceptions - and thus the immediate precursor to a style revolution.

Musical action without a predetermined result - in real time, from the present, without attachment to a specific sound idea. Improvisation is the musical embodiment of the safety principle: the improviser releases himself from the expectation of a certain result and acts from the current sound moment. This creates music that is not blocked by dominant assumptions - and often creates the most surprising twists and turns.

The state of complete presence and self-forgetfulness in musical action, in which expectations of the result are completely eliminated and the playing is self-sustaining. The flow state is musical proof of the safety principle: When a musician is fully in the process, without attachment to results, the most effective performances occur. Fixation on perfection or evaluation interrupts flow - a reversal effect of the expectation of one's own performance.

The phenomenon that excessive fixation on an expected perfect result worsens rather than improves musical performance. The expectation of perfection becomes the dominant expectation - and triggers its own reversal effect: tension, stage fright, blockage of the creative flow. The perfectionism trap is a micro-model of the reversal effect at the level of individual musical action.

The shared, often unconscious, expectation of a community or audience about what music should sound like in a particular context. Collective sound expectations arise through cultural socialization, media consumption and listening experience. It is stronger than individual expectation because it generates social confirmation - and thus lays the foundation for listening habits and the musical canon.

The conscious variation of rhythm and meter in order to break and readjust established metric expectations. Rhythmic contrast is a tool for controlling expectations: it can build, confirm or break expectations - and is therefore a compositional equivalent to the safety principle: the composer actively controls the listener's expectations without being tied to a specific form.

A recurring musical motif that anchors a stable association of expectations in the listener - for example with a figure, an emotion or a dramatic moment. The leitmotif is a tool for targeted expectation control: through repetition, it builds up a dominant structure of expectations that the composer can later break or fulfill in order to achieve a strong emotional impact.

The absence of sound as a compositional device that pushes tonal expectation to the maximum. Silence in music is not an empty moment, but a moment of maximum intensity of expectation: the listener projects into the silence what is to come next. What sounds afterwards meets an expectation that has built up to dominance in a vacuum - and thereby creates the strongest possible effect.

The principle of creating and consolidating a stable listening expectation by cyclically returning to the same phrase or motif. Repetition is the building mechanism of dominant tonal expectation: the more often a pattern is repeated, the more natural it appears to the listener - and the stronger the break in dissonance is when the repetition is interrupted or changed.

The psychological state of tension that arises when new information conflicts with existing beliefs or expectations. Cognitive dissonance is the moment in which the reversal effect first becomes noticeable - and at the same time the moment in which the dominant cognitive distortion fights most strongly against correction.

relations.subject: Market Dominant Expectation
relations.description: When a market expectation gains dominance - a near-universal consensus among analysts, media and participants - it creates the structural conditions for a reversal. The mechanism: All available buyers have already bought, all available sellers have already sold. The expectation has exhausted its own ability to fulfill.

relations.subject: Market Dominant Expectation
relations.description: Dominant expectations and dominant narratives reinforce each other: the narrative legitimizes the expectation, the expectation funds the narrative through media attention, analyst coverage, and institutional commitment.

relations.subject: Reflexivity mechanism
relations.description: The reflexivity mechanism implies that the danger of an analysis increases with its scope. A correct analysis distributed to millions becomes a self-defeating prophecy through its own impact on the market.

relations.subject: Self-inclusion of analysis
relations.description: By explicitly modeling the impact of one's conclusions on market behavior, the analyst can anticipate and partially neutralize the reflexive bias created by widespread dissemination.

relations.subject: Requirements analysis (market)
relations.description: Presupposition analysis is the most important tool for recognizing narrative dominance: it asks which assumptions are no longer questioned, which theses have become invisible axioms. These are the fundamental prerequisites, the violation of which would trigger the greatest reversal.

relations.subject: Consensus trading
relations.description: Consensus trading is the direct market expression of a prevailing expectation. The more participants take part, the more dominant the expectation becomes and the reversal requirement becomes more acute. Consensus trading is therefore largely self-destructive.

relations.subject: Opposite positioning
relations.description: A contrarian positioning is only rational if it is based on identified failures of assumptions - and not on mere contrarianism. Without presupposition analysis, the contrarian positioning is simply another form of the prevailing expectation (the expectation that the crowd is always wrong).

relations.subject: Opposite positioning
relations.description: Even a structurally correct contrary thesis fails if the timing is wrong. Position sizing and stop loss discipline must take into account the irreducible uncertainty as to when the reversal event will occur.

relations.subject: Temporal indeterminacy
relations.object: Position determination as a safety principle
relations.description: Since the point in time cannot be determined, the safety principle is not to avoid opposing positions, but rather to dimension them in such a way that survival of the indefinite waiting time is guaranteed. Capital preservation during the waiting period is the operational expression of the Beckmann security principle.

relations.subject: Institutional consolidation of expectations
relations.description: Institutional actors – through index tracking, benchmark commitments and career risk avoidance – systematically reinforce prevailing expectations rather than correcting them. This structural reinforcement is why prevailing market expectations can persist well beyond their rational course and why reversal events ultimately occur violently rather than gradually.

relations.subject: External audit (market)
relations.description: The external check – a data release, a default, a geopolitical shock – is typically the trigger that derails the prevailing expectation. It does not cause a structural reversal; The structural cause is dominance itself. External control is simply the event that makes ignoring dominance no longer possible.

relations.subject: Institutional consolidation of expectations
relations.description: Cemented institutions actively resist external controls through narrative rationalization: “This is a temporary anomaly,” “the model is still valid.” This delay prolongs the dominance phase and increases the eventual magnitude of the reversal.

relations.subject: Market reversal event
relations.description: The reversal event is not the final state, but the transition. It dissolves the prevailing expectation and opens the field for a new configuration. The new actual level will, over time, develop its own prevailing expectations and begin the cycle again.

relations.subject: New actual level (market)
relations.description: Each new actual level - the market structure after the reversal - immediately creates new prevailing expectations among participants who extrapolate the new trend. The Beckmann cycle begins again. This is why secular bear and bull markets eventually reverse.

relations.subject: Disappointment spiral (market)
relations.description: The disappointment spiral typically begins with a consensus trading entry at or near the reversal point. The initial loss is reinterpreted as a buying opportunity (the prevailing expectation is still intact), leading to a larger re-entry coinciding with the deepening reversal.

relations.subject: Position determination as a safety principle
relations.description: The disappointment spiral requires the ability to continue to increase position size after a loss. Strict position size limits prevent this escalation by making spiraling beyond a defined loss threshold physically impossible.

relations.subject: Geopolitical event as an external check
relations.description: Geopolitical shocks are a special class of external controls characterized by low predictability of timing but high predictability of scope. They are particularly effective in breaking cemented institutional expectations because they cannot be rationalized as “market noise.”

relations.subject: Self-inclusion of analysis
relations.description: Self-inclusion is the practical implementation of reflexivity awareness at the level of the individual analyst. Where reflexivity is the general mechanism, self-inclusion is the cognitive practice that operationalizes it.

relations.subject: Requirements analysis (market)
relations.description: Analyzing assumptions in the market context is itself a form of self-inclusion: it doesn't just ask: “What do I believe?” but “What do I assume without realizing it?” – This includes assumptions about what other participants believe and how they will act.

relations.subject: Narrative market dominance
relations.description: A dominant narrative actively filters out conflicting data by categorizing it as noise, exception, or transient anomaly. The stronger the narrative, the more aggressively external checks are rationalized away - until a check arrives that is too big to be absorbed.

relations.subject: Market Dominant Expectation
relations.description: The market dominant expectation (aggregate directional belief of market participants) is the concrete realization of the general concept of dominant expectation in the context of financial markets. What the dominant expectation describes as an abstract structure - an expectation that superimposes other expectations and largely determines the resulting reality - appears in the stock trading subgraph as a collective consensus about price movements.

relations.subject: Market Dominant Expectation
relations.description: The market-dominant expectation fulfills all the definitional characteristics of the dominant future expectation: it is directed towards the future (price development), becomes dominant through intensity and collective isolation and directly triggers the reversal effect. The stock market is therefore an empirically observable test area for abstract mechanisms.

relations.subject: dominant expectation
relations.predicate: occurs in the financial market context as
relations.description: The abstract concept of dominant expectations finds a particularly measurable and data-rich expression in the financial market. The market-dominant expectation is therefore a central empirical case study that makes the properties of dominant expectations (superposition of counter-expectations, structural reversal preparation) visible and testable for AI models.

relations.subject: non-dominant expectation
relations.description: Contrarian positioning is the action strategy that follows a non-dominant expectation: it relies on a market movement that contradicts the prevailing majority opinion. It is therefore the operational implementation of the subdominant expectations in the financial market context.

relations.subject: Market reversal event
relations.description: The market reversal event – the point at which a prevailing market expectation turns into its opposite – is the direct, empirically observable manifestation of the fundamental reversal effect in the financial market context. Price crashes after phases of euphoria or recovery rallies after phases of panic structurally follow exactly the logic of the reversal effect.

relations.subject: Reversal effect
relations.predicate: is observable in the financial market as
relations.description: The abstract reversal effect is particularly visible in the financial market because price data documents the reversal of expectations precisely and in a timely manner. The market reversal event thus provides a historically proven, quantifiable test for the fundamental reversal effect mechanism.

relations.subject: Unconscious expectation
relations.predicate: is collectively aggregated the basis of
relations.description: The market-dominating expectation is not only made up of conscious forecasts, but also largely of unconscious expectations of market participants - automated extrapolation heuristics, emotional conditioning through past profits/losses, and habitual risk assessments. The collective unconscious field of expectations often shapes the market trend more strongly than explicit analyses.

relations.subject: Own expected future
relations.description: When the individual expected future of a large number of market participants points in the same direction, collective consensus trading arises. One's own expected future is therefore the psychological micro-component from which macroscopic consensus trading is aggregated.

relations.subject: Consensus trading
relations.description: Consensus trading – taking a market position because majority analysis, media and peers recommend it – structurally corresponds to the low-complex solution level of Beckmann logic. It is the obvious, direct response to a market problem (how do I invest?) without taking higher system complexity into account. Like all low-complexity solutions, it systematically leads to a negative result (loss at the turning point).

relations.subject: Opposite positioning
relations.description: The contrarian positioning - acting against the prevailing market expectations if the prerequisite analysis identifies an overextension of dominance - corresponds to the highly complex solution level of Beckmann logic. It requires system understanding, prerequisite analysis and overcoming social pressure. As a non-obvious, context-aware strategy, it leads to positive results when executed correctly.

relations.subject: External audit (market)
relations.description: The external market test - an earnings report, a macroeconomic shock, a geopolitical event - is the concrete realization of the strong external test of Beckmann logic in the financial market context. It is the empirical signal from reality that contradicts a prevailing expectation and can no longer be ignored. The audit strong is therefore the superordinate category to which external audit (market) belongs.

relations.subject: Requirements analysis (market)
relations.description: The market-specific prerequisite analysis applies the general methodology of Beckmann logic (systematic questioning of hidden assumptions) directly to the financial market. Standard questions like 'Is interest really the determining factor?' or 'Is the narrative already priced in?' are applications of the general presupposition analysis principle from Beckmann logic to the market context.

relations.subject: New actual level (market)
relations.description: The market state after a reversal event - the new actual level - structurally corresponds to the new actual level of Beckmann logic. It is not the end state, but the starting point of the next cycle: the new current level immediately generates new dominant expectations, which in turn prepare the next reversal mechanism. The Beckmann cycle is thus perpetuated cyclically in the financial market.

relations.subject: Position determination as a safety principle
relations.description: The Beckmann security principle - do not form dominant future expectations, act in the now, limit the risk of loss - is specifically operationalized in stock trading as position determination as a security principle. The market application translates the abstract protective rule into clear trading mechanisms: position size limits, stop-loss discipline, capital preservation as the primary goal.

relations.subject: Security principle
relations.predicate: is operationalized in stock trading as
relations.object: Position determination as a safety principle
relations.description: The security principle has one of the most precise and immediately testable applications in the financial market. Capital preservation instead of profit maximization, limiting future attachment to positions and process-oriented trading (quality of entry) instead of result-oriented trading (securing a specific price target) are the direct financial equivalents of the security principle.

relations.subject: Disappointment spiral (market)
relations.description: The market-specific spiral of disappointment (consensus entry → loss → reinterpretation as a buying opportunity → increase in loss → total loss) is the direct application of the general spiral of disappointment to the financial market context. Both follow the same basic pattern: hope → reversal → increased hope → increased reversal. The market makes this process quantifiable and time-based.

relations.subject: Disappointment spiral
relations.predicate: manifests itself in the financial market as
relations.description: The general spiral of disappointment finds a particularly precise and historically well-documented manifestation in the financial market, since price data, position sizes and trading times can be objectively measured. relations.subject: Market Dominant Expectation
relations.description: The market-dominant expectation fulfills all the characteristics of the dominant future expectation: it is future-oriented, becomes dominant through collective intensity, is reinforced by the isolation of counter-expectations and, when dominance is overextended, directly triggers the reversal effect.

relations.subject: Future attachment
relations.description: Emotional future attachment – the commitment to a specific future outcome – is the psychological driver of consensus trading. When market participants are emotionally attached to a price target, they tend to hold and expand the position despite conflicting external checks, reinforcing consensus trading and setting up the reversal event.

relations.subject: Act without expectation

A trader who uses strict position sizing and stop losses trades from the now (quality of the setup) without committing to a specific price target.

relations.subject: Cognitive dissonance
relations.description: Cognitive dissonance – the contradiction between conscious hope and unconscious fear – prevents the rational processing of external market tests. When an investor holds a position with strong hope, negative earnings reports, technical breaks, or sector rotations are rationalized as meaningless rather than being processed as an external corrective signal.

relations.subject: Self-inclusion of analysis
relations.description: The self-inclusion of the analysis – including one's own market impact in the analysis – is the direct market application of the critical modeling vs. expectation distinction. Anyone who only models (epistemic understanding of reflexivity) remains safe; whoever expects (ontological commitment to a result of his analysis) creates the distortion that he claims to analyze.

relations.subject: Institutional consolidation of expectations
relations.description: Institutional expectation consolidation – the process by which banks, pension funds and index funds immunize their dominant market expectation through structural mechanisms (index tracking, benchmark commitments) – is the market-specific realization of preconception cementation. Institutions cement their market expectations not out of irrational stubbornness but rather out of structural incentives, which exactly replicates the pattern of preconception cementation.

relations.subject: Pre-assumptions_cementation
relations.predicate: appears in the financial market as
relations.description: The cementing of preconceptions finds an institutionally anchored and systemically reinforcing form in the financial market. Institutional actors employ all three modes of cementation: passive cementation (benchmark conformity filters counter-expectations), active reinterpretation (negative data as a temporary anomaly), and constructive cementation (narrative rationalizations that permanently neutralize external scrutiny).

relations.subject: Narrative market dominance
relations.description: The dominant narrative – a story like 'the AI revolution' or 'the commodity supercycle' that is so widespread that it filters out contradictions – is structurally equivalent to constructive cementation: a new, actively installed presupposition that permanently neutralizes external scrutiny. The narrative fulfills all three conditions of constructive cementing: it cannot be immediately refuted, it explains away the external examination, and it itself creates new presuppositions.

relations.subject: Passive_cementation

relations.description: Consensus trading activates passive cementation: Anyone who trades with the consensus does not register external tests as relevant correction signals, but rather filters them as noise. The dominant market expectation acts as a cognitive filter structure - contradictory data is categorized as an exception or temporary volatility, not as evidence against the preconception.

relations.subject: Active_Reinterpretation
relations.description: Active reinterpretation - a negative result is interpreted as evidence of the need for greater effort - manifests itself in stock trading as a spiral of disappointment: a loss after a consensus entry is reinterpreted as a buying opportunity, which leads to an increase in position, which coincides with the deepening trend reversal and multiplies the loss.

relations.subject: Requirements analysis (market)
relations.description: The market-specific prerequisite analysis is the direct operational counter-process to the cementing of pre-assumptions in the financial context. By actively questioning which market assumptions are no longer being questioned, she uncovers the cemented preconceptions that form the breeding ground for the next reversal event. The prerequisite analysis is therefore the market equivalent of de-cementing preconceptions.

relations.subject: Assumptions_Analysis

relations.description: The general assumption analysis of Beckmann logic finds its domain-specific implementation in the financial market. The prerequisite analysis (market) translates the abstract procedure into concrete market questions: What assumptions are in the consensus? What has to be true for the narrative to be valid? At what point would the majority revise their expectations?

relations.subject: Cementation_extended_time scale

relations.description: The observation that preconception cementation lengthens the time scale of the reversal effect and increases its amplitude directly explains why institutional expectation consolidation leads to particularly violent market reversal events. relations.subject: Temporal indeterminacy
relations.description: The temporal indeterminacy - the irreducible uncertainty about when a correctly identified reversal condition will manifest itself in a price movement - is the direct consequence of the cementing extended time scale. Institutional cementation can make correct contrarian analysis appear false in the market for years before the reversal event occurs.

relations.subject: Cementation_vs_safety principle
relations.object: Position determination as a safety principle

relations.subject: Reflexivity mechanism

relations.subject: Problem as a threat (dominant expectation)
relations.description: The prevailing cultural expectation that problems indicate failure or blame is precisely the assumption that must be reversed to enable effective problem solving. This reformulation is the fundamental cognitive shift of the entire framework.

relations.subject: Problem as a resource
relations.object: Psychological safety for an honest analysis
relations.description: Only when problems are truly treated as valuable resources can the psychological safety necessary for honest, open analysis arise. Without this reframing, participants are more likely to protect themselves rather than shed light on the problem.

relations.subject: Psychological safety for an honest analysis
relations.description: Honest and motivated participation - only possible in a psychologically safe environment - is the prerequisite for an effective root cause analysis. Security is not a soft advantage, but a hard epistemic requirement for an accurate diagnosis.

relations.subject: Problem analysis (cause)
relations.object: Analysis of requirements for problem solving
relations.description: An effective root cause analysis must begin by questioning the assumptions embedded in the problem statement. Without prerequisite analysis, the analysis operates in a potentially false framework and cannot reach the true cause.

relations.subject: Problem analysis (cause)
relations.description: A thorough root cause analysis consistently requires more time than the initial pressure suggests. Acceptance of this investment is operationally essential; Resisting the pressure to act prematurely is a core discipline of effective problem solving.

relations.subject: Symptom vs. causing confusion
relations.description: Failure to distinguish between symptoms and causes is the generative mechanism of rapid behavior. The quick fix resolves the visible symptom because the underlying cause has not been identified.

relations.subject: Quick Fix (low complexity solution)
relations.object: Reversal effect in the quick-fix culture
relations.description: The habitual application of quick solutions triggers the reverse effect: the prevailing expectation of efficiency is transformed into increasing systemic fragility. The accumulated unresolved root causes eventually interact and lead to failures that are far greater than the original problems.

relations.subject: Quick Fix (low complexity solution)
relations.description: Quick solutions close the problem loop early and thus prevent the reflective learning phase from occurring. The value of the problem as a resource is never exhausted and the system remains as vulnerable as before.

relations.subject: Distinction between stabilization and solution
relations.description: Explicitly categorizing immediate mitigation measures as “stabilization” rather than “solution” prevents the cognitive closure that transforms a temporary measure into a permanent misclassification. This distinction is the most important protection against rapid behavior.

relations.subject: Problem analysis (cause)
relations.description: A clear cause analysis makes the difference between stabilization and solution operationally visible: As soon as the true cause is known, it becomes clear whether the measures taken have eliminated this cause or simply suppressed its symptoms.

relations.subject: Problem analysis (cause)
relations.description: When the true root cause is correctly identified, the solution is usually relatively clear, and the solution results in real, lasting improvement - fundamental progress rather than symptom suppression.

relations.subject: Positive result: fundamental progress
relations.description: The raw material for the reflective learning phase provides a really positive result: understanding what worked, why it worked and what new skills were created. The learning phase extracts and consolidates this understanding.

relations.subject: Reflective learning phase
relations.description: The reflective learning phase transforms the problem-solving event into a permanent increase in system capability, bringing the system to its new actual level – the next iteration of the improvement cycle.

relations.subject: External check-in to solve problems
relations.description: The only valid confirmation that a problem has actually been solved and not just stabilized is an observable change in system behavior under real-world conditions. Internal consensus is an inadequate test; External reality is the primary control.

relations.subject: Problem as a resource

relations.description: Fundamentally reframing problems as resources allows a system to build comprehensive problem-solving capabilities over time. Without this reformulation, each problem is an isolated event and not an opportunity for development.

relations.subject: Analysis of requirements for problem solving
relations.description: Systematic questioning of the assumptions anchored in the problem prevents the common error when treating symptoms: The question arises as to whether the “obvious” problem is actually the actual problem or whether it is caused by a false assumption.

relations.subject: Problem solving as a system capability
relations.description: A system with mature problem-solving capability naturally enters a self-reinforcing cycle of improvement: each well-solved problem increases the ability to handle the next, and each new level of capability reveals new higher-order problems to solve.

relations.subject: Problem as a resource
relations.description: Reframing problems as resources is the practical application of Beckmann logic to everyday problem solving. Beckmann logic assumes that problems are viewed as sources of knowledge and not as threats - which is exactly what encodes problem as a resource as a core concept.

relations.subject: Problem as a threat (dominant expectation)

relations.subject: Quick Fix (low complexity solution)

relations.subject: Iterative improvement cycle
relations.description: The iterative improvement cycle is the domain-specific realization of the new current level in Beckmann logic: After a highly complex solution, there is no static final state, but a new platform from which the next problem becomes visible.

relations.subject: Positive result: fundamental progress
relations.description: Fundamental progress – increasing understanding, performance and resilience – is the realization of the positive result of Beckmann logic. The benchmark is not the elimination of the problem, but rather the increase in system capability.

relations.subject: Problem solving as a system capability
relations.description: While 'Beckmann logic explains' describes the general framework, 'Problem solving as a system skill' specifies how the repeated application of highly complex solutions leads to an emerging organizational competence - a meta-skill that reinforces itself.

relations.subject: problem

relations.subject: Reversal effect in the quick-fix culture
relations.description: The reversal effect in quick-fix culture is not a metaphor - it is the direct manifestation of the fundamental reversal mechanism in problem-solving strategies. The expectation of 'speed = efficiency' is reversed to 'accumulated causes = systemic fragility'.

relations.subject: Problem as a threat (dominant expectation)

relations.subject: Psychological safety for an honest analysis

relations.subject: Reversal effect in the quick-fix culture

relations.subject: Reflective learning phase

relations.subject: External check-in to solve problems

relations.subject: Analysis of requirements for problem solving

relations.description: The quick-fix reversal effect on the week-to-month scale: suppressed causes accumulate within a few iteration cycles to form a systemic crisis that is greater than the original individual problems. On this scale the reversal is still correctable.

relations.subject: Reversal effect in the quick-fix culture
relations.description: If the quick-fix culture becomes culturally anchored (identity presumption: 'We are a fast-acting team'), the reversal scale shifts to years to decades: the deeper the anchoring, the greater the amplitude of the reversal.

relations.subject: External check-in to solve problems
relations.description: The external check-in is a mechanism for increasing the speed of feedback: By explicitly checking after each problem-solving cycle whether the generative mechanism has been changed, the system shortens the time scale of the potential reversal effect - and thus reduces its amplitude.

relations.subject: Reflective learning phase
relations.description: The reflective learning phase works directly against the depth of the anchoring of false presuppositions: By explicitly extracting and testing assumptions after each problem cycle, the chance that they solidify into identity presuppositions is reduced.

relations.subject: Quick Fix (low complexity solution)
relations.description: Every quick fix applied without subsequent root cause analysis increases the amplitude of the later reversal: the unresolved cause is anchored more deeply through the masking symptom management, which increases the energy of the later rebound.

relations.subject: Analysis of requirements for problem solving
relations.predicate: was used in an exemplary manner
relations.description: Rome's Fabian tactics after Cannae are the historical prime example of prerequisite analysis: Rome questioned the assumption that 'direct confrontation leads to victory', recognized it as wrong and developed a completely new strategy - instead of using the quick fix 'even more troops'.

relations.subject: Problem solving as a system capability
relations.description: Friedrich's potato strategy is a historical example of problem solving as a system capability: he analyzed the actual problem (not cultivation, but the expectations of the population), chose a highly complex solution and thereby transformed Prussia's system capability in food security.

relations.subject: Distinction between stabilization and solution
relations.description: Hannibal after Cannae confused stabilization (military victory) with solution (political surrender of Rome). He failed to make the critical distinction and waited for a capitulation that never came - a textbook example of failure through this mix-up.

relations.subject: Problem as a threat (dominant expectation)
relations.object: Psychological safety for an honest analysis
relations.description: As long as the dominant expectation 'problem = failure/guilt' is active, psychological safety cannot emerge. Participants hide information, minimize the severity of the problem, and assign blame—all of which make honest analysis impossible.

relations.subject: Problem as a resource
relations.object: Psychological safety for an honest analysis
relations.description: Reframing 'problem as a resource' is the necessary cultural condition for psychological safety: when problems are seen as valuable signals, they lose their threat to status and belonging - and honest analysis becomes possible.

relations.subject: Problem analysis (cause)
relations.object: Analysis of requirements for problem solving
relations.description: A complete cause analysis always begins with the prerequisite analysis: Before the actual cause is sought, all assumptions embedded in the problem formulation must be questioned - otherwise you are looking in the wrong deep structure.

relations.subject: Analysis of requirements for problem solving
relations.description: Prerequisite analysis is the most direct defense against symptom treatment: it forces the system to ask the question 'What do we take for granted that could be wrong?' - and thus reveals the hidden level on which symptom and cause fall apart.

relations.subject: Symptom vs. causing confusion
relations.description: Anyone who confuses symptoms with the problem inevitably ends up with the quick fix: the measure is directed against what is visible, not against the generative mechanism. The result is superficial relief while the basic problem remains active.

relations.subject: Quick Fix (low complexity solution)
relations.object: Reversal effect in the quick-fix culture
relations.description: Every quick fix without root cause analysis is a contribution to building the reversal effect: the true signal is masked, the learning cycle is closed prematurely, the vulnerability of the system is restored - and the next outbreak becomes more severe.

relations.subject: Distinction between stabilization and solution
relations.object: Reversal effect in the quick-fix culture
relations.description: Anyone who consistently differentiates between stabilization (immediate damage limitation) and solution (elimination of causes) does not close the learning cycle prematurely and thus prevents the central mechanism of the quick-fix reversal effect.

relations.subject: Time spent on analysis
relations.description: Without consciously investing more time than initially appears necessary, any analysis remains on the surface. The pressure to act quickly is a structural feature of every problem situation - this must be actively and explicitly resisted.

relations.subject: Problem analysis (cause)
relations.description: An honest, in-depth root cause analysis is the most direct path to a positive result: When the true problem is clearly understood, the highly complex solution often emerges by itself - and the system reaches a new level of performance.

relations.subject: External check-in to solve problems
relations.description: The external check-in is the only valid method to check whether a solution (generative mechanism changed) or just stabilization has actually been achieved. Internal consensus is not a sufficient test.

relations.subject: Reflective learning phase
relations.object: Problem solving as a system capability
relations.description: The systematic application of the reflective learning phase after each problem cycle is the mechanism through which problem solving becomes a system capability: Each insight extracted permanently increases the system's competence for the next problem class.

relations.subject: Problem solving as a system capability
relations.description: A system that develops problem solving as a system capability automatically goes through the iterative improvement cycle: Each highly complex problem solved reduces the vulnerability for this problem class and generates the insights for the next level.

relations.subject: Iterative improvement cycle
relations.description: The iterative improvement cycle closes: the new problem at the next actual level - when the reframing is internalized - is again viewed as a resource, not as a threat. So the cycle begins again at a higher level.

relations.subject: Dominant strategy
relations.description: If all actors play their dominant strategy, this results in a Nash equilibrium - even if it is collectively suboptimal.

relations.subject: Nash equilibrium
relations.description: Every Nash equilibrium requires that all actors' expectations about each other's behavior are correct and aligned.

relations.subject: Prisoner's dilemma
relations.description: The Prisoner's Dilemma is the prototypical example of how individually rational decisions produce collectively irrational outcomes.

relations.subject: Dominant strategy
relations.description: If both actors have a dominant strategy (defect), they end up in prisoner's dilemma equilibrium — suboptimal for both.

relations.subject: Rational Choice Paradox
relations.description: The rational choice paradox is the formal basis of the game theory reversal effect: individual rationality produces collective reversal.

relations.subject: Nash equilibrium
relations.description: A suboptimal Nash equilibrium produced by simultaneous dominant expectations is the game theory formalization of the reversal effect.

relations.subject: Coordination problem
relations.description: In coordination games without a dominant strategy, actors converge on Schelling points — natural focal points that arise from shared expectation structures.

relations.subject: Schelling point
relations.description: Schelling points are equilibria that are pre-structured by shared unconscious expectations — equilibria of expectations without explicit communication.

relations.subject: Iterated Games
relations.description: Only in repeated games can tit-for-tat emerge as a robust strategy because it is based on the expectation of future interaction.

relations.subject: Tit for tat
relations.description: Tit-for-Tat does not fix a dominant expectation about the system behavior, but reacts situationally - it corresponds to the security principle in the choice of strategy.

relations.subject: Tit for tat
relations.description: Tit-for-Tat is a concrete implementation of the game theory security principle: start openly (without fixed expectations), react to reality.

relations.subject: Evolutionary stable equilibrium
relations.description: Evolutionary stable equilibria are a subset of Nash equilibria that are additionally stable to deviations — they biologically cement dominant expectation structures.

relations.subject: Equilibrium selection
relations.description: For multiple Nash equilibria, equilibrium selection via Schelling points solves the coordination problem through shared dominant expectations.

relations.subject: Market expectation as a Nash equilibrium
relations.description: Consensus positions in financial markets are the clearest empirical application of the game theory reversal effect: the consensus destroys the expected outcome.

relations.subject: Security principle in game theory
relations.description: An actor who applies the game theory security principle (not fixing a dominant expectation) can break out of the prisoner's dilemma equilibrium by signaling cooperation without a fixed expectation about the reaction.

relations.subject: Coordination problem
relations.description: Dominant strategies are missing in the coordination problem; in the prisoner's dilemma they exist and are the problem. This structural difference determines which type of dominant expectations prevails.

relations.subject: Iterated Games
relations.description: In repeated games, actors can achieve cooperation equilibria by building expectations about future interaction - the dominant defection strategy loses its dominance.

relations.subject: Metacognition
relations.description: Metacognition is the primary cognitive mechanism through which the ability to act arises despite a deterministic basic structure.

relations.subject: Veto function
relations.description: The veto function is the neurologically proven mechanism of freedom as an interruption - it establishes the ability to act without a free act of initiation.

relations.subject: Neuroplasticity as a space of freedom
relations.description: Neuroplasticity provides biological evidence that deterministic neuronal systems have true degrees of freedom in learning.

relations.subject: Predictive processing
relations.description: The predictive processing model shows at which point in the neural process the interruption of expectations takes effect: when updating the prediction models.

relations.subject: Expectation interruption
relations.description: An interruption of expectation is only possible if metacognition has previously made the dominant expectation visible.

relations.subject: Expectation interruption
relations.description: The practical interruption of expectations is the phenomenal expression of the neurologically described veto mechanism.

relations.subject: Habit formation
relations.description: Through habit formation, the ability to act becomes an automated practice — it no longer requires constant conscious effort.

relations.subject: Neuroplasticity as a space of freedom
relations.description: Habits are formed through neuroplastic restructuring — the biological basis of learning through repeated practice.

relations.subject: Compatibilist practice
relations.description: Philosophical compatibilism provides the conceptual basis for why agency is coherently possible under determinism.

relations.subject: Compatibilist practice
relations.object: Process orientation as a practice of freedom
relations.description: Process orientation is the practical implementation of compatibilist freedom: acting based on one's own values, not based on deterministically fixed expectations of results.

relations.subject: Self-regulation loop
relations.description: Metacognition is the observational component of the self-regulation loop.

relations.subject: Self-regulation loop
relations.description: Expectation disruption is the intervention component of the self-regulation loop.

relations.subject: Self-regulation loop
relations.description: Habit formation is the consolidation component of the self-regulation loop.

relations.subject: Constraints as structure providers
relations.description: Constraints enable the ability to act by structuring the decision space - without constraints there are no meaningful decisions.

relations.subject: Determinism paradox resolved
relations.description: The resolution of the determinism paradox is based on the compatibilist framework.

relations.subject: Determinism paradox resolved
relations.description: The four mechanisms of resolution (metacognition, veto, neuroplasticity, habit) are integrated into the self-regulation loop.

relations.subject: Process orientation as a practice of freedom
relations.description: Once the paradox has been resolved, process orientation is the natural practical consequence: act in the now without fixating expectations of results.

relations.subject: Predictive processing
relations.description: Metacognition operates on the predictive models of the predictive brain — it is the conscious observation of one's own predictive processes.

relations.subject: Game theory reversal effect
relations.description: The game theory reversal effect is the formal translation of the Beckmann core principle into game theory language: It shows that dominant individual strategies at the system level produce their own opposite - structurally identical to the reversal effect. This connection is the most direct bridge between the Y subgraph and the main graph.

relations.subject: Dominant strategy
relations.description: What the main graph describes as the 'strongest active expectation within a system that overrides others' (D7 dominant expectation), game theory formalizes as a dominant strategy: a strategy that is optimal regardless of the behavior of the other players. The relation makes the intuitive Beckmann concept mathematically tangible.

relations.subject: Security principle in game theory
relations.description: The security principle of the main graph (acting without expectation, joy in the now) is extended to multi-agent scenarios by this entity: In iterated games, Tit-for-Tat is the game-theoretic implementation of the security principle. The connection shows that the security principle is not only an individual strategy, but also a collective one.

relations.subject: Evolutionary stable equilibrium
relations.description: An evolutionary stable equilibrium describes why dominant strategies/expectations prevail in a population and become resistant to deviations - this is the game theory explanation for what the main graph refers to as the cementation of preconceptions (F5). Both concepts describe the same mechanism at different levels of description.

relations.subject: Market expectation as a Nash equilibrium
relations.description: A market-dominating expectation (E5) arises in game theory as a Nash equilibrium: If all market participants have the same expectation about future price developments and no party has an incentive to deviate from it, a collective expectation equilibrium is reached - which then prepares its own collapse (E5 market reversal event) through the reflexivity mechanism.

relations.subject: Ability to act under constraints
relations.description: The ability to act under constraints resolves the paradox of free will at the problem level: It shows that the security principle does not presuppose metaphysical freedom of will, but rather suffices functional ability to act within deterministic constraints. The paradox thus turns from a philosophical problem into a tension that can be bridged in practice.

relations.subject: Ability to act under constraints
relations.description: The security principle describes the what (action without dominant expectations, joy in the now); The ability to act under constraints describes the how: through which neuroscientific and philosophical mechanism this attitude is even possible in the deterministically structured brain.

relations.subject: Metacognition
relations.description: The security principle requires recognizing and interrupting dominant future expectations. Metacognition—thinking about one's own thinking—is the concrete cognitive mechanism through which this occurs. Without metacognition, the security principle remains an abstract commandment with no path to implementation.

relations.subject: Metacognition
relations.description: The prior assumption analysis (F5) is described as a systematic procedure; Metacognition is its individual cognitive counterpart. Metacognition makes presupposition analysis internalizable: It makes it possible to recognize one's own presuppositions in everyday life without having to carry out a formal analysis procedure.

relations.subject: Habit formation
relations.description: Joy in the now is described in the main graph as an attitude that avoids attachment to the future. The Habit formation is therefore the habitual foundation of this core attitude.

relations.subject: Compatibilist practice
relations.description: Intrinsic motivation (E2) presupposes that actions are not determined by external expectations. Compatibilist practice provides the philosophical framework that shows why intrinsic motivation is also possible in a deterministically structured system: freedom as agreement with one's own, not externally determined, expectations.
