relations.subject: Determinism paradox resolved
relations.object: Free will and the safety principle (nuclear voltage)
relations.description: The core tension between free will and the security principle (F7) is the strongest open tension in the main graph. The determinism paradox resolved (X1) is the direct synthesis node: It shows that the security principle does not presuppose libertarian free will, but is compatibilistically coherent - and thus resolves the core tension in terms of content.

relations.subject: Constraints as structure providers
relations.description: The security principle is itself a constraint: a self-imposed restriction on the formation of expectations. The

relations.subject: Confirmation bias
relations.description: Confirmation bias is the primary self-reinforcer of dominant cognitive distortions: every new piece of information is interpreted in such a way that it confirms the existing dominance.

relations.subject: Anchor effect
relations.description: The initial anchor — often the first dominant expectation — becomes the immovable reference against which all subsequent information is measured.

relations.subject: Availability heuristic
relations.description: Vivid, emotionally charged expectations are rated as more likely by the availability heuristic — further increasing their cognitive dominance.

relations.subject: Selective perception
relations.description: Selective perception is the basis of confirmation bias: only those who filter unconsciously can selectively prefer confirming information.

relations.subject: Selective perception
relations.description: The availability heuristic operates on the filtered data stream that selective perception creates — emotionally charged content passes through the filter more easily.

relations.subject: Loss aversion
relations.description: The intense sensation of impending loss makes the expectation of loss the dominant cognitive force, displacing all other considerations.

relations.subject: Overconfidence effect
relations.description: Self-overestimation makes the actor immune to corrective signals: those who believe their judgment to be infallible do not notice any indications to the contrary.

relations.subject: Sunk cost fallacy
relations.description: Investments that have already been made bind the actor to the original expectation - giving up would mean recognizing the investment as a mistake.

relations.subject: Dominant cognitive distortion
relations.description: When many individuals share the same cognitive bias, the social signal reinforces the sense of rightness — the dominant bias becomes the collective belief.

relations.subject: Herd behavior
relations.description: Herd behavior is the mechanism that drives the bandwagon effect: the more people join in, the stronger the pull.

relations.subject: Groupthink
relations.description: Groupthink systematically eliminates any counterfactual consideration because deviant scenarios are considered disloyal or disruptive.

relations.subject: Bandwagon effect
relations.description: The bandwagon effect turns a common cognitive bias into a social fact — the dominant expectation receives external confirmation through mere diffusion.

relations.subject: Dominant cognitive distortion
relations.description: When reality can no longer be ignored, cognitive dissonance arises - this is the moment when the dominant distortion meets resistance.

relations.subject: Cognitive dissonance (psychology)
relations.description: In response to the state of tension, the brain increasingly searches for confirmatory information - dissonance paradoxically reinforces the confirmation bias.

relations.subject: Premortem analysis
relations.description: The forced confrontation with failure breaks down the cognitive immunity of self-overestimation.

relations.subject: Counterfactual thinking
relations.description: Actively playing through alternative scenarios puts the fixing power of the initial anchor into perspective.

relations.subject: Premortem analysis
relations.description: Both mechanisms work together: counterfactual thinking provides the alternative scenarios, premortem analysis gives them a structured framework.

relations.subject: Cognitive dissonance (psychology)
relations.description: In the best case, the state of tension of cognitive dissonance leads to a revision of the dominant expectation - this corresponds to the moment immediately before the reversal effect.

relations.subject: Selective perception
relations.description: The anchoring effect only works because selective perception preferentially processes anchor-consistent information.

relations.subject: Herd behavior
relations.description: When everyone thinks the same thing, social pressure arises that institutionalizes groupthink.

relations.subject: Dominant cognitive distortion
relations.description: The dominant cognitive distortion (Y2) and the dominant expectation (D7) structurally describe the same phenomenon at different levels of observation: While D7 describes the systemic takeover by a single expectation at the macro level, Y2 models the same mechanism at the individual psychological level. Both block access to external reality and increase the probability of the reversal effect.

relations.subject: Dominant cognitive distortion
relations.description: When a cognitive distortion becomes dominant, corrective signals from external reality become invisible — the very condition that, according to Beckmann logic, makes the reversal effect inevitable. The dominant cognitive distortion is therefore the individual psychological triggering mechanism of the reversal effect.

relations.subject: Premortem analysis
relations.description: The security principle requires not to form dominant expectations about the future and to act in a process-oriented manner. Premortem analysis is the most concrete method available for implementing this principle: it forces you to mentally assume failure, thereby separating expectation from the hoped-for result and making the reversal effect visible before it occurs.

relations.subject: Herd behavior
relations.description: Herd behavior is the social amplification mechanism through which individual cognitive biases are transformed into collective dominant expectations. The more individuals share the same expectation, the more dominant it becomes in terms of the E2 subgraph — and the more likely its reversal effect becomes. Herd behavior is therefore the direct social precursor of the E2 source of danger.

relations.subject: Selective perception
relations.description: Preconception cementation describes the process by which a system protects its preconceptions from refutation — by not recognizing contrary reality as a test. Selective perception is the basic neurological mechanism that makes this process possible: The unconscious filter, which only allows information that is consistent with assumptions, structurally deactivates the system's ability to learn and thus explains why cementation is not a conscious decision.

relations.subject: Individual expectation strategy
relations.description: Many individual expectation strategies, each of which reacts to the anticipated strategies of others, cumulate emergently to form a collective anchoring of the dominant expectation - without central control.

relations.subject: Rational conformity
relations.description: Because deviation carries costs, the rational actor chooses conformity as a strategy — and this choice shapes his individual expectation strategy.

relations.subject: Collective anchoring of expectations
relations.description: The collective anchoring of the dominant expectation is the process by which the expectation-Nash equilibrium reaches and maintains its stable state.

relations.subject: self-reinforcing mechanism
relations.description: The feedback loop of rational conformity actively keeps the equilibrium stable — as long as the self-reinforcing mechanism is intact, the equilibrium is resistant to individual attempts to deviate.

relations.subject: Deviation costs
relations.description: Only the anticipated costs of deviation make conformity a rational strategy - without costs, individual deviation would not be a game-theoretic decision, but arbitrary.

relations.subject: Deviation costs
relations.description: Because deviation costs enforce conformity, equilibrium reinforces itself through its own cost dynamics — deviation costs are the fuel of the self-reinforcing mechanism.

relations.subject: Equilibrium blindness
relations.description: As long as equilibrium blindness prevails, actors cannot recognize the signal point - blindness is the primary reason why equilibrium breaks occur unexpectedly and rapidly.

relations.subject: self-reinforcing mechanism
relations.description: The feedback loop of rational conformity fixes collective attention on the dominant expectation and systematically renders alternative scenarios invisible.

relations.subject: Signal point
relations.description: When deviation costs fall below a critical threshold and the first actors deviate, the chain reaction of equilibrium breaking begins - the signal point is the game-theoretical tipping moment.

relations.subject: Break in equilibrium
relations.object: Reversal effect as an equilibrium consequence
relations.description: From Beckmann's perspective, the collapse of the equilibrium is the reversal effect event: the dominant collective expectation reverses into its opposite because the equilibrium can no longer be maintained.

relations.subject: Expectation coordination problem
relations.description: The coordination problem — which expectation becomes dominant — is solved by the focal point: the most salient, historically familiar, or socially visible expectation wins without explicit communication.

relations.subject: Focal point expectation
relations.description: The focal point expectation is the starting point of collective anchoring: it provides the initial dominant expectation around which the actors' rational conformity decisions are organized.

relations.subject: Expectation-Nash equilibrium
relations.object: Reversal effect as an equilibrium consequence
relations.description: A stable expectation-Nash equilibrium carries with it the condition of its own collapse - and thus the reversal effect: the more stable the equilibrium, the more rapid and complete the break.

relations.subject: Security principle as a deviation strategy
relations.description: Anyone who acts in a process-oriented manner without expectations of the future is structurally not part of the expectation-Nash equilibrium - and therefore not affected by equilibrium blindness. The safety principle is the game-theoretically correct prophylaxis.

relations.subject: Security principle as a deviation strategy
relations.description: Anyone who was not in equilibrium from the start bears no deviation costs when the equilibrium is broken - the safety principle is the only strategy that permanently avoids deviation costs.

relations.subject: Expectation coordination problem
relations.description: Only because the coordination problem exists does an equilibrium form at all - the equilibrium is the emergent solution to the coordination problem under rational expectation strategies.

relations.subject: Expectation-Nash equilibrium
relations.description: The expectation Nash equilibrium is the domain-specific application of the general Nash equilibrium to collective expectation structures in the Beckmann model. It explains how the Nash principle - no actor deviates unilaterally - is transferred to expectations as a strategy variable and thereby formalizes the reversal effect in game theory.

relations.subject: Expectation-Nash equilibrium
relations.description: The dominant future expectation is the substantive core phenomenon in the Beckmann model - the expectation-Nash equilibrium provides its game-theoretical explanatory structure. It shows why the dominant expectation does not arise from irrational herd behavior, but must emerge as a rational equilibrium result from individual strategies.

relations.subject: Reversal effect as an equilibrium consequence
relations.description: The reversal effect is given a game-theoretical level of explanation by The X2 entity formalizes why the rupture occurs rapidly and completely — game theory makes it structurally predictable.

relations.subject: Security principle as a deviation strategy
relations.description: The security principle is shown by X2 not only as an ethical recommendation, but also as a dominant strategy in game theory. Those who act in a process-oriented manner without future expectations are structurally outside the expectation-Nash equilibrium - they bear no deviation costs when the equilibrium is broken and are immune to equilibrium blindness.

relations.subject: Security principle as a deviation strategy
relations.description: The Y1 entity names the security principle as a game theoretical concept, X2 provides the complete mechanism description: why process orientation becomes the dominant strategy, how it permanently avoids deviation costs and why it is the only strategy that remains rational regardless of the equilibrium state of the system.

relations.subject: Expectation coordination problem
relations.description: The general coordination problem (Y1) takes on its Beckmann-specific form in This explains why dominant expectations are often historically contingent and not rationally optimal.

relations.subject: Focal point expectation
relations.description: The Schelling point (Y1) is the basic game theory principle according to which actors coordinate without communication. X2 transfers this to expectation structures: The focal point expectation is a Schelling point in the expectation space - it becomes dominant because everyone expects that everyone else expects it to be dominant, without any explicit coordination being necessary.

relations.subject: self-reinforcing mechanism
relations.description: Systemic feedback resistance (H1) describes at the H1 level how systems actively become resistant to change. X2 provides the game-theoretic microfoundation of this phenomenon: The self-reinforcement mechanism shows how individual rational conformity decisions emergently generate systemic resistance — without central control.

relations.subject: Equilibrium blindness
relations.description: The expectation firewall (E2) describes the shielding against corrective feedback. X2 explains the game theory mechanism behind it: Equilibrium blindness is not an individual cognitive failure, but a structural feature of stable equilibria — the self-reinforcement mechanism actively creates the blindness by fixating collective attention on the dominant expectation.

relations.subject: Rational conformity
relations.description: Expectation-driven control (H1) is the macroscopic control principle of dominant expectations. X2 provides the microfoundation at the actor level: Rational conformity explains why individual actors not only passively accept the dominant expectation, but actively choose it as a strategy — whereby expectation-driven control emerges bottom-up.

relations.subject: Break in equilibrium
relations.description: The market reversal event (E5) is the observation in the financial market that dominant expectations are dramatically reversing. X2 provides the game-theoretic explanation: A market reversal event is a break in equilibrium — if enough actors deviate from the signal point, the expectation-Nash equilibrium collapses rapidly and in a chain reaction manner.

relations.subject: Collective anchoring of expectations
relations.description: The dominant expectation (E5) is the financial market equivalent of the dominant expectation in the Beckmann model. X2 explains their stability: Collective anchoring of expectations through decentralized rational conformity decisions makes the market-dominant expectation robust - not because its content is convincing, but because the equilibrium supports it systemically.

relations.subject: Expectation-Nash equilibrium
relations.description: The game theory reversal effect (Y1) names the phenomenon at the Y1 level. X2 provides the complete mechanism description: The expectation-Nash equilibrium shows how equilibrium stability and equilibrium breaking interact to make the reversal effect structurally inevitable — the more stable the equilibrium, the more rapid the breaking.

relations.subject: Deviation costs
relations.description: The Rational Choice Paradox (Y1) describes the tension between individual rationality and collective suboptimal outcomes. X2 provides a concrete instance of this paradox: deviance costs make individual conformity rational, even if the dominant expectation is objectively misguided — collective irrationality arises as an aggregate of rational individual decisions.

relations.subject: Focal point expectation
relations.object: Market expectation as a Nash equilibrium
relations.description: The market expectation as a Nash equilibrium (Y1) is the basic game theory description of stable market expectations. X2 complements the emergence mechanism: market expectations become Nash equilibria because focal point expectations solve the coordination problem — the most salience expectation wins without explicit communication and stabilizes through rational conformity.

relations.subject: Signal point
relations.description: Equilibrium selection (Y1) is the general problem that prevails among several possible equilibria. X2 ergaenzt die Dynamik des Gleichgewichtsendes: Der Signalpunkt ist der Moment, an dem ein bestehendes Gleichgewicht kollabiert und die Systemkraefte eine Neuauswahl erzwingen — er ist die spieltheoretische Schnittstelle zwischen Gleichgewichtsstabilitaet und Gleichgewichtstransition.

relations.subject: Collective anchoring of expectations
relations.description: The dominant expectation (D7) is the macroscopic result of a process that X2 describes at the micro level: collective expectation anchoring - the emergent aggregate of rational conformity decisions - is the emergence mechanism of the dominant expectation. D7 describes the condition, X2 Collective Anchoring of Expectations describes the process.

relations.subject: Security principle as a deviation strategy
relations.description: Process orientation (E2) is the operational implementation of the security principle. X2 provides the game-theoretical rationale: Those who act in a process-oriented manner deviate structurally from the expected Nash equilibrium - they are therefore the only class of actors that permanently avoids deviation costs and is not affected by equilibrium blindness.

relations.subject: Deviation costs
relations.description: Outcome orientation (E2) violates the security principle — X2 explains why it is persistent anyway: Deviation costs make it rational for each individual actor to hold on to the outcome-oriented dominant expectation as long as everyone else does. Result orientation is not an irrational error, but rather conformity enforced by game theory.

relations.subject: Equilibrium blindness
relations.description: Preconception Analysis (F5) examines how cemented preconceptions distort perception. X2 provides the game-theoretic structural dimension: Equilibrium blindness shows that preconception cementation is not only cognitively determined, but is actively generated and maintained by the self-reinforcement mechanism of equilibrium — it is system-structural, not just individual.

relations.subject: Expectation coordination problem
relations.description: Conscious expectations (D6) can be controlled at the individual level. X2 shows how the coordination problem overshadows the conscious formation of expectations: Even if an actor consciously sets his expectations, he is guided by the anticipated expectations of others - the coordination problem transforms individual expectation formation into an interactive strategy game.

relations.subject: Expectation-Nash equilibrium
relations.description: The expectation equilibrium (Y1) is the concept at the Y1 level. X2 provides its complete mechanism description: how individual expectation strategies, rational conformity, and focal points work together to produce a stable equilibrium of expectations — and why this equilibrium carries its own collapse.

relations.subject: Climate system
relations.description: The entire system contains time-delayed effects that pull apart expectations and visible feedback.

relations.subject: Climate system
relations.description: Reinforcing feedbacks make the system nonlinear and difficult to control by linear expectation.

relations.subject: Climate system
relations.description: Stabilizing feedback explains why the system does not collapse immediately, but appears resilient at times.

relations.subject: Positive feedback
relations.description: The loss of reflective surfaces can further drive change and thus make the reinforcement logic visible.

relations.subject: Positive feedback
relations.description: Thawing soils can release additional greenhouse gases, further driving the original change.

relations.subject: Oceanic heat storage
relations.description: The buffering effect of the oceans can delay visible consequences and thereby create apparent stability.

relations.subject: Climate inertia
relations.description: If feedback comes late, the lack of immediate consequences can be read as a false sense of security.

relations.subject: False positive all-clear
relations.description: The all-clear stabilizes the assumption that old patterns can continue without major corrections.

relations.subject: Fossil normality assumption
relations.description: If normality is to be maintained, later technology becomes the expected relief from current actions.

relations.subject: Technological rescue expectation
relations.description: A dominant expectation of rescue can replace robust contemporary practice and thereby postpone necessary adaptation.

relations.subject: Adaptation without future attachment
relations.description: Process-oriented adaptation reduces the likelihood that late feedback appears as an abrupt loss of control.

relations.subject: External climate testing
relations.description: Material consequences test all-clear assumptions through real stress instead of through debate.

relations.subject: External climate testing
relations.description: If external scrutiny arrives too late or too strongly, the reversal becomes visible as a crisis.

relations.subject: tipping point
relations.description: The tipping point is the threshold area at which the prepared reversal transitions into a new system state.

relations.subject: Positive feedback
relations.description: Reinforcing feedbacks can accelerate a system toward a threshold region.

relations.subject: Negative feedback
relations.description: Stabilizing mechanisms can slow down threshold transitions as long as their buffer capacity is not overwhelmed.

relations.subject: Albedo loss
relations.description: The loss of reflective surfaces can turn an expected linear development into a more rapid change.

relations.subject: Permafrost release
relations.description: Released gases can increase a late counter-reaction of the system.

relations.subject: Climate system
relations.description: The system corrects human expectations through measurable and tangible feedback.

relations.subject: Adaptation without future attachment
relations.description: Robust adaptation takes feedback seriously without deriving a guaranteed future salvation from it.

relations.subject: Climate system
relations.description: The climate system is the general systemic framework within which a regional positive climate can be understood as a local use case. The connection expands the existing climate node from a location factor to a broader feedback and expectation dynamic.

relations.subject: Climate system
relations.description: Both nodes describe systems in which small changes and coupled dynamics limit linear predictions. The climate system transfers this non-linearity to a socially and materially relevant domain.

relations.subject: Climate inertia
relations.description: Climate inertia slowly makes feedback legible and allows dominant expectations to remain deeply anchored. The connection explains why climatic reversal effects appear on long time scales rather than in immediate feedback loops.

relations.subject: tipping point
relations.description: The tipping point is the physical threshold form of a prepared reversal effect: an expectation that seemed stable for a long time is abruptly refuted by real system dynamics. This means that the abstract reversal effect can be modeled as a material climate event.

relations.subject: False positive all-clear
relations.description: False positive all-clear turns the lack of visible feedback into an apparent confirmation of the old assumption. In this way, it protects cemented preconceptions from external scrutiny and delays correction.

relations.subject: Technological rescue expectation
relations.description: The technological rescue expectation is future-oriented and can become dominant if it replaces current actions. It is therefore a climate-related form of dominant future expectation that can prepare the reversal effect.

relations.subject: Adaptation without future attachment
relations.description: Adaptation without future attachment translates the security principle into climate practice: robust present actions instead of guaranteed expectations of the future. The relation makes the new subgraph operationally compatible with the core protection principle of the main graph.

relations.subject: Framing
relations.description: Framing sets the interpretative framework within which a narrative can become dominant. Without a frame, there is no dominant narrative - the frame determines which expectations are considered natural and which are considered absurd.

relations.subject: Agenda setting
relations.description: Agenda setting determines which topics are actually framed. It is the preliminary stage: first it is determined what a community deals with, then how it understands it.

relations.subject: Repetition as a truth mechanism
relations.description: Through repeated reproduction, the dominant narrative becomes the unquestioned basis. Repetition transforms what is consciously said into what is unconsciously assumed - the strongest stabilizing mechanism of narrative dominance.

relations.subject: Linguistic construction of reality
relations.description: Because language not only describes reality but also constructs it, a narrative can become a social reality. Linguistic construction of reality is the basic principle that gives dominant narratives their effectiveness.

relations.subject: Dominant narrative
relations.description: A dominant narrative shapes perception, perception shapes action, action creates seemingly confirming facts — the narrative loop is the result of this feedback dynamic.

relations.subject: Narrative loop
relations.description: The narrative loop feeds its seemingly confirming facts back into the dominant narrative, thereby increasing its stability and resistance to corrections.

relations.subject: Dominant narrative
relations.description: A strong dominant narrative creates social pressure that silences minority opinions, thereby increasing the apparent consensus breadth of the narrative.

relations.subject: Spiral of silence
relations.description: The spiral of silence makes counter-narratives invisible, even if they exist in many minds. Counter-narratives can only become effective when the spiral of silence is broken.

relations.subject: Dominant narrative
relations.description: An established dominant narrative develops linguistic defense mechanisms against criticism and counter-information - the narrative expectation firewall protects the narrative from correction.

relations.subject: Narrative expectation firewall
relations.description: The Narrative Expectation Firewall discredits external sources and corrective signals before they can reach the dominant narrative. It is the primary mechanism by which narrative loops immunize themselves against reality testing.

relations.subject: Counter-narrative
relations.description: Counter-narratives accumulate and create cracks in the dominant narrative. When accumulation exceeds a threshold, the narrative tipping point arises at which the dominant narrative loses its stabilizing power.

relations.subject: External narrative review
relations.description: External reality testing provides the empirical counter-information that can trigger a narrative tipping point when the discrepancy between narrative and reality can no longer be bridged.

relations.subject: Narrative tipping point
relations.description: The narrative tipping point creates the window of time in which reframing can take effect. Without a tipping point, reframing has no point of attack - it can be used to establish a new frame of reference.

relations.subject: Reframing
relations.description: Reframing leaves the existing frame instead of fighting it. This bypasses the narrative expectation firewall, which is only effective within the existing frame.

relations.subject: Narrative tipping point
relations.description: At the narrative tipping point, the dominant narrative turns into its opposite. This reversal effect on the narrative level corresponds structurally to the reversal effect in the model: what was previously dominant becomes marginalized, and what was marginalized becomes dominant.

relations.subject: Narrative reversal effect
relations.description: After the narrative reversal effect, a new dominant narrative emerges - the previous counter-narrative. The cycle begins again: the new dominant narrative can develop the same stabilization mechanisms as the old one.

relations.subject: Narrative security mode
relations.description: Process-open narratives that explicitly name uncertainties and plan for revision interrupt the feedback structure of the narrative loop before it leads to uncorrectability.

relations.subject: Narrative security mode
relations.description: A safety mode narrative actively seeks external corrective signals rather than rejecting them. Narrative security mode and external narrative scrutiny reinforce each other.

relations.subject: Repetition as a truth mechanism
relations.description: Repetition is the main mechanism through which linguistic constructs acquire the status of constructed reality. The more often a narrative element is repeated, the stronger its reality-constructing effect becomes.

relations.subject: External narrative review
relations.description: External scrutiny disrupts the feedback structure of the narrative loop by introducing facts that are not controlled by the narrative itself.

relations.subject: Dominant narrative
relations.description: A dominant narrative is the linguistic-social form of realization of a dominant expectation: it creates, stabilizes and legitimizes it on the collective level. What appears in the model as a dominant expectation inside a system appears externally as a dominant narrative.

relations.subject: Narrative expectation firewall
relations.description: The narrative expectation firewall is the linguistic mechanism through which preconceptions are cemented at the societal level. Discrediting sources, tabooing questions and relabeling criticism as misinformation are the linguistic tools used to structurally deactivate a system's ability to learn.

relations.subject: Narrative tipping point
relations.description: The narrative tipping point is the linguistic harbinger of the reversal effect: the dominant narrative loses its stabilizing power before or while reality tips over. Narrative tipping points are therefore the communicative preliminary stage of the fundamental mechanism in which dominant expectation turns into manifest reality.

relations.subject: Narrative security mode
relations.description: The security principle (no dominant expectations of the future, process orientation, acting in the now) has a direct counterpart on the linguistic level: narratives that explicitly name uncertainties, plan for revision and do not make any interpretation claims that are fixed on results. The Narrative Security Mode is the communicative application of the security principle.

relations.subject: Narrative loop
relations.description: Narrative loops — feedback structures in which a narrative generates apparently confirming facts and draws confirmation from them — are a central mechanism through which dominant future expectations accumulate and become immune to correction. They accelerate the transformation of a conscious expectation into a dominant expectation, which triggers the reversal effect.

relations.subject: External narrative review
relations.description: External narrative checking and the expectation firewall are structural counterparts at different levels: The expectation firewall is the individual or technical protection mechanism against dominant future expectations; External narrative testing is the social corrective mechanism that tests narratives against sources of reality that are not controlled by the narrative itself. Both pursue the same goal: interrupting self-reinforcing loops of expectations.

relations.subject: Expectation diagnostics
relations.description: The diagnostics do not work with a single piece of evidence, but with several indicators that together make dominance visible.

relations.subject: Dominance indicator
relations.description: Intensity is a basic signal because strong emotional and cognitive engagement can override alternatives.

relations.subject: Dominance indicator
relations.description: Correction resistance shows that an expectation shields itself from external feedback.

relations.subject: Dominance indicator
relations.description: Repeated reproduction makes an expectation familiar and therefore seemingly self-evident.

relations.subject: Dominance indicator
relations.description: Affective charge shows how strongly a system is emotionally attached to an expectation.

relations.subject: Dominance indicator
relations.description: Pressure to conform makes visible when social costs prevent cross-checking.

relations.subject: Intensity of expectations
relations.description: The more intensely an expectation is pursued, the more strongly it binds emotion and identity.

relations.subject: Repetition density
relations.description: Repetition makes counterinformation less likely because the expectation appears as normality.

relations.subject: Pressure to conform
relations.description: Social pressure narrows public expression and makes the dominant expectation appear more often reproduced.

relations.subject: Threshold model
relations.description: The model does not consider indicators in isolation, but rather as a bundle of thresholds.

relations.subject: Threshold model
relations.description: The weighting of the indicators creates a profile of the current dominance situation.

relations.subject: Expectation diagnostics
relations.description: The threshold model translates individual observations into a structured diagnosis.

relations.subject: Dominance profile
relations.description: The profile decides whether only observation is necessary or whether protective mechanisms should be activated.

relations.subject: External dominance test
relations.description: External checking prevents the profile from merely repeating the internal logic of the expectation.

relations.subject: Feedback delay
relations.description: When feedback comes late, real testing becomes difficult to distinguish from apparent calm.

relations.subject: Feedback delay
relations.description: The gap between cause and consequence can be misread as an all-clear signal.

relations.subject: False confirmation
relations.description: Apparent confirmation protects the expectation especially when real testing is pending.

relations.subject: Early warning signal
relations.description: Early signals disrupt the false security before it stabilizes as the dominant interpretation.

relations.subject: Early warning signal
relations.description: Recurring early signals provide material for testing outside the logic of expectations.

relations.subject: Security diagnostics
relations.description: The safety diagnosis requires renewed external testing if the dominance profile becomes critical.

relations.subject: Security diagnostics
relations.description: Protective practice begins with reducing intensity and separating action from fixation on results.

relations.subject: Expectation diagnostics
relations.description: In the graph, diagnostics is not an end in itself, but rather leads to a decision about safe process control.

relations.subject: Expectation diagnostics
relations.description: Expectancy diagnostics makes the abstract concept of dominant expectation testable. It describes which indicators can be used to recognize that one expectation overshadows other expectations and thus becomes relevant to the reversal effect.

relations.subject: Intensity of expectations
relations.description: Intensity of expectations specifies why expectations of the future can become dangerous. The stronger the affective and cognitive attachment to a result becomes, the more likely it is to reach the status of a dominant future expectation.

relations.subject: External dominance test
relations.description: External dominance testing provides an upstream check for the security principle. It recognizes whether an expectation is already so strong that present-oriented action and release of future attachment are necessary.

relations.subject: Security diagnostics
relations.description: Security diagnostics describes when a system should activate protective mechanisms against dominant future expectations. The expectation firewall can thus be read not only as a protective idea, but also as a triggerable reaction to a critical dominance profile.

relations.subject: Correction resistance
relations.description: Correction resistance describes why a dominant narrative persists despite counterinformation. The new subgraph thus provides a diagnostic criterion for the social form of dominant expectation.

relations.subject: False confirmation
relations.description: False confirmation generalizes the mechanism by which lack of or delayed feedback is misunderstood as security. The climatic false-positive all-clear is a concrete domain form of this diagnostic error.

relations.subject: Reversal effect
relations.description: Early warning signals make the preliminary phase of a possible reversal effect visible before the result becomes manifest. In this way, Y5 connects the central mechanics of the main graph with a preventive diagnostic level.

relations.subject: Harmonic tension
relations.description: Harmonious tension is the activated form of tonal expectation: It arises when the expectation is not immediately fulfilled, but is built up and intensified.

relations.subject: Tonal expectation
relations.description: A built-up tonal expectation can discharge into resolution - the expected, stabilizing end that corresponds to time course A.

relations.subject: Tonal expectation
relations.description: Alternatively, the tonal expectation can result in a dissonance break—the unexpected break that corresponds to time course B and triggers the musical reversal effect.

relations.subject: Harmonic tension
relations.description: The greater the accumulated harmonic tension, the more powerful the emotional effect of a dissonance break - analogous to the amplitude correlation between depth of expectation and reversal effect force in the main graph.

relations.subject: Repetition
relations.description: Repetition is the primary mechanism by which tonal expectation becomes the dominant expectation: through cyclical confirmation of a pattern, it becomes the taken-for-granted norm.

relations.subject: Leitmotif
relations.description: The leitmotif is a specialized application of repetition: it links a musical phrase with a semantic expectation — a figure, a feeling, a place — and makes the anchoring of the expectation meaningful in terms of content.

relations.subject: Leitmotif
relations.description: Through targeted placement and variation, the leitmotif controls which listener's expectations are active at which moment.

relations.subject: silence
relations.description: Silence pushes the harmonic tension to its maximum: in the absence of sound, the listener projects the strongest possible expectation into the next moment.

relations.subject: Rhythmic contrast
relations.description: Rhythmic contrast is a tool for deliberately interrupting an established metrical expectation - as a milder form of dissonance break that readjusts rather than breaks expectations.

relations.subject: Listening habit
relations.description: Listening habits transform individual tonal expectations into a collectively anchored norm: what has often been heard is considered 'natural' and is therefore difficult to question.

relations.subject: Collective sound expectation
relations.description: Collective sound expectations and listening habits reinforce each other: shared listening stabilizes individual expectations, and stable individual expectations reproduce collective norms.

relations.subject: Musical canon
relations.description: The musical canon transforms collective sound expectations into institutional norms: through curricula, criticism and support systems, the dominant expectation becomes the official yardstick.

relations.subject: Stylistic rigidity
relations.description: When the musical canon no longer tolerates any deviation, stylistic rigidity arises: the dominant expectation is so entrenched that alternatives are systematically excluded.

relations.subject: Stylistic rigidity
relations.description: Stylistic rigidity is the immediate precursor to the style revolution: a style that no longer allows any deviation creates the pressure that is released in a collective dissonance rupture.

relations.subject: Innovation paradox
relations.description: The innovation paradox describes the transition mechanism to the style revolution: the innovator, who is initially rejected, prepares the ground for the later style revolution.

relations.subject: Dissonance break
relations.description: Any break in dissonance that is strong enough creates the innovation paradox: first rejection (expectation violated), then — after enough time — canonization (expectation recalibrated).

relations.subject: improvisation
relations.description: Improvisation is the direct counter-practice to the perfectionism trap: it makes result attachment structurally impossible because no result is fixed in advance.

relations.subject: improvisation
relations.description: Improvisation is the primary route to the musical flow state: the absence of a fixed result allows complete presence in the sonic moment.

relations.subject: Perfectionism trap
relations.description: The perfectionism trap is often an internalization product of collective sound expectations: the musician adopts the norms of the musical canon as an internal expectation of themselves - with the corresponding blocking effect.

relations.subject: Flow state
relations.description: In the flow state, the musician releases himself from tonal expectations - he follows the sonic moment, not the standardized logic of expectations. This can result in unexpected, powerful twists that lie outside the usual architecture of expectations.

relations.subject: silence
relations.description: Silence creates space for presence: it interrupts the automatic flow of expectations and opens a moment in which the musician and listener are in complete attention at the same time.

relations.subject: Listening habit
relations.description: Listening habits are the social buffer that delays stylistic revolutions: collectively anchored sound expectations protect themselves against innovation - until stylistic rigidity makes the break inevitable.

relations.subject: Tonal expectation
relations.description: Tonal expectation is the musical embodiment of dominant expectation: it overrides all other sound perceptions, acts collectively and unconsciously, and determines what counts as 'right' or 'natural' - structurally identical to the mechanism of dominant expectation in the main graph. The transition from tonal expectation to dominance follows the same threshold pattern as described in the main graph.

relations.subject: Dissonance break
relations.description: The dissonance break is the acoustic operationalization of the reversal effect on the level of the individual work: a tonal expectation that has built up to the point of dominance is not fulfilled, but is broken - with an emotional effect that is stronger the deeper the expectation was anchored. The amplitude of the dissonance break correlates directly with the intensity of the preceding harmonic voltage, analogous to the amplitude correlation in the main graph.

relations.subject: Style revolution
relations.description: Style revolutions are collective reversal effects at the epoch level: a musical style becomes so dominant that it creates its own rigidity and is eventually replaced by a countermovement — the same pattern as the reversal effect in the main graph, but on a time scale of decades to centuries. The historical examples (atonality vs. late romanticism, punk vs. progressive rock) correspond structurally to the historical case studies in the main graph.

relations.subject: Style revolution
relations.description: Style revolutions demonstrate the cross-scale coupling of the reversal effect in the musical field: from the dissonance break in an individual work to the style break of an innovator to the epochal style revolution, the same mechanism scales over time spans from seconds to centuries. The music subgraph thus provides another empirical example of the timescale invariance of the reversal effect described in the graph.

relations.subject: Listening habit
relations.description: Listening habit is the music-specific form of cementing preconceptions: collectively shared sound expectations protect themselves against refutation through emotional rejection of the new — identical to the three-mode mechanism of cementation in the main graph. Listening habits operate predominantly in the mode of passive cementation: new sounds are not actively reinterpreted, but are simply perceived as disturbing and avoided.

relations.subject: improvisation
relations.description: Improvisation is the musical embodiment of the safety principle: The improviser acts without fixed expectations of the future, completely out of the present sound moment - identical to the description of presence, action without expectation and process orientation in the safety principle of the main graph. The comparison with Rehhagel's success model is obvious: both describe a mode in which non-attachment to the result produces superior results.

relations.subject: Perfectionism trap
relations.description: The perfectionism trap is a micro-model of the dominant future expectation at the level of individual musical action: the fixation on an expected perfect result becomes the dominant expectation - and triggers its own reversal effect (blockage, stage fright, tension instead of performance). The music subgraph thus provides a directly tangible, everyday instance of the abstract concept.
